questions.text: In 2012, Albania exported tomatoes at far lower prices than other European suppliers — how big was the price gap, and what did it reveal about Albania's competitive position?
questions.source_paper: Fresh Tomatoes: Ideas to Build a Productive Eco-System

paper_ground_truth.answer: Albania receives the lowest average price for fresh tomatoes across Europe and Asia, earning between $0.45 and $0.72 per kg — less than 60% of what higher-end markets command. To put this in context, average import prices in the broader European/Eurasian region range from $1.29/kg in Russia to $2.21/kg in Switzerland. So Albanian producers are being paid roughly a third of what the top-end markets pay. This extreme price discount reflects a combination of factors: Albania's exports go almost exclusively to lower-value neighboring markets (Kosovo, Serbia, Montenegro, Macedonia, Bulgaria, Greece), its production is highly atomized with no quality control or traceability, and its collectors operate on a spot-transaction basis with little market knowledge. In short, Albania is participating in the tomato trade but capturing very little of its value.
supporting_quotes: the prices that Albanian producers get is very low - the lowest in Europe for some products like tomatoes
Albanian tomatoes are paid less than 60% of the price received by higher-end markets. It ranges from about $ 0.45/Kg to $/Kg 0.72 in the best case
the average import prices (which vary a lot throughout the year) range from a minimum of $/Kg 1.29 in Russia to a maximum of $/Kg 2.21 in Switzerland
Albania receives the lowest average price for its fresh tomatoes in Europe and Asia

questions.text: Between 2005 and 2012, Russia was the dominant driver of tomato import growth in Europe and Eurasia — how dominant was it, and what did that mean for exporters targeting the region?
questions.source_paper: Fresh Tomatoes: Ideas to Build a Productive Eco-System

paper_ground_truth.answer: Russia alone accounts for 62% of total tomato import growth in the European and Eurasian region since 2005. The broader regional market is massive — approximately 3.6 million tons per year, representing about two-thirds of total world tomato imports — and has been growing at a steady 4% CAGR over eight years. This makes Russia by far the most important growth engine for tomato exporters in the region. Paradoxically, Albania — which is geographically positioned within this region — has been increasing its export participation in countries that are *not* among the fastest-growing importers. Albania's current export destinations (Kosovo, Serbia, Montenegro, Macedonia, Bulgaria, Greece) are its immediate neighbors but are relatively minor players in regional import growth. This misalignment between where Albania sells and where demand is growing represents a structural strategic gap.

questions.text: As of 2012, how did Albania's tomato export market share compare to dominant players like the Netherlands and Spain, and what did this say about the competitiveness gap?
questions.source_paper: Fresh Tomatoes: Ideas to Build a Productive Eco-System

paper_ground_truth.answer: The European and Eurasian fresh tomato export market is highly concentrated: the Netherlands and Spain alone hold 55% of the regional export share, and the top 10 exporters account for virtually 100% of all exports — with only two of them classifiable as developing countries. Albania's share is close to 0%. Despite doubling its tomato exports between 2011 and 2013, Albania's participation remains negligible in regional terms. The dominance of Netherlands and Spain also reflects a quality and technology gap: these countries achieve yields roughly three times higher than Albania's (~330,000 Hg/Ha), command prices two to three times higher per kilogram, and have sophisticated supply chains with established long-term client relationships. Albania, by contrast, sells almost entirely to lower-value neighboring markets on a spot-transaction basis.

questions.text: According to 2014 projections, how much could Albania grow its tomato exports just by capturing a small slice of the German market, and what would it take to meet that demand?
questions.source_paper: Fresh Tomatoes: Ideas to Build a Productive Eco-System

paper_ground_truth.answer: Capturing just 1% of Germany's fresh tomato imports would increase Albanian export volumes by 53% — a striking demonstration of how large and high-value the German market is relative to Albania's current export base. This is because Germany is a major high-value importer (with average prices around or above $2/kg), while Albania currently exports negligible volumes to Western Europe. To meet this additional demand without transforming the entire agricultural system, Albania would need to expand greenhouse facilities by only 10% (requiring an investment of approximately $6 million) and reduce current post-harvest losses by two-thirds. Post-harvest losses currently run at about 20% and amount to roughly 3,500 tons — a 2/3 reduction in those losses alone would yield a 3% increase in usable production and a 15% increase in export volumes. Combined, these two relatively modest interventions could unlock the 53% volume growth implied by 1% German market penetration.

questions.text: Based on 2014 scenarios, what would happen to Albania's tomato exports if it simply matched Macedonia's export prices through better production practices?
questions.source_paper: Fresh Tomatoes: Ideas to Build a Productive Eco-System

paper_ground_truth.answer: Even benchmarking against Macedonia — a much lower-value market than top producers like Spain or France — would dramatically transform Albania's export performance. If improving production organization and practices led to: (a) export prices rising to Macedonia's level, (b) a 20% yield improvement, and (c) a 50% reduction in quality-related losses, the combined effect would be a 14% increase in total production volume, a 60% increase in export volumes, and a 135% increase in export value. The export value doubling-plus comes from both higher volumes and higher prices per kilogram. This is notable because the benchmark is not France or Spain — it is Macedonia, a neighboring developing country. The gap between Albania's current performance and even this modest benchmark underscores how much value Albania is leaving on the table due to organizational and quality deficiencies rather than fundamental geographic or resource constraints.

The result of just improving the organization of production and apply better practices would be an additional 14% increase in the volume of production and an additional 60% increase in the volume of exports and 135% in the value of exports

questions.text: Fresh tomatoes trade regionally almost everywhere in the world — as of 2012, does Albania's geography give it a realistic shot at higher-value European markets?
questions.source_paper: Fresh Tomatoes: Ideas to Build a Productive Eco-System

paper_ground_truth.answer: Yes, and geography is actually one of Albania's underutilized advantages. Fresh tomatoes are highly perishable and trade almost exclusively within regional proximity — the USA imports 99% of its tomatoes from Canada and Mexico, Brazil imports 100% from Argentina, and Australia imports 99% from New Zealand. This means distance is a fundamental structural constraint in the tomato trade globally. Albania sits within 2,200 km by land of major high-value markets like Germany, Switzerland, the Netherlands, and France — and within 1,500 km of emerging growth markets like Ukraine. These distances are entirely viable for fresh produce logistics. The irony is that despite this geographic advantage, Albania currently sells almost all its tomatoes to its immediate low-value neighbors (Kosovo, Serbia, Montenegro, Macedonia, Bulgaria, Greece), bypassing the higher-paying Western European and rapidly growing Eurasian markets that are geographically accessible.
supporting_quotes: Tomatoes are perishable vegetables which cannot be profitably trade to very distant locations. The higher-end markets of Europe (Germany, Switzerland, Netherlands, France, etc) are no more than 2,200 Km away by land (in the case of Amsterdam, the most distant location)
New trendy markets (for example) like Ukraine are less than 1,500 Km away

questions.text: Historically and in recent years, what did South Africa's position in the product space tell us about how hard it was for the country to diversify its exports?
questions.source_paper: Final Recommendations of the International Panel on ASGISA

paper_ground_truth.answer: South Africa has historically specialized in a poorly connected part of the product space — meaning its existing export basket sits far from many other products in terms of the capabilities and know-how required to produce them. In the product space framework, moving into new export categories is easier when a country's current exports are 'nearby' — sharing capabilities with many other products. A poorly connected position makes structural transformation harder because there are fewer adjacent opportunities to jump into. However, more recent trends offer some cause for optimism: South Africa appears to have been moving toward denser, better-connected parts of the product space, suggesting that future diversification may be more feasible than it has been historically.
supporting_quotes: Using new techniques we analyzed the position of South Africa and found that it has traditionally specialized in a poorly connected part of the product space, making structural transformation harder.
Recent trends in diversification of the South African economy suggest that future progress should be easier, given that South Africa has moved to products in denser parts of the product space.
paper_ground_truth.paper_title: Final Recommendations of the International Panel on ASGISA

questions.text: Between 1979 and 2005, Peru experienced a massive export collapse and then a recovery — did the country actually diversify its export base during that period?
questions.source_paper: Growth Diagnostics in Peru

paper_ground_truth.answer: No — Peru showed virtually no structural transformation over its export collapse and recovery cycle. The same sectors that drove the collapse (mining, hydrocarbons, and traditional agriculture) were the exact same sectors that led the recovery. About 81% of the export decline from 1979 to 1993 was attributable to traditional products, and then 78% of the recovery from 1993 to 2005 was also driven by those same traditional products. In real per capita terms, Peru's exports in 2005 ($547) were only marginally above the 1979 level ($522), meaning three decades of upheaval produced essentially no structural change in the export basket.
supporting_quotes: 81% of the fall was due to traditional products
78% of the recovery was also due to traditional products

the same activities that declined – mining and energy – are the ones that are leading the current recovery in exports to levels that in real per capita terms are similar to those achieved 30 years ago.
These are the very same sectors that collapsed in the 70s and 80s!

data_points.metric: share of export decline from traditional products
share of export recovery from traditional products

questions.text: Between 1979 and 2005, Peru's non-traditional agriculture sector grew significantly — why isn't it considered a success story for export diversification?
questions.source_paper: Growth Diagnostics in Peru

paper_ground_truth.answer: While non-traditional agriculture tripled in per capita terms between 1979 and 2005, it remains too small to meaningfully reshape Peru's export structure. It contributed only 6.1% of post-1993 export growth and represented just 5.8% of the overall export basket in 2005 — tiny figures compared to the 78% of export recovery driven by traditional sectors like mining and hydrocarbons. The impressive growth rate is in part a function of starting from a very small base. So while the sector shows promise, it has not come close to offsetting Peru's continued dependence on capital-intensive primary commodities.

However, Figure 4 shows that the non-traditional agriculture sector was of minor importance in the export recovery post-1993, contributing only 6.1% of the export growth. In terms of the overall export basket in 2005, non-traditional agriculture only amounts to 5.8%.

questions.text: Between 1975 and 2000, how did Peru's movement through the product space compare to Malaysia's?
questions.source_paper: Growth Diagnostics in Peru

paper_ground_truth.answer: The contrast is dramatic. Between 1975 and 2000, Peru made minimal movement through the product space despite experiencing a massive export collapse and recovery that might have been expected to force structural transformation. Malaysia, starting from a broadly comparable developing-country position in 1975, moved significantly and purposively through the product space over the same period — upgrading into more sophisticated and diverse products. This comparison highlights that Peru's export recovery was a restoration of the status quo rather than a structural upgrade, while Malaysia used the same timeframe to fundamentally transform its export composition.
supporting_quotes: Compare this to Malaysia, which has moved significantly and purposively through the product space in the same period.

questions.text: Between 1993–1997 and 2001–2005, how much of Peru's export growth actually came from producing more goods, versus just benefiting from higher prices?
questions.source_paper: Growth Diagnostics in Peru

paper_ground_truth.answer: Very little of Peru's export growth has come from increased production volumes. Analysis of growth from 1993 to 1997 and again from 2001 to 2005 shows that the quantity component of export growth — actual increases in how much is being produced and exported — is a minority of total export growth. The bulk of the dollar-value increase in exports came from price effects, meaning Peru was largely riding commodity price cycles rather than expanding productive capacity. This makes the recovery fragile and dependent on continued favorable commodity prices, rather than reflecting a genuine expansion of Peru's productive base.
supporting_quotes: Figure 14 shows recent overall export growth in blue, and the component of that growth that is through an increase in quantities in purple, first from 1993 to 1997, then from 2001 to 2005. Very little of the current growth in exports is from increased production.

questions.text: How did Peru's export per capita evolve from its peak of $522 in 1979 through a severe collapse to $176 by 1993, and its subsequent recovery to $547 by 2005?
questions.source_paper: Growth Diagnostics in Peru

paper_ground_truth.answer: Peru's export trajectory over this period forms a deep U-shape with almost no net progress. Real exports per capita stood at $522 in 1979, then fell 40% by 1983, and collapsed by over 80% to just $176 by 1993 — the trough. By 2005, after more than a decade of recovery, exports per capita had reached $547, only modestly above the 1979 peak. In other words, it took Peru roughly 26 years just to return to where it started in per capita export terms, and the composition of those exports was essentially unchanged — still dominated by mining and hydrocarbons.

questions.text: Why did non-traditional agriculture emerge as a new export sector in Peru during the 1990s–2005 period when other sectors failed to diversify, particularly given the agrarian reform coverage of nearly all plantations by 1975?
questions.source_paper: Growth Diagnostics in Peru

paper_ground_truth.answer: The emergence of non-traditional agriculture appears to be directly connected to which parts of Peru's agricultural sector were insulated from the 1969 agrarian reform. The reform targeted large plantations, and by 1975 had affected nearly all of them — decimating traditional export crops like cotton and sugar. Non-traditional agriculture, however, emerged precisely in areas not covered by the reform and in smaller landholdings that were less vulnerable to large-scale expropriation. In other words, the new export sector grew not because of a deliberate diversification strategy, but because it operated in a space where property rights were more secure. This pattern underscores how the risk of expropriation shaped which export activities could develop.
supporting_quotes: the only new activities to emerge (the non-traditional export sector) are in areas not affected by the agrarian reform and in smaller landholdings which are not as sensitive to large-scale expropriation.

questions.text: Between 1960 and 2004, how did South Africa's export growth compare to other natural resource exporters like Australia, Canada, and Malaysia?
questions.source_paper: South Africa's Export Predicament

paper_ground_truth.answer: South Africa's export performance has been remarkably poor even by the standards of other natural resource-dependent economies. From 1960 to 2004, exports per capita grew at just 0.64% per year, rising from $663.91 to $918.58 in constant USD — a near-stagnant performance over 44 years. When ranked against all countries with a population over 4 million and GDP per capita at least 25% of South Africa's, it places 50th out of 56 countries. What makes this especially striking is that countries like Argentina, Australia, Canada, and Malaysia — all natural resource exporters as of 1960 — vastly outperformed South Africa over the same period. So South Africa can't simply attribute its poor export growth to a 'resource curse' affecting all commodity exporters; the problem appears specific to South Africa's structural and compositional challenges.
supporting_quotes: Exports per capita in constant USD in 2004 were $918.58, up from $663.91 in 1960, representing an annualized growth rate of only 0.64% p.a.

Considering all countries with a population over 4 million and GDP per capita of at least 25% of South Africa's (a relevant comparator group), South Africa is an outlier in terms of export performance, ranking 50th out of 56 countries.

Yet this poor export performance is low even among natural resource exporters... Each of these countries was a natural resource exporter as of 1960, and still vastly outperformed South Africa over the subsequent four decades.
paper_ground_truth.paper_title: South Africa's Export Predicament

questions.text: Between 1960 and 2005, what explains South Africa's stagnant export performance—is it just the mining sector declining, or is there more to it?
questions.source_paper: South Africa's Export Predicament

paper_ground_truth.answer: The story has two interlocking parts. First, mining — South Africa's traditional export engine — faces a largely fixed resource endowment while the population nearly tripled, from 17 million in 1960 to 47 million in 2004, so mining exports per capita have declined substantially. Second, manufacturing, which could have picked up the slack, expanded in the 1960s and early 1970s but has been essentially stagnant since then. The result is that South Africa's export basket remains heavily concentrated in mining and metals — gold, coal, other mining, and basic iron and steel — with little diversification into new sectors. So it's not one factor but a combination: a diluted resource base and a failure of manufacturing to scale up and diversify.
supporting_quotes: A principal cause behind the poor export performance is the fact that mining faces a rather fixed endowment in a country where the population has been rising. For example, in 1960, South Africa was a country with a population of 17 million. Today, South Africa is a country of 47 million.
Figure 7 shows that manufacturing per capita did expand in the 60s and early 70s, but since then has been quite stagnant.

Even today, South Africa's comparative advantage in exports is concentrated in mining and metals. As shown in Figure 8, the principal sectors showing large net exports are mining (gold (230), coal (210), other mining (220) and basic iron and steel (351)).

data_points.value: gold, coal, other mining, basic iron and steel

questions.text: As of 2004, how does South Africa's position in the product space compare to Malaysia's based on trends from 1975–2000, and what does that mean for its future diversification prospects?
questions.source_paper: South Africa's Export Predicament

paper_ground_truth.answer: South Africa's production is concentrated on the periphery of the product space — areas with sparse connections to other products — and this has changed little from 1975 to 2000. There has been some recent movement into more central goods like filtering and purifying machines, furniture, and paper products, but on the whole, structural transformation has been minimal. Malaysia, by contrast, has undergone significant structural transformation over the same period, moving from peripheral goods into the dense electronics cluster in the product space. This matters enormously for future diversification: South Africa's open forest — the measure of nearby, reachable products — is currently lower than both Mexico's and Malaysia's, even though all three are traditionally natural resource exporters. And unlike Mexico and Malaysia, South Africa has seen its open forest declining since 2001, which does not bode well for future increases in export sophistication.
supporting_quotes: These figures show that South Africa's production is largely located on the periphery of the product space. This is particularly true of earlier years, and there has been some recent movement to more central goods such as filtering & purifying machines for liquids and natural gasses (and parts thereof), furniture, and paper products. Yet on the whole, these figures show peripheral production with little structural transformation to new products in South Africa.

Compare this to the equivalent figures for Malaysia. Although that country has not moved heavily into the industrial core, there has been significant structural transformation, represented by rapid movement of production from peripheral goods to the cluster of electronics related goods in the upper-right portion of the space.

The improvement in open forest during the 1990s was significant, but its reversal has again placed South Africa's options for future structural transformation below those of Mexico and Malaysia, even though they are both also traditionally natural resource exporters.

data_points.value: South Africa lower than Mexico and Malaysia

paper_ground_truth.paper_title: South Africa's Export Predicament

questions.text: Between 1985 and 2000, is South Africa's failure to diversify its exports explained by factors beyond its location in the product space, or is the product space itself the main constraint?
questions.source_paper: South Africa's Export Predicament

paper_ground_truth.answer: Interestingly, when researchers control for open forest (South Africa's position in the product space), there is no statistically significant tendency for South Africa to diversify faster or slower than would be predicted by its structural position alone. This is a meaningful finding: for some countries — like Spain, Romania, El Salvador, and Zimbabwe — there are important factors beyond product space location that accelerate or retard structural transformation. But for South Africa, its predicament is largely explained by where it sits in the product space itself. The country's peripheral position, with few densely connected nearby products, constrains the pace of diversification. Fix the product space problem — move into better-connected products — and the structural transformation challenge largely resolves itself.
supporting_quotes: So while some factors other than location in the product space are particularly important in the cases of Spain, Romania, El Salvador, and Zimbabwe, in the case of South Africa there is no statistically significant tendency towards rapid or slow structural transformation controlling for open forest. That is, in the case of South Africa, much of its structural transformation predicament boils down to its placement in the product space.
paper_ground_truth.paper_title: South Africa's Export Predicament

questions.original_text: Is South Africa's failure to diversify its exports explained by factors beyond its location in the product space, or is the product space itself the main constraint?

questions.text: As of 2004, which sectors score best for South Africa's structural transformation depending on the strategic objective—and are textiles and clothing ever a good bet?
questions.source_paper: South Africa's Export Predicament

paper_ground_truth.answer: The sector rankings shift meaningfully depending on the strategic objective, but textiles and clothing consistently underperform across most strategies. In a balanced strategy, machinery (0.24) and agriculture (0.19) lead; in a low-hanging-fruit strategy, agriculture dominates (0.68), followed by coke and refined products (0.32); and in an externality-generation strategy, machinery is a clear winner (0.80), with metal fabrication a distant second (0.13). Textiles and clothing score negative in all three of these strategies. They only appear attractive in a labor-absorbing strategy, where clothing (0.64) and textiles (0.50) rank high — but even there, agriculture outscores them once the analysis is rationalized (agriculture 1.15, clothing 0.82, textiles 0.80). So textiles and clothing are only competitive if job creation for unskilled workers is the overriding and near-exclusive priority, and even then agriculture performs better. Aerospace consistently scores at the bottom across all strategies.
supporting_quotes: Table 2.B Low Hanging Fruit Strategy, Sector Average: agriculture 0.68, coke and refined products 0.32, basic chemicals 0.07... clothing -0.42, aerospace -0.42
Table 2.A Balanced Strategy, Sector Average: machinery 0.24, agriculture 0.19... clothing -0.20, aerospace -0.29
Table 2.D Externality Generation Strategy, Sector Average: machinery 0.80, metal fabrication 0.13... textiles -0.22, clothing -0.28, aerospace -0.29
Table 3.C Labor Absorbing Strategy, Rationalized: agriculture 1.15, clothing 0.82, textiles 0.80... machinery 0.02

We see that, consistent with the findings in Section 3, the clothing and textiles sector scores quite low unless an extremely high weight is placed on unskilled labor intensity, and even in that case these sectors score below agriculture.

questions.text: As of 2004, why does agriculture rank so highly as a diversification target for South Africa, especially compared to textiles and clothing?
questions.source_paper: South Africa's Export Predicament

paper_ground_truth.answer: Agriculture's appeal comes from two reinforcing product-space advantages: proximity and strategic value. Agricultural, food, and animal products are much closer to South Africa's current export basket than textiles and clothing — an average distance of roughly 0.7 hectares versus 1.4 hectares for textiles. More importantly, agriculture offers far higher strategic value for future structural transformation, contributing roughly 12,000 units to South Africa's open forest compared to only about 1,500 for textiles and clothing. This means that by developing capabilities in agriculture, South Africa unlocks pathways to many more adjacent products in the future. Textiles and clothing, by contrast, sit in a peripheral, weakly connected cluster in the product space. The result is that agriculture scores at the top or near the top across balanced, low-hanging-fruit, and even labor-absorbing strategies — making it arguably the most robust sectoral priority for South Africa's structural transformation.
supporting_quotes: Comparing these two categories of products in terms of distance and strategic value, we see in this case that a sectoral view does show some meaningful differences: agriculture, animals, and food products are much nearer to South Africa's current productive structure (Figure 25), and are also of a much higher strategic value for South Africa (Figure 26) when compared to clothing and textiles.

Figure 26 Average Strategic Value for South Africa's future Structural Transformation, 2004: Agriculture, animals, food, etc [~12,000] vs. Textiles and Clothing [~1,500]

questions.text: Between 1960 and 2004, how does South Africa's GDP per capita growth compare to peers like Mexico, Egypt, and Malaysia?
questions.source_paper: South Africa's Export Predicament

paper_ground_truth.answer: South Africa's economic growth over the 1960–2004 period has been distinctly underwhelming compared to a wide range of peer economies. Its GDP per capita grew only 40% over those 44 years. By comparison, Mexico achieved 85% growth, Egypt 130%, and Malaysia a striking 168%. This means Malaysia's economy grew more than four times as fast as South Africa's in per capita terms over the same period. These are not just advanced economies; they include developing countries at broadly comparable starting points. This underperformance is closely linked to South Africa's export stagnation, as export growth and structural transformation are key channels through which countries raise productivity and incomes.
supporting_quotes: South Africa's output growth since 1960 has been rather disappointing, with GDP per capita in 2004 only 40% higher than it was in 1960. This is compared to an increase over the equivalent period of 85% in Mexico, 130% in Egypt, and 168% in Malaysia.
paper_ground_truth.paper_title: South Africa's Export Predicament

questions.original_text: South Africa has been lagging in GDP per capita growth since 1960 — how does this compare to peers like Mexico, Egypt, and Malaysia?

questions.text: As of 2004, how well did South Africa's official industrial strategy align with what the product space analysis suggested it should prioritize?
questions.source_paper: South Africa's Export Predicament

paper_ground_truth.answer: There is a significant mismatch. The DTI's industrial strategy nominally targets 854 out of 1,241 HS 4-digit products — about 69% of all products — which means it is not highly focused at the product level despite being framed in sectoral terms. The products it omits are, tellingly, largely inside the efficient frontier: they are both far from South Africa's current productive structure and offer little strategic value for future transformation. In other words, the omitted products are the ones that would be hardest to develop and least useful as stepping stones. Meanwhile, the strategy includes sectors like textiles, clothing, and aerospace, which score poorly or negatively across most product-space-informed strategic criteria. Agriculture — which scores highest or near-highest across multiple diversification strategies — receives comparatively less emphasis despite being closer to South Africa's current capabilities and offering far greater strategic value.
supporting_quotes: The first point of note is that even though this strategy is prioritized in terms of sectors, the strategy is not highly focused at the product level, as 854 products out of a total of 1241 in the HS-4digit system are targeted.

Figure 27 shows where these omitted products lie in South Africa's open forest in terms of the distance/strategic value tradeoff. As can be seen, these omitted products are largely inside the efficient frontier, meaning that they are both far away from South Africa's current productive structure, and they offer little in terms of providing capabilities valuable for future structural transformation.

We see that, consistent with the findings in Section 3, the clothing and textiles sector scores quite low unless an extremely high weight is placed on unskilled labor intensity, and even in that case these sectors score below agriculture. In addition, the aerospace sector consistently scores quite low.

data_points.value: largely inside efficient frontier (far and low strategic value)

paper_ground_truth.paper_title: South Africa's Export Predicament

questions.text: Based on data from 1995–2000, how likely was it that a country exporting raw cotton would actually develop a comparative advantage in processed cotton within 5 years?
questions.source_paper: Examining Beneficiation

paper_ground_truth.answer: The chances are surprisingly low. Only 9% of countries that exported raw cotton in 1995 but lacked comparative advantage in processed cotton managed to develop that capability within the next 5 years. This is particularly striking because intuition might suggest that having the raw material gives a country a natural stepping stone into downstream processing. Instead, the data shows that raw cotton exporters are far more likely to diversify laterally — 25% of those without comparative advantage in fruit developed it over the same 5-year window. This suggests that the productive capabilities needed to export raw cotton (resource rights, basic logistics) are more closely related to other agricultural sectors than to textile manufacturing.
supporting_quotes: Furthermore, of the countries that exported raw cotton in 1995 but did not export processed cotton, only 9% were able to gain comparative advantage in processed cotton over the next 5 years.
And one quarter of raw cotton exporters who could not export fruit with comparative advantage in 1995 were able to develop comparative advantage in the fruit sector over the next 5 years.
paper_ground_truth.paper_title: Examining Beneficiation

questions.original_text: How likely is it that a country exporting raw cotton will actually develop a comparative advantage in processed cotton within 5 years?

questions.text: For raw sugar exporters in 1995–2000, was it more common to move into confectionery products (the downstream industry) or into garments (an unrelated sector)?
questions.source_paper: Examining Beneficiation

paper_ground_truth.answer: The data shows a clear and counterintuitive pattern: raw sugar exporters are far more likely to develop exports in garments — an apparently unrelated sector — than in confectionery, which is the direct downstream product of sugar. At any given point, two-thirds of raw sugar exporters also export apparel, while only one-third export confectionery. Even more striking, when looking at transitions over a 5-year window, only 3% of raw sugar exporters who lacked confectionery exports in 1995 developed them by 2000, compared to 30% who transitioned into garments. This implies that the productive capabilities needed to export raw sugar (e.g., tropical climate, agricultural labor, basic processing) are more compatible with labor-intensive garment manufacturing than with the food processing skills required for confectionery.
supporting_quotes: Only one third of raw sugar exporters also export that natural resource's downstream industry: confectionary products. But two thirds export manufactured apparel.
Only 3% of raw sugar exporters who did not export confectionary products in 1995 were able to discover that sector by 2000, whereas the percentage of transitions from sugar to garments was 30%.
paper_ground_truth.paper_title: Examining Beneficiation

questions.original_text: For raw sugar exporters, is it more common to move into confectionery products (the downstream industry) or into garments (an unrelated sector)?

questions.text: As of 1995, did forward linkages between raw materials and their downstream products actually explain international trade patterns better than factor intensities?
questions.source_paper: Examining Beneficiation

paper_ground_truth.answer: Across all the empirical measures tested, factor intensities — particularly whether two products share similar labor and human capital requirements — are far stronger predictors of joint export patterns than forward linkages (i.e., whether one product is an input into another). The regression coefficients tell the story clearly: the LEAMER factor intensity measure has a coefficient of about 4.95 on the probability that two countries jointly export a pair of products, while forward linkages have a coefficient of only 0.615. The LALL technological sophistication measure also dominates forward linkages with a coefficient of 3.4. In practical terms, moving from the weakest to the strongest forward linkage in the dataset shifts the joint-exporting probability by only about one-tenth of a standard deviation — a negligible economic effect. These findings are robust across multiple time periods from 1975 to 2000. Notably, capital intensity is the one factor measure that is NOT significant, which is consistent with capital being internationally mobile, while labor and human capital are not.
supporting_quotes: Going from the 1st percentile in forward linkage strength (-0.197, from footwear manufacturing to stationary and related manufacturing) to the 99th percentile in forward linkage strength (3.13, from leather products to footwear) has an effect on distance of only 2, which is barely 1/10th of one standard deviation. Compare this to the impact of the pair of products having the same broad factor intensities (LEAMER) or technological sophistication (LALL), which have a relationship with (contemporaneous) distance of 5 and 3.4, respectively.

It is also interesting to note that all of our measures of factor intensities are significant, with the exception of K. This is consistent with the intuition that capital is the most internationally mobile factor and therefore the least important in determining international patterns of production.
paper_ground_truth.paper_title: Examining Beneficiation

questions.original_text: Do forward linkages between raw materials and their downstream products actually explain international trade patterns, or do factor intensities matter more?

questions.text: As of 1995, were forward linkages between raw materials and their downstream products weaker or stronger than forward linkages between manufactured goods?
questions.source_paper: Examining Beneficiation

paper_ground_truth.answer: Forward linkages are significantly weaker for raw materials than for manufactured goods in predicting joint export patterns. The regression coefficient on forward linkage strength is 0.532 for raw material product pairs, compared to 0.908 for manufactures — a statistically significant difference. This is an important finding because beneficiation policies (policies that mandate or incentivize local processing of raw materials) are explicitly premised on the idea that raw material exporters have a natural advantage in their downstream industries. The data suggests the opposite: the production capabilities required to extract and export raw commodities appear to be less transferable to downstream processing than capabilities shared between manufactured goods.
supporting_quotes: Both with the Leamer category and the Lall category most often associated with forward linkage-based policies, the coefficient on forward linkages is smaller than that for manufactures, and this difference is statistically significant.
paper_ground_truth.paper_title: Examining Beneficiation

questions.original_text: Are forward linkages between raw materials and their downstream products weaker or stronger than forward linkages between manufactured goods?

questions.text: As of 1995, did forward linkages predict which industries developed countries moved into?
questions.source_paper: Examining Beneficiation

paper_ground_truth.answer: Even when restricting the analysis to developed countries — where colonial-era trade distortions, resource extraction policies, and structural constraints are less relevant — forward linkages still fail to meaningfully predict export transitions. While the joint-exporting coefficient for developed countries is slightly higher (0.77 vs. the broader sample), the transition coefficient — which measures whether countries actually move into downstream sectors they didn't previously export — is only 0.016 and is not economically significant. This suggests the weakness of forward linkages as a driver of structural transformation is not a phenomenon unique to developing economies with institutional barriers; it appears to be a general feature of how economies diversify.
supporting_quotes: Even when limiting attention to developed countries with no colonial legacy, patterns of production are not associated with forward linkages in a significant way. Although the probability of joint exporting along vertical linkage lines is slightly higher among developed countries, it is not economically significant.

data_points.metric: Forward linkage coefficient - joint exporting, developed countries
Forward linkage coefficient - transition measure, developed countries

paper_ground_truth.paper_title: Examining Beneficiation

questions.original_text: Even among developed countries, do forward linkages predict which industries countries move into?

questions.text: In 1995, why were log exporters more likely to also export fish than pulp or processed wood products, even though pulp had a stronger forward linkage to logs?
questions.source_paper: Examining Beneficiation

paper_ground_truth.answer: This pattern reflects how productive capabilities — rather than input-output relationships — drive export diversification. Exporting logs successfully requires a mix of natural resource property rights, firms with sufficient capital to harvest the resource, and basic rural infrastructure to gather and ship it. These capabilities — oriented around resource extraction, capital deployment in remote areas, and logistics for bulky commodities — are more similar to what's needed to export fish than to what's needed to run a pulp mill. Pulp mills require centralized factories, large pools of unskilled workers conditioned for factory work, and manufacturing coordination capabilities that are quite distant from logging. In other words, countries diversify into products that use similar underlying capabilities, not simply into products that use their exports as inputs.
supporting_quotes: What are the capabilities needed to export logs? Probably a mix of natural resource property rights, firms with sufficient capital to harvest the resource, and simple rural infrastructure to gather the resource and ship it to port efficiently and securely. This mix of national-level capabilities seems much more relevant to fish exports than pulp mills, which require centralized factories with armies of unskilled workers that are conditioned for factory work.
Nevertheless, even with this policy bias, we see that more log exporters exported fruit than plywood.

data_points.metric: Log exporters also exporting fish vs. pulp mills
Log exporters also exporting fruit vs. plywood
data_points.value: fruit > plywood despite policy bias toward plywood

paper_ground_truth.paper_title: Examining Beneficiation

questions.original_text: Why might log exporters be more likely to also export fish than pulp or processed wood products, even though pulp has a stronger forward linkage to logs?

questions.text: Between 1996 and 2001, did Ghana's ban on log exports, intended to boost downstream wood processing, actually lead to significant growth in processed wood exports?
questions.source_paper: Examining Beneficiation

paper_ground_truth.answer: No. Despite the explicit intent to shift value addition toward processed and semi-processed wood products, the policy failed to deliver meaningful results. Earnings from processed and semi-processed wood did not increase significantly in the years following the 1996 ban. Even more strikingly, the combined earnings from both the processed and unprocessed wood sectors actually declined after the policy was implemented. This real-world outcome is consistent with the broader empirical finding that forward linkages between raw materials and their downstream products are weak drivers of structural transformation — countries cannot easily leverage raw material expertise to build comparative advantage in processing industries.
supporting_quotes: According to a 2001 study, earnings from processed and semi-processed wood did not increased significantly after this policy was implemented, and earnings from the processed and unprocessed wood sectors combined actually declined (World Bank, 2001).

data_points.metric: Change in processed wood earnings post-ban
Change in total wood sector earnings post-ban

questions.original_text: Did Ghana's ban on log exports in 1996, intended to boost downstream wood processing, actually lead to significant growth in processed wood exports?

questions.text: In 1995, when looking at precious metals exporters, did they tend to diversify into downstream metal processing or into seemingly unrelated sectors?
questions.source_paper: Examining Beneficiation

paper_ground_truth.answer: Precious metal exporters show a similar pattern to other raw material exporters — lateral diversification into unrelated sectors is at least as common as downstream processing. Only one-quarter of gold and silver exporters also export those metals in more processed forms, and one-third export jewelry and silverware. But strikingly, more than half — over 50% — of precious metals exporters also export fish, which has no direct forward linkage to mining. The probability of jointly exporting fish is actually higher than the probability of jointly exporting processed precious metals. This again suggests that the productive capabilities underlying raw mineral extraction (resource rights, capital-intensive extraction operations, remote logistics) translate more naturally into other resource-extraction activities like fishing than into the manufacturing processes required for metal refining or jewelry production.
supporting_quotes: One-quarter of raw gold and silver exporters also export those metals in more processed forms, and one third also export jewelry and silverware.
The same proportion also export raw cotton, apparel, and other minerals, whereas over half also export fish.

data_points.metric: Joint exporting with processed precious metals

paper_ground_truth.paper_title: Examining Beneficiation

questions.text: How did the relationship between forward linkages and export patterns change over time between 1975 and 2000?
questions.source_paper: Examining Beneficiation

paper_ground_truth.answer: The relationship between forward linkages and joint export patterns has been consistently weak and dominated by factor intensities throughout the entire 1975–2000 period. While the forward linkage coefficient shows some variation — rising from approximately 0.431 in 1975 to 0.719 by 2000 — it remains far below the LEAMER factor intensity coefficient, which ranged from about 4.855 in 1975 to 5.200 in 2000. The gap between these two measures is large and persistent across all time periods, indicating that the weak role of forward linkages in determining international trade patterns is not a temporary or recent phenomenon, but a structural feature of the global economy over at least a 25-year span.
supporting_quotes: All results are for 1995 (results for 1975 to 2000 for A→B and 1975-1995 for A→B_t+5 are provided in the Appendix, with all findings discussed below continuing to hold in that data).
paper_ground_truth.paper_title: Examining Beneficiation

questions.text: Between 1975 and 2000, what was the typical probability that a country would develop comparative advantage in a new product within 5 years, and how did this compare to the probability of already jointly exporting two products in 1995?
questions.source_paper: Examining Beneficiation

paper_ground_truth.answer: The typical probability of jointly exporting two products at a given point in time is around 27% (with a standard deviation of 19%), but the probability of a country transitioning into a new product within 5 years — when it currently doesn't export it — averages only 8% (standard deviation of 12%). The transition rate is roughly one-third of the joint-exporting rate, reflecting the substantial difficulty of building new export capabilities. Given that the average 5-year transition rate is 8%, the 9% transition from raw cotton to processed cotton and 3% transition from raw sugar to confectionery fall at or below average — meaning that downstream processing is no easier to break into than a typical new product, despite having the raw material input at hand.
supporting_quotes: A → B_t : mean=27, standard deviation=19
A → B_t+5 : mean = 8, standard deviation=12
Furthermore, of the countries that exported raw cotton in 1995 but did not export processed cotton, only 9% were able to gain comparative advantage in processed cotton over the next 5 years.
Only 3% of raw sugar exporters who did not export confectionary products in 1995 were able to discover that sector by 2000

Transition from raw sugar to confectionery

questions.text: As of 1995, was the logging sector unusual compared to cotton and sugar when it came to raw material exporters also exporting their downstream products?
questions.source_paper: Examining Beneficiation

paper_ground_truth.answer: Yes, logging is a partial exception among raw material sectors. While cotton and sugar show very low rates of joint exporting with their downstream industries (24% and 33% respectively), more than half of the world's log exporters also export sawmill output, and transitions from log exporting into sawmill output were relatively common over the 1995–2000 period. This is partly attributed to the widespread adoption of explicit beneficiation policies in the forestry sector — many countries have passed legislation requiring local processing of timber before export. Even so, even with this policy tailwind, more log exporters exported fruit than plywood — a higher-value downstream wood product — suggesting the broader pattern still holds: lateral diversification based on shared capabilities tends to dominate downstream processing even in sectors where policy actively promotes value addition.
supporting_quotes: One interesting case is the logging sector. We see that more than half of the world's log exporters also export sawmill output, and it was common to see countries that exported logs but not mill output in 1995 transition to export mill output by 2000.
Nevertheless, even with this policy bias, we see that more log exporters exported fruit than plywood.
We are trying to measure natural economic relationships in a distortion-free equilibrium, but when using real-world export data our measures incorporate the effects of real-world policies.

data_points.metric: Log exporters also exporting sawmill output
Log exporters also exporting fruit vs. plywood
Cotton exporters also exporting processed cotton

questions.text: South Africa's auto industry policy around 2008 uses high import tariffs on finished vehicles but also gives rebates that let companies import more cheaply — so what's the net effect on import volumes?
questions.source_paper: Reconfiguring Industrial Policy: A Framework with an Application to South Africa

paper_ground_truth.answer: This is one of the more counterintuitive outcomes of South Africa's Motor Industry Development Programme (MIDP). While the 30% tariff on finished vehicles (effectively 34% in calculations) would normally suppress imports, the IRCC duty rebate scheme simultaneously incentivizes OEMs to import vehicles to take advantage of the rebates earned through exports. These two forces — tariff-induced import suppression and rebate-induced import stimulation — have largely canceled each other out, leaving import volumes roughly unchanged compared to what they would have been without either mechanism. So despite a complex and costly policy architecture, the net trade balance impact on import penetration is essentially neutral.
supporting_quotes: By rebating import tariffs, the IRCC scheme encourages OEMs to import vehicles. The import-repressing effect of the higher tariff is offset by the import-boosting effect of the duty rebate. The numbers indicate that these two countervailing effects on import volumes have roughly canceled each other out.
The results of such an exercise reveal that the MIDP has had a large impact on exports and virtually no impact on import penetration (relative to the counterfactual just discussed).

On the other hand, import tariffs on finished vehicles still remain high (at 30%), suggesting considerable consumer costs, and the fiscal cost of the duty rebates granted through the IRCC scheme is also quite significant.

data_points.metric: import tariff rate on finished vehicles

paper_ground_truth.paper_title: Reconfiguring Industrial Policy: A Framework with an Application to South Africa

questions.text: As of 2008, why hadn't South Africa been able to build a strong domestic auto parts supplier base despite having several global OEMs operating locally?
questions.source_paper: Reconfiguring Industrial Policy: A Framework with an Application to South Africa

paper_ground_truth.answer: The challenge reflects a classic coordination trap in value chain development. OEMs need a well-developed domestic supplier base to be globally competitive — importing components from great distances imposes significant logistics costs that erode their competitiveness. However, first-tier suppliers that could serve those OEMs require large-scale operations to be viable, and they are reluctant to invest at that scale in South Africa if it means being dependent on a single OEM customer. That concentration risk leaves them exposed — if the OEM reduces production or exits, the supplier has no alternative buyers. Without multiple OEMs committing simultaneously, no individual supplier wants to move first, and without suppliers, OEMs remain uncompetitive. This mutual dependency means neither side resolves the impasse independently, making it a structural barrier to deepening South Africa's auto value chain.
supporting_quotes: Many (but not necessarily all) of the OEMs can see a path of scaling up to a level at which they can be competitive, but they are in need of a well-developed domestic supplier base for this to become a reality (in view of the logistics cost of importing components from great distances).

Potential first-tier suppliers themselves, on the other hand, have to operate at large scale to meet the needs of the OEMs, but they are hesitant to make the investment in South Africa if that means that they will be tied to a single OEM and therefore left hostage.
paper_ground_truth.paper_title: Reconfiguring Industrial Policy: A Framework with an Application to South Africa

questions.text: As of 2008, was mining in South Africa just a dead-end commodity sector, or did it generate capabilities that could feed into more complex industries?
questions.source_paper: Reconfiguring Industrial Policy: A Framework with an Application to South Africa

paper_ground_truth.answer: Mining is often characterized as an economic dead-end in complexity frameworks because it typically requires limited transferable know-how. However, the South African case illustrates an important nuance: the operational demands of mining generate highly transferable industrial capabilities. Specifically, mining operations require sophisticated engineering solutions for pumps, valves, and specialized capital equipment designed to perform under extreme conditions. These capabilities — precision engineering, materials science, industrial design — are applicable across many other industries globally, not just in mining. This suggests that rather than treating mining as a sector to escape, South Africa could leverage the engineering and manufacturing knowledge embedded in its mining supply chain as a platform for diversifying into higher-complexity capital goods exports.
supporting_quotes: Meanwhile, the needs of mining generate a host of skills and capabilities in the design and production of high performance pumps and valves and other capital equipment for the mining industry. These capacities can be further applied and developed not only in mining but in many other industries worldwide.
paper_ground_truth.paper_title: Reconfiguring Industrial Policy: A Framework with an Application to South Africa

questions.original_text: Is mining in South Africa just a dead-end commodity sector, or does it generate capabilities that can feed into more complex industries?

questions.text: As of 2008, did being a major diamond mining country naturally give a country an advantage in diamond cutting, polishing, or jewelry exports?
questions.source_paper: Reconfiguring Industrial Policy: A Framework with an Application to South Africa

paper_ground_truth.answer: Surprisingly, no — at least not in terms of productive capabilities. The know-how required to cut and polish diamonds or manufacture jewelry is fundamentally different from what is needed to mine diamond ore. Mining involves geological knowledge, extraction engineering, and logistics, while cutting and polishing require highly specialized artisanal and optical skills, precision craftsmanship, and an entirely different industrial base. This is a concrete example of low product space 'relatedness' — two products that seem connected by a shared raw material actually sit far apart in terms of the capabilities needed to produce them. A country like South Africa that dominates diamond mining cannot automatically translate that position into competitiveness in downstream diamond processing or jewelry without deliberately building a distinct set of capabilities.
supporting_quotes: For example, the skills involved in cutting and polishing diamonds and in the jewelry industry are quite different from those involved in mining diamond ore.

data_points.value: low capability overlap between mining and downstream processing

paper_ground_truth.paper_title: Reconfiguring Industrial Policy: A Framework with an Application to South Africa

questions.text: Between 1995 and 2014, how did Sri Lanka's export basket change, and what did that tell us about its diversification progress?
questions.source_paper: Constraints to Sustained and Inclusive Growth in Sri Lanka: An Initial Diagnosis and Key Topics for Discussion

paper_ground_truth.answer: Sri Lanka's export basket has barely changed since the mid-1990s. In both 1995 and 2014, the country's exports were dominated by the same products: garments (women's undergarments, t-shirts, suits, sweaters), tea, and rubber products. As of 2014, Sri Lanka exported $11.1B total, with tea alone accounting for 12% of exports, women's undergarments 5%, and t-shirts 4%. This roughly two-decade stagnation in export composition is a striking sign of failed diversification — the country has not meaningfully moved up the value chain or into new product categories despite significant time passing.
supporting_quotes: Sri Lanka's export basket has changed little since mid-1990s... [comparing 1995 and 2014 exports showing dominance of Women's undergarments, T-shirts, Tea at both periods]
Exports, 2014... $11.1B USD total... [treemap showing Women's undergarments 5%, Tea 12%, T-shirts 4%, Women's suits 4%, Sweaters/pullovers 3%, Gloves/mittens 2%, etc.]
paper_ground_truth.paper_title: Constraints to Sustained and Inclusive Growth in Sri Lanka: An Initial Diagnosis and Key Topics for Discussion

questions.original_text: How has Sri Lanka's export basket changed over the past 20 years, and what does that tell us about its diversification progress?

questions.text: As of 2014, was Sri Lanka one of the world's biggest tea exporters, and if so, was that actually a strong competitive position given who else exported tea?
questions.source_paper: Constraints to Sustained and Inclusive Growth in Sri Lanka: An Initial Diagnosis and Key Topics for Discussion

paper_ground_truth.answer: Sri Lanka holds an impressive 28% global share of tea exports — the largest of any country — but the competitive dynamics of tea are problematic for Sri Lanka's economic trajectory. Its main competitors are countries with dramatically lower income levels: Kenya at $1,300 GDP per capita and India at $1,600, compared to Sri Lanka's $3,800. This means Sri Lanka is competing in a labor-intensive commodity against countries that have a structural cost advantage. With tea making up 12% of Sri Lanka's export basket, continued dependence on this product at its current income level raises serious questions about long-run cost competitiveness, since wages in Sri Lanka are naturally higher than those of its key rivals.
supporting_quotes: Tea: Low income competitors... Global Share of Tea Exports (Net) in 2014 [showing Sri Lanka 28%, China 24%, India 14%, Kenya 15%] and GDP per capita showing Sri Lanka at ~$3,800, Kenya at ~$1,300, India at ~$1,600

questions.original_text: Sri Lanka is one of the world's biggest tea exporters, but is that actually a strong competitive position given who else exports tea?

questions.text: In 2014, how did Sri Lanka's income level compare to the countries it competed against in garment exports?
questions.source_paper: Constraints to Sustained and Inclusive Growth in Sri Lanka: An Initial Diagnosis and Key Topics for Discussion

paper_ground_truth.answer: Sri Lanka faces a significant income-competitiveness mismatch in its garment exports. At a GDP per capita of $3,800, Sri Lanka competes in women's undergarments against Bangladesh ($1,045), Cambodia ($1,105), and Vietnam ($2,050) — all countries with dramatically lower income levels and therefore lower labor costs. Since garments are a labor-intensive, lower-complexity product, these competitors have a structural cost advantage over Sri Lanka. Given that garments collectively form a large share of Sri Lanka's $11.1B export basket, this mismatch represents a core vulnerability: as Sri Lanka's income rises, maintaining competitiveness in these products becomes harder without moving up the value chain.
supporting_quotes: Women's Undergarments: Low income competitors... Global Share of Women's Undergarments Exports (Net) in 2014 showing Sri Lanka in competition with China, India, Bangladesh, Cambodia, Vietnam, Thailand, with GDP per capita chart showing Sri Lanka at ~$3,800 while competitors are mostly under $2,500

questions.text: In 2014, were there any Sri Lankan exports that actually competed against wealthier countries rather than poorer ones?
questions.source_paper: Constraints to Sustained and Inclusive Growth in Sri Lanka: An Initial Diagnosis and Key Topics for Discussion

paper_ground_truth.answer: Yes — retreaded tires are a notable exception to the pattern seen in Sri Lanka's other major exports. Sri Lanka holds a remarkable 33% global share of retreaded tire exports and competes against much wealthier nations: Belgium ($46,000 GDP per capita), Germany ($37,000), Japan ($35,000), and Brazil ($11,000). This is the opposite of the situation in garments and tea, where Sri Lanka competes against lower-income countries. Retreaded tires account for about 3% of Sri Lanka's exports, suggesting this is a niche success story that bucks the general trend but hasn't been scaled into broader export diversification.
supporting_quotes: Retreaded Tires: Middle and high income competitors... Global Share of Retreaded Tires Exports (Net) in 2014 [showing Sri Lanka 33%, China 14%, Japan 11%, Brazil 9%] with GDP per capita showing Sri Lanka at ~$4,000 vs Belgium $47,000, Germany $37,000, Japan $36,000, Brazil $11,000

questions.text: Which countries had export baskets similar to Sri Lanka's in 2012–2014, and what did they do differently afterward?
questions.source_paper: Constraints to Sustained and Inclusive Growth in Sri Lanka: An Initial Diagnosis and Key Topics for Discussion

paper_ground_truth.answer: Three countries — Thailand, Turkey, and Costa Rica — had export baskets closely resembling Sri Lanka's current structure at various points in the past, but all three subsequently diversified while Sri Lanka did not. Thailand in 1991 had an export basket dominated by garments and primary products similar to Sri Lanka's 2012–2014 basket, but went on to become a major exporter of electronics, machinery, and transport equipment. Turkey in 1994 was heavily concentrated in textiles and garments (like Sri Lanka today), but diversified into road vehicles, electrical machinery, and chemicals. Costa Rica in 1996 exported mainly coffee, agricultural goods, and garments, then successfully pivoted to electronics and electrical machinery. In all three cases, the starting point was structurally similar to Sri Lanka's current situation — the divergence lies in what happened next. Sri Lanka's export basket in 2012–2014 continues to mirror those earlier, pre-diversification snapshots without having followed the same upward trajectory.
supporting_quotes: Thailand's export basket in 1991 was similar to Sri Lanka's now, but then it changed a lot... [showing Thailand 1991 composition similar to Sri Lanka 2012-2014, but Thailand 2010 highly diversified into electronics/machinery while Sri Lanka 2012-2014 remains concentrated in clothing and tea]

Turkey's export basket in 1994 was similar to Sri Lanka's now, and then it changed... [showing Turkey 1994 export composition similar to Sri Lanka 2012-2014, but Turkey shows substantial shift to road vehicles, electrical machinery by 2000s]

questions.text: How dramatically did Thailand transform its export base between 1962 and 2010, and what does that look like in absolute terms?
questions.source_paper: Constraints to Sustained and Inclusive Growth in Sri Lanka: An Initial Diagnosis and Key Topics for Discussion

paper_ground_truth.answer: Thailand's export transformation is one of the most dramatic examples of economic diversification in the developing world. In 1962, Thailand's exports totaled just $1.36 billion and were concentrated in primary commodities like rubber and rice. By 2010, total exports had grown to approximately $187 billion — a roughly 137-fold increase — and the composition had fundamentally shifted toward electronics, machinery, and transport equipment. This transformation unfolded over roughly five decades and took Thailand from a commodity-exporting agrarian economy to a sophisticated manufacturing exporter, a trajectory that Sri Lanka has so far not replicated.
supporting_quotes: Thailand's exports in 1962... [showing rubber, rice, cement dominant] vs Thailand's exports in 2010 [showing electronics, machinery, transport equipment dominant]... exports growing from $1.36B USD to approximately $187B USD

questions.text: Between 2010 and 2015, did Sri Lanka's trade deficit pose a serious macroeconomic risk, and what's the evidence?
questions.source_paper: Constraints to Sustained and Inclusive Growth in Sri Lanka: An Initial Diagnosis and Key Topics for Discussion

paper_ground_truth.answer: The evidence suggests Sri Lanka's trade imbalance is a persistent and serious constraint on growth. The deficit in goods and services peaked at approximately 15% of GDP in both 2011 and 2015, with multiple spikes in the deficit historically coinciding with slowdowns in GDP per capita growth. This balance of payments pressure has also manifested in significant currency depreciation: the Sri Lankan Rupee weakened from roughly 110 LKR/USD in 2010 to around 145 LKR/USD by 2015 — a depreciation of about 32%. Compounding the problem, Sri Lanka's external debt stood at roughly 270% of exports and primary income in 2014, which is 119 percentage points above the level expected for a country at its income level. Together, these indicators point to a country that is structurally importing more than it earns from exports and other primary income sources, leaving it vulnerable to recurring currency crises.
supporting_quotes: But growth is limited by the Balance of Payments... [showing] Growth in GDP per capita and Deficit in goods and services from 1990-2015, with multiple spikes in the deficit coinciding with growth slowdowns

External debt is already pretty high as a share of exports and primary income (including remittances)... Difference from expected value = +119 percentage points

questions.text: As of 2013, how did Sri Lanka's tourism revenue compare to what you'd expect given its income level, and what did that gap mean?
questions.source_paper: Constraints to Sustained and Inclusive Growth in Sri Lanka: An Initial Diagnosis and Key Topics for Discussion

paper_ground_truth.answer: Sri Lanka's tourism revenue is substantially below what would be expected for a country at its income level. In 2013, Sri Lanka received approximately $50–100 in tourism receipts per capita, placing it in a cluster of much lower-income countries despite having a GDP per capita of around $3,800. The gap between Sri Lanka's actual tourism receipts per capita and the expected level for its income was approximately $598 — meaning Sri Lanka is leaving nearly $600 per person per year on the table relative to peer economies. This suggests significant untapped potential in the tourism sector, which could be a meaningful source of foreign exchange earnings if developed further.

questions.text: As of 2014, could remittances serve as a sustainable driver of export earnings for Sri Lanka given its position?
questions.source_paper: Constraints to Sustained and Inclusive Growth in Sri Lanka: An Initial Diagnosis and Key Topics for Discussion

paper_ground_truth.answer: While remittances are a positive contributor to Sri Lanka's external accounts — at roughly $310–320 per capita in 2014, they are $120 above the level expected for a country at Sri Lanka's income level — they are not considered a viable long-term growth engine. The above-expected remittance receipts indicate that a disproportionate share of Sri Lanka's workforce has emigrated in search of higher-paying jobs abroad, which is itself a symptom of limited domestic productive opportunities rather than a source of sustainable structural transformation. Unlike export diversification, remittances depend on external labor markets and do not build the domestic productive capabilities needed to sustain long-run growth.

questions.text: Between 1992 and 2017, how did Sri Lanka's export performance compare to its regional peers, and what drove the gap?
questions.source_paper: Recommendations for Trade Adjustment Assistance in Sri Lanka

paper_ground_truth.answer: Sri Lanka's export performance has significantly lagged behind regional peers, and the core problem is stalled diversification. Over the last 25 years, Sri Lanka has seen virtually no significant diversification of exports — particularly into manufactured goods linked through global value chains, which have been the primary engine of growth for other Asian economies. As a result, exports have failed to grow at anywhere near the pace of neighboring countries. This has created a structural imbalance: the broader economy has consistently expanded faster than exports have grown, setting off cyclical balance of payments crises. In essence, Sri Lanka has outgrown its economic structure without developing new export industries to sustain that growth.
supporting_quotes: Sri Lanka has seen virtually no significant diversification of exports over the last 25 years, and especially little diversification into manufactured goods linked through global value chains, which have spurred growth elsewhere in Asia.
In recent years, exports have failed to grow at anywhere near the pace of other countries in the region, and that is because export diversification has fundamentally stalled.
The economy has consistently expanded faster than exports have grown, leading to cyclical balance of payments crises.

data_points.product: manufactured goods in global value chains

questions.text: As of 2017, how did Sri Lanka's tariff structure create a bias against new industries and export diversification?
questions.source_paper: Recommendations for Trade Adjustment Assistance in Sri Lanka

paper_ground_truth.answer: Sri Lanka's tariff system creates multiple overlapping biases that collectively suppress new industry development. First, extremely high effective rates of protection for some incumbent products — typically those whose producers are already established and can lobby for protection — crowd out investment in newer activities. Industries with existing presence tend to enjoy low customs duties, para-tariff exemptions, and rate stability, while potential new industries face much lower or even negative effective rates of protection, limiting their investment appeal. Second, the system is non-transparent, complex, and unpredictable, making it difficult for new entrants to plan investments. Third, exporters must still pay para-tariffs on imported inputs, which in many cases are actually higher than the customs duties themselves — a direct cost burden on export-oriented activity. Fourth, manufacturing inputs like rubber, plastic, metal, and fabric are themselves highly protected, raising production costs across the board. The net result is a tariff architecture that entrenches incumbents while making it harder for Sri Lanka to break into new export categories or global value chains.
supporting_quotes: The extremely high effective rates of protection for some products creates a bias against investments in the production of other products. For industries that already have a presence in Sri Lanka and are able to lobby for protection, duties are generally low, para-tariffs are usually exempt, and rates are not subject to change. Much lower (or even negative) effective rates of protection for other products limit the space for investment in new economic activities.
The current tariff structure, which is non-transparent, complex and unpredictable, is one such cause.
Exporters must still pay para-tariffs on their imported inputs, which in many cases are much higher than the customs duties themselves.

Although protection tends to be the most extreme for food items, there are widespread materials of rubber, plastic, metal, and fabric, among others that are common inputs to manufacturing processes, that are made expensive by the current tariff system.

questions.text: As of 2017, why did Sri Lanka's tariff exemption system for exporters actually hurt the development of local supply chains?
questions.source_paper: Recommendations for Trade Adjustment Assistance in Sri Lanka

paper_ground_truth.answer: Sri Lanka's policy of exempting exporters from certain tariff fees — while non-exporters remain subject to them — creates an artificial wedge between the two groups. Because exporters can import inputs duty-free but cannot pass those savings on to domestic input suppliers, they are strongly incentivized to source inputs from abroad rather than from local producers. This undermines the development of domestic supply chains: local manufacturers who could potentially supply inputs to exporters are priced out because they must pay the full tariff burden on their own imported materials. The result is a structural barrier to the kind of upstream-downstream industrial linkages that typically support export diversification and value chain integration in other developing economies.
supporting_quotes: The system of exempting exporters from some fees creates an artificial barrier between exporters and non-exporters. This creates a strong bias against the development of local supply chains, since exporters are incentivized to import the inputs they need versus buying from domestic producers.

questions.text: As of 2017, what would tariff rationalization in Sri Lanka look like in practice, and what trade-offs would it involve for currently protected industries?
questions.source_paper: Recommendations for Trade Adjustment Assistance in Sri Lanka

paper_ground_truth.answer: A well-designed tariff rationalization in Sri Lanka would pursue two simultaneous goals: lowering overall nominal rates of protection (especially on inputs to production) and reducing the variance in effective rates of protection across industries. One practical approach would be to consolidate the import duty and external para-tariffs into a single external tariff, then simplify this into a set of tariff bands. This would reduce the spread of nominal protection while preserving the relative ranking of which industries receive the most protection — a politically important feature. For industries that currently rely on tariff protection, the adjustment would be cushioned by a key offsetting mechanism: as tariffs on inputs fall, production costs for those same industries would decline, partially compensating for lost nominal protection. This offsetting effect could be very large for some firms. Critically, tariff protection is described as a second-best solution — the deeper constraints facing protected industries (productivity, competitiveness) are better addressed through targeted government-industry collaboration than through blunt tariff instruments.
supporting_quotes: A smart rationalization plan must remove the bias the system creates against new industries by lowering overall nominal rates of protection (especially on potential inputs to production) and reducing the variance of effective rates of protection.

If the import duty and external para-tariffs were to be rolled into one single external tariff and simplified into a number of tariff bands, it would be a fairly straightforward exercise to reduce the spread of nominal protection while maintaining the ranking of what industries get the most protection.

Tariff rationalization should itself support the adjustment of many firms and industries by lowering input costs... Some industries that see their nominal tariff protection reduced over time will simultaneously see the prices of many of their inputs fall. This will offset some of the lost nominal protection for many industries, and this offsetting effect could be very large for some firms.

Any industries lean on tariff protection as a second-best solution to deeper constraints to productivity growth and competitiveness. Versus tariff protection, which is an extremely blunt instrument, there are almost always more targeted actions that government can take in collaboration with self-organized industries in order to resolve biggest issues that industries face to doing business.

consolidate into bands to reduce spread while preserving ranking

data_points.metric: input cost changes from tariff rationalization
data_points.value: prices of protected inputs will fall, offsetting effect could be very large

questions.text: Through 2017, Sri Lanka's economy had been growing — so why did that growth actually create a trade problem rather than solve one?
questions.source_paper: Recommendations for Trade Adjustment Assistance in Sri Lanka

paper_ground_truth.answer: Sri Lanka's economic growth has outpaced its export growth for an extended period, which is a structurally dangerous pattern. When domestic demand and GDP expand faster than export revenues, the country needs more foreign exchange to pay for imports than it earns from exports, generating recurring balance of payments pressures. The underlying issue is that Sri Lanka has essentially outgrown its existing economic structure — the same narrow export base that supported earlier growth stages is no longer sufficient to sustain a larger economy — but it has not diversified into new export industries to fill the gap. The lack of FDI inflows into new export sectors, combined with a tariff structure that discourages new industry formation, means growth has become increasingly import-intensive without a corresponding expansion of export capacity.
