Sri Lanka has, in effect, outgrown its economic structure, but it has lacked the inflows of FDI needed to establish new export industries.

Sri Lanka has seen virtually no significant diversification of exports over the last 25 years, and especially little diversification into manufactured goods linked through global value chains, which have spurred growth elsewhere in Asia.

questions.text: By 2017, how far did Jordan's actual exports fall short of what forecasters had expected in 2014?
questions.source_paper: Jordan: The Elements of a Growth Strategy

paper_ground_truth.answer: By 2017, Jordan's goods exports had massively underperformed projections. They came in 20% below what the IMF's 2014 Article IV forecast had projected, and a full 33% below what the IMF had forecast in its 2012 Article IV review. This gap reflects not just cyclical weakness but the compounding effects of regional conflict disrupting trade routes after 2014 — a shock that neither the 2012 nor 2014 forecasts had anticipated at that scale. The actual cumulative decline of 8.2% in nominal exports (11.5% in real terms) between 2014 and 2017, combined with stagnation in the preceding period, explains why the divergence from trend projections was so severe.
supporting_quotes: By the end of 2017, total exports of goods were 20% lower than the IMF Article IV 2014 forecast; and 33% below the Article IV 2012 forecast.
After 2014, the intensification of armed conflicts in the region and the interruption of important trade routes caused exports to plummet by a cumulative 8.2% (11.5% in constant dollars).

questions.text: Between 2000 and 2016, how did the composition of Jordan's export base shift between goods and services, and what does that mean for its vulnerability to regional shocks?
questions.source_paper: Jordan: The Elements of a Growth Strategy

paper_ground_truth.answer: Services have become an increasingly central pillar of Jordan's exports. From just $1.6 billion in 2000, service exports grew to $7.1 billion by 2014 — nearly a 4.5x increase — driven primarily by travel, tourism, transportation, and logistics. By 2016, services accounted for nearly 45% of Jordan's total exports, which is relatively high compared to benchmark countries. This growing services share helped cushion Jordan's overall export performance between 2009 and 2014, when goods exports stagnated but services kept expanding. However, the services-heavy composition also created a major vulnerability: because tourism, transport, and logistics are acutely sensitive to regional instability, the same sectors that had driven growth stagnated sharply after 2014 when conflicts in neighboring countries intensified. This meant that the diversification into services did not provide insulation from the regional shock — both goods and services were hit simultaneously.
supporting_quotes: Jordan's service exports grew steadily – with a minor hiccup after the financial crisis – from US $1.6 billion in 2000 to US $7.1 billion in 2014.

Travel, tourism, transportation and logistics delivered the bulk of this growth
Regional instability caused these sectors to stagnate from 2014 onwards.

questions.text: What happened to Jordan's imports between 2014 and 2017, and how did that compare to the 2008 peak?
questions.source_paper: Jordan: The Elements of a Growth Strategy

paper_ground_truth.answer: Jordan's imports fell by 10% in total between 2014 and 2017. But when adjusted for inflation and population growth, the picture is even more severe: real per capita imports were 20% lower in 2017 than in 2014, and a striking 44% below their 2008 peak. This steep decline in real per capita import capacity reflects the combined impact of export stagnation, reduced foreign exchange earnings, and the broader economic slowdown driven by regional instability. Given that both exports and imports were contracting in this period, Jordan was experiencing a significant compression of its trade volumes on both sides of the ledger.

questions.text: Between 1995 and 2015, Jordan's Economic Complexity Index fell steadily — how does that square with the export growth the country experienced during 2000–2008?
questions.source_paper: Jordan: The Elements of a Growth Strategy

paper_ground_truth.answer: This is a genuinely counterintuitive finding. Jordan's exports grew at nearly 17% per year between 2000 and 2008, and the country made significant market share gains in garments, chemicals, and agricultural goods. Yet its Economic Complexity Index — which measures the sophistication and diversity of a country's export basket — has been on a declining trend for two decades. This divergence suggests that Jordan's export growth was driven more by volume and price effects in relatively less-complex product categories than by a genuine upgrading into more sophisticated, knowledge-intensive exports. It also implies that the market share gains in garments, agriculture, and basic chemicals, while meaningful, did not represent a shift toward the kinds of complex, high-know-how products that raise ECI. The falling ECI signals a structural vulnerability: Jordan's export base may not be diversifying into the more complex products that typically correlate with sustained long-run growth.
supporting_quotes: Jordan's ECI, as measured by its goods exports, has been falling over the last 20 years.
This went beyond a mere commodity boom: it entailed significant gains in market share for Jordan's exports, in particular, for garments, chemicals, and agricultural goods.
both the economic boom and the ensuing slowdown were export-driven

questions.text: Between 2008 and 2017, how did Jordan's services exports perform relative to goods exports during the post-crisis slowdown, and what eventually derailed services growth by 2014–2017?
questions.source_paper: Jordan: The Elements of a Growth Strategy

paper_ground_truth.answer: Services exports proved more resilient than goods exports in the immediate post-crisis years. While goods export market share gains in garments and chemicals stagnated between 2008 and 2014, services exports continued to expand, growing from around $4.8 billion in 2008 to $7.1 billion by 2014. This divergence meant that by 2016 services accounted for nearly 45% of Jordan's total exports — a high share by international standards. The key drivers were travel, tourism, transportation, and logistics, which benefited from Jordan's regional connectivity. However, this services growth engine stalled after 2014: regional conflicts disrupted the same trade routes and tourism flows that had powered services expansion, causing stagnation in all of these categories. The irony is that Jordan's most dynamic export sector was also the most geographically exposed, meaning regional instability hit both goods and services exports simultaneously.

questions.text: By 2015, in what sectors does Jordan show revealed comparative advantage, and does this align with its Economic Complexity Index trajectory between 1995 and 2015?
questions.source_paper: Jordan: The Elements of a Growth Strategy

paper_ground_truth.answer: Jordan exhibits revealed comparative advantage (high relative presence) in public administration, transportation and communications, finance and insurance, and real estate — sectors that are largely non-tradable or service-oriented. On the goods side, Jordan made notable market share gains in garments, chemicals, and agricultural goods during the 2000s boom. However, these sectors — particularly garments and agriculture — tend to be relatively lower in the product complexity spectrum. This composition helps explain a key paradox: despite strong export volume growth, Jordan's Economic Complexity Index has been on a declining trend for roughly 20 years. The sectors where Jordan has comparative advantage are either services (often less captured by goods-based ECI metrics) or lower-complexity goods, suggesting the country has not been moving up the complexity ladder toward more sophisticated, knowledge-intensive products.
supporting_quotes: Jordan exhibits a high presence in other industries such as, public administration; transportation, communications and public utilities; and finance, insurance and real estate.

questions.text: During 2000–2008, was Jordan's export boom primarily driven by rising global commodity prices, or was there genuine structural improvement in export competitiveness?
questions.source_paper: Jordan: The Elements of a Growth Strategy

paper_ground_truth.answer: The evidence points to genuine structural improvement, not just a commodity price windfall. Jordan's exports grew by a factor of 3.6 between 2000 and 2008 at a CAGR of 17.3% in nominal terms and 14.1% in constant dollars — the real-dollar figure indicating growth well beyond price effects. More importantly, this growth involved significant market share gains in garments, chemicals, and agricultural goods, meaning Jordan was capturing a larger slice of global demand in these categories, not merely benefiting from price tailwinds. That said, the broader regional context — fueled by the Gulf oil boom — did play a role, including through FDI flows from Gulf countries that averaged $2.1 billion per year (12.9% of GDP) from 2003–2009. The subsequent stagnation in market share after 2008, even before the post-2014 regional crisis, suggests the competitiveness gains of the boom era were not deeply entrenched or self-sustaining.
supporting_quotes: This went beyond a mere commodity boom: it entailed significant gains in market share for Jordan's exports, in particular, for garments, chemicals, and agricultural goods.
From 2000 to 2008, Jordan's exports grew by a factor of 3.6, expanding at a CAGR of 17.3% per annum (14.1% in constant dollars).
Between 2008 and 2014, export growth decelerated (3.4% per year, 1.8% in constant dollars), as global trade slowed down and Jordan's market share gains in garment and chemical exports stagnated.
Foreign investment over the 2003-2009 period averaged US $2.1 billion – 12.9% of GDP – fueled by countries benefiting from the prolonged oil bonanza.

questions.text: As of 2016, how closely does the new structural measure of economic complexity agree with the original ECI, and does the choice of estimation method matter?
questions.source_paper: A Structural Ranking of Economic Complexity

paper_ground_truth.answer: The structural approach and the original ECI agree quite strongly at the country level. The rank correlation between the structural OLS-based ranking and the original ECI is 0.96, indicating broad agreement. When comparing two variants of the structural approach itself — one estimated with OLS and one with PPML — the correlation rises above 0.995, meaning the choice of gravity model estimator makes very little practical difference. So while the structural method has a stronger theoretical foundation, it largely validates the ordering produced by the original ECI at the country level.
supporting_quotes: The rank correlation with the one derived from using PPML in the first step is larger than .995, and even with the original ECI that starts from a binary country-product matrix indicating country-product pairs with RCA of at least one it has a rank correlation of .96.

data_points.metric: Rank correlation: structural OLS vs structural PPML

questions.text: As of 2016, are product complexity rankings as reliable as country complexity rankings, and where do the two approaches disagree most?
questions.source_paper: A Structural Ranking of Economic Complexity

paper_ground_truth.answer: Product complexity rankings are meaningfully less robust than country rankings. The rank correlation between the original Product Complexity Index (PCI) and the structural OLS-based alternative is 0.84, compared to 0.96 for country rankings. A striking example of disagreement is musical instruments (HS 92): the original PCI places them 4th most complex globally, while the structural approach ranks them 35th. Part of the reason for lower robustness is a statistical limitation — the product similarity matrix is constructed using export data from 127 countries to evaluate only 97 products, so there is less information per product than per country in the estimation.
supporting_quotes: The product ranking is somewhat less robust, which may not come as a surprise given that we use export data from 127 countries to evaluate the similarities of 97 products.
For instance, 'Musical instruments, parts and accessories of such articles' (92) are ranked 4th by the original PCI, but 35th according to our structural alternative using OLS in the first step.

data_points.metric: Rank correlation: structural OLS vs original PCI (products)

questions.text: How does economic complexity relate to GDP per capita, and does a high complexity score simply mean a rich country?
questions.source_paper: A Structural Ranking of Economic Complexity

paper_ground_truth.answer: Economic complexity and GDP per capita capture related but distinct dimensions of a country's economy. Complexity rankings are tied to comparative advantage — what a country can produce relative to others — rather than absolute advantage, which more directly drives income levels. This means complexity reveals information about the depth and diversity of a country's productive capabilities that GDP per capita alone does not capture. In other words, two countries with similar income levels could have very different complexity scores if their export baskets differ in diversity and sophistication, and vice versa.
supporting_quotes: This ranking is tied to comparative advantages as opposed to absolute advantages. Hence, it reveals information different from GDP per capita on the deep underlying economic capabilities of countries.

data_points.value: Correlated but distinct; ECI captures comparative advantage-based capabilities

questions.text: As of 2015, how did Jordan's economic complexity compare to countries at a similar income level, and what did that imply for its growth prospects?
questions.source_paper: A Roadmap for Investment Promotion and Export Diversification: The Case of Jordan

paper_ground_truth.answer: Jordan's Economic Complexity Index (ECI) is actually lower than what would be expected given its income per capita level. This is significant because research shows that the gap between a country's complexity and its income level is predictive of future growth: countries whose complexity exceeds what their income would predict tend to grow faster, while countries like Jordan — where income is relatively higher than complexity would suggest — tend to grow more slowly. In other words, Jordan's productive structure is not as sophisticated as its current income level implies, which is a warning sign for future growth trajectories.
supporting_quotes: Jordan shows an ECI index that is lower than what can be expected for its income per capita level.

Countries with an economic complexity greater than predicted by their level of income tend to grow faster than countries with higher income than predicted by their current level of economic complexity.
In both cases, Jordan is poorly ranked relative to its comparable countries and, more broadly, to the rest of the world.

questions.text: In 2015, Jordan had a significant manufacturing sector — did that give it a strong base of economic complexity relative to its peers?
questions.source_paper: A Roadmap for Investment Promotion and Export Diversification: The Case of Jordan

paper_ground_truth.answer: Not really. While manufacturing accounts for roughly half of Jordan's industry diversity, that doesn't translate into a particularly strong position relative to benchmark economies. Jordan ranks approximately 5th among its benchmark group in manufacturing diversity. So despite manufacturing being a major component of what Jordan produces, it hasn't built the breadth of manufacturing capabilities that peer countries have. Interestingly, Jordan actually ranks 1st or 2nd among its benchmark peers in sectors like public administration, transportation/communications/utilities, and finance/insurance/real estate — suggesting its relative strengths lie more in services-adjacent sectors than in manufacturing.
supporting_quotes: The results indicate that even though manufacturing accounts for roughly half of Jordan's diversity, the country's diversity in manufacturing is not particularly high in comparison to benchmark economies.

In the case of the public administration; transportation, communications and public utilities; and finance, insurance and real estate sectors, Jordan ranks first or second in diversity among the group.
paper_ground_truth.paper_title: A Roadmap for Investment Promotion and Export Diversification: The Case of Jordan

questions.original_text: Jordan has a significant manufacturing sector — does that give it a strong base of economic complexity relative to its peers?

questions.text: By 2016, services made up a large share of Jordan's exports — how did that compare to other countries, and how had that share grown since 1995?
questions.source_paper: A Roadmap for Investment Promotion and Export Diversification: The Case of Jordan

paper_ground_truth.answer: Services are a standout feature of Jordan's export profile. By 2016, services accounted for nearly 45% of total Jordanian exports — a proportion that is notably higher than that of benchmark countries. This wasn't always the case: Jordanian service exports grew from just $1.7 billion in 1995 to $4.8 billion in 2008, and then continued expanding to $7.1 billion by 2014, even shrugging off most of the impact of the 2008–2009 Global Financial Crisis. This resilience and scale of services exports distinguishes Jordan from many of its regional peers.
supporting_quotes: By 2016, services accounted for nearly 45% of total Jordanian exports, a proportion that is relatively higher than that of benchmark countries.

From 1995 to 2008, Jordanian service exports grew steadily from US$ 1.7 billion to US$ 4.8 billion. Despite the strong impacts of the 2008-2009 Global Financial Crisis on the global economy, Jordanian service exports only experienced a minor hiccup thereafter, and eventually grew to US$ 7.1 billion in 2014.
paper_ground_truth.paper_title: A Roadmap for Investment Promotion and Export Diversification: The Case of Jordan

questions.original_text: Services make up a large share of Jordan's exports — how does that compare to other countries, and has that share been growing?

questions.text: As of 2015, why does complexity analysis suggest it's hard for a developing country like Jordan to move into its most strategically valuable industries?
questions.source_paper: A Roadmap for Investment Promotion and Export Diversification: The Case of Jordan

paper_ground_truth.answer: There's a fundamental trade-off embedded in the product space for developing economies. The industries that score highest on complexity (Product Complexity Index) and strategic positioning (Complexity Outlook Gain) tend to be the ones furthest from a country's existing capabilities — meaning they require know-how and inputs that aren't currently present. Conversely, the industries that are closest to what a country already does well tend to be less complex and offer weaker long-term strategic value. This negative correlation between distance and complexity/strategic gain means that the easiest moves in the product space often yield the lowest returns, while the highest-return moves require the biggest capability leaps.
supporting_quotes: While PCI and COG tend to be positively correlated, there tends to be negative correlation between Distance and each of these indicators in developing economies. This reflects an important trade-off: the most complex industries and those with the best strategic positioning tend to be further away from existing capabilities, while less complex industries tend to be closer.
paper_ground_truth.paper_title: A Roadmap for Investment Promotion and Export Diversification: The Case of Jordan

questions.original_text: Why does complexity analysis suggest it's hard for a developing country like Jordan to move into its most strategically valuable industries?

questions.text: How did Jordan's economic performance between 2008 and 2017 shape the urgency of its export diversification challenge?
questions.source_paper: A Roadmap for Investment Promotion and Export Diversification: The Case of Jordan

paper_ground_truth.answer: Jordan experienced a prolonged and severe economic deterioration: nine consecutive years (2008–2017) of negative GDP per capita growth, resulting in a cumulative loss of 14% in GDP per capita over the decade from 2009 to 2018. This makes Jordan's case for diversification particularly urgent. Its economic complexity is already lower than what its income level would predict — itself a warning signal for slower future growth — and its export base is less diversified than benchmark peers, with industries that tend to be more common and less sophisticated globally. The combination of a shrinking economic base and a relatively weak productive structure underscores why broadening and upgrading exports is critical.
supporting_quotes: Failure to cope with a 50.4% growth in population led to nine consecutive years (2008-2017) of negative growth rates in GDP per capita, resulting in a cumulative loss of 14.0% over the past decade (2009-2018).
Jordan shows an ECI index that is lower than what can be expected for its income per capita level.
Jordan, with a diversity of 178, ranks second-to-last among the benchmark countries.

questions.text: Between 1990 and 2000, how much did having more immigrant diversity actually move the needle on a country's economic complexity?
questions.source_paper: Does Birthplace Diversity Affect Economic Complexity? Cross-country Evidence

paper_ground_truth.answer: The effect is meaningful but modest: a one standard deviation increase in birthplace diversity is associated with a country's Economic Complexity Index rising by 0.1 to 0.18 standard deviations above the mean. To put that in concrete export terms, the same increase in diversity is linked to roughly 12 additional products being exported competitively (with a revealed comparative advantage above 1) — about a 10% increase relative to the sample average of around 120 competitive products.
supporting_quotes: Our cross-country analysis suggests that countries with higher birthplace diversity by one standard deviation are more economically complex by 0.1 to 0.18 standard deviations above the mean.

For instance, an increase of one standard deviation in our birthplace diversity index is associated with an increase in the number of goods exported competitively by around 12 (roughly a 10 percent increase, based on the sample mean of about 120, with standard deviation equal to 81).

data_points.metric: ECI increase per 1 SD birthplace diversity
Additional competitive export products per 1 SD birthplace diversity

questions.original_text: How much does having more immigrant diversity actually move the needle on a country's economic complexity?

questions.text: During 1990–2000, which countries benefited most from immigrant diversity in terms of economic complexity — already advanced economies or developing ones?
questions.source_paper: Does Birthplace Diversity Affect Economic Complexity? Cross-country Evidence

paper_ground_truth.answer: The benefit is most concentrated in developing countries sitting in the middle of the economic complexity distribution — specifically, those in the second tercile of the ECI ranking. For these countries, a one standard deviation increase in birthplace diversity raises economic complexity by 0.141 to 0.148 standard deviations, and causal IV estimates push that even higher to 0.166 to 0.224. By contrast, the least complex (bottom tercile) and already highly complex (top tercile) economies show weaker effects. This non-linear pattern suggests diversity is most impactful when a country already has some productive base to build on but hasn't yet exhausted its diversification potential.
supporting_quotes: Our results are particularly strong among developing countries that are in the middle of the economic complexity distribution, suggesting that an increase in skill variety is particularly relevant for boosting countries development path.

Combining both approaches (gravity model and shift-share based instruments), our estimates support a causal interpretation for the effect of diversity on economic complexity, in particular among developing countries.
paper_ground_truth.paper_title: Does Birthplace Diversity Affect Economic Complexity? Cross-country Evidence

questions.original_text: Which countries benefit most from immigrant diversity in terms of economic complexity — already advanced economies or developing ones?

questions.text: Between 1990 and 2000, was economic complexity part of the reason why more diverse immigrant populations tended to raise a country's income?
questions.source_paper: Does Birthplace Diversity Affect Economic Complexity? Cross-country Evidence

paper_ground_truth.answer: Yes, and the data provide a way to quantify how much. When economic complexity is added as a control in regressions linking birthplace diversity to GDP per capita, the partial correlation between diversity and income drops by about 20%. This means ECI acts as a meaningful — though partial — channel through which immigrant diversity translates into higher income. In other words, one reason diverse immigrant populations raise incomes is that they help countries export a broader and more sophisticated range of products, which is exactly what economic complexity captures.
supporting_quotes: Note that when ECI is controlled for there is a significant reduction of the partial correlation between diversity and GDP per capita, of around 20%. This suggests that ECI is, in part, one of the channels through which birthplace diversity affects income.

data_points.metric: Share of diversity-to-income effect mediated by ECI

paper_ground_truth.paper_title: Does Birthplace Diversity Affect Economic Complexity? Cross-country Evidence

questions.original_text: Is economic complexity part of the reason why more diverse immigrant populations tend to raise a country's income?

questions.text: During 1990–2000, when immigrant diversity improved economic complexity, was it because countries were getting immigrants from more different places, or because the mix of existing source countries was changing?
questions.source_paper: Does Birthplace Diversity Affect Economic Complexity? Cross-country Evidence

paper_ground_truth.answer: The primary driver is the expansion in the number of origin countries — what statisticians call the 'extensive margin' or 'between' component of diversity. Using a Theil index decomposition, most of the effect of diversity on economic complexity comes from countries drawing migrants from an ever-wider set of origins, rather than from shifts in the relative shares of already-represented nationalities. This is particularly true for developing countries in the middle of the complexity distribution. The implication is that adding new source countries of immigration — not just rebalancing existing flows — is what most expands a country's productive variety.
supporting_quotes: we find that most of the effect is driven by the 'between' component, which can be interpreted as the extensive margin of diversity in the sense that it captures the contribution of new origin countries to the evolution of immigration diversity.
paper_ground_truth.paper_title: Does Birthplace Diversity Affect Economic Complexity? Cross-country Evidence

questions.original_text: When immigrant diversity improves economic complexity, is it because countries are getting immigrants from more different places, or because the mix of existing source countries is changing?

questions.text: Between 1990 and 2000, did immigrant diversity help countries export more unique, hard-to-replicate products, or mainly just more products overall?
questions.source_paper: Does Birthplace Diversity Affect Economic Complexity? Cross-country Evidence

paper_ground_truth.answer: Birthplace diversity primarily helps countries export a broader range of products (extensive margin), but its effect on product uniqueness is less clear-cut. More diverse countries do export significantly more products with a revealed comparative advantage (RCA > 1) — about 12 additional competitive products per standard deviation increase in diversity. However, the relationship with export ubiquity — how rare or unique those products are in world trade — is not statistically significant, even though the directional trend suggests slightly less ubiquitous (more unique) exports. So diversity expands the export basket more reliably than it deepens the sophistication of individual products.
supporting_quotes: While not all point estimates are statistically different from zero based on the conventional confidence intervals, we do find significant coefficients for the number of products with RCA above one (columns 7 to 9).

Panel B shows the partial correlations between skill-specific migration diversity and export ubiquity. Overall the estimates are not statistically different from zero, however the direction of the point estimates is toward a reduction of products ubiquity.

data_points.metric: Birthplace diversity effect on products with RCA>1
Birthplace diversity effect on product ubiquity

questions.original_text: Does immigrant diversity help countries export more unique, hard-to-replicate products, or mainly just more products overall?

questions.text: Between 1990 and 2000, could the link between immigrant diversity and economic complexity just be a statistical artifact — maybe wealthier immigrants bring their home country's complexity with them rather than creating new capabilities?
questions.source_paper: Does Birthplace Diversity Affect Economic Complexity? Cross-country Evidence

paper_ground_truth.answer: This 'wealth transfer' or 'complexity transfer' hypothesis has been tested directly by including a control for the origin-country economic complexity of migrants — i.e., accounting for whether immigrants simply transplant their home country's productive knowledge. Even after this control, the relationship between birthplace diversity and destination-country ECI remains positive and statistically significant. Additionally, instrumental variable estimates using gravity-model-predicted migration flows are actually larger than ordinary least squares estimates, suggesting that if anything, standard OLS understates the true causal effect due to attenuation bias rather than overstating it due to reverse causality. The effect also survives controlling for historical diversity from 1960, ruling out confounding from earlier immigrant generations.
supporting_quotes: Once we include simultaneously the birthplace diversity index and the origin-specific term, the partial correlation between birthplace diversity and economic complexity remains positive and statistically significant.
Moreover, all the 2SLS point estimates are larger than the OLS estimates presented in Table 3 when we instrument both endogenous variables, suggesting the correction of an attenuation bias.
However, the estimates for current immigration diversity over the subsample of countries belonging to the 2nd tercile are positive and significant, in particular among highly educated immigrants.

data_points.metric: Birthplace diversity coefficient (with origin ECI control)

questions.text: Between 1970 and 2010, how did the composition of migrants into OECD countries shift, and what might that mean for economic complexity?
questions.source_paper: Does Birthplace Diversity Affect Economic Complexity? Cross-country Evidence

paper_ground_truth.answer: The shift has been dramatic. Between 1970 and 2010, the share of non-OECD migrants in OECD destination countries grew by a factor of 8.7, while the share of intra-OECD migrants barely budged — growing by only a factor of 1.2 over the same period. At the same time, the overall foreign-born share in OECD countries nearly doubled, from 7% to 13%. This shift toward migrants from more diverse, non-OECD origin countries is precisely the type of change — expanding the 'between' component of birthplace diversity — that the research identifies as the primary driver of complexity gains. This suggests the post-1970 migration wave may have carried more complexity-building potential than its raw numbers imply, as it substantially widened the variety of knowledge and skills flowing into OECD economies.
supporting_quotes: the average share of non-OECD migrants growing by a factor of 8.7 over the 1970-2010 period against just 1.2 for OECD migrants over the same period.

While the global share of immigrants has remained stable over time –at about three percent of the world population– the share of the foreign-born population in OECD countries has increased from seven to thirteen percent on average between 1970 and 2010.

we find that most of the effect is driven by the 'between' component, which can be interpreted as the extensive margin of diversity in the sense that it captures the contribution of new origin countries to the evolution of immigration diversity.

data_points.metric: Growth factor in non-OECD migrant share in OECD
Growth factor in intra-OECD migrant share

questions.text: Between 1990 and 2000, was there a meaningful difference in how economic complexity related to export diversification versus export concentration?
questions.source_paper: Does Birthplace Diversity Affect Economic Complexity? Cross-country Evidence

paper_ground_truth.answer: Yes, and the correlations are quite different in magnitude. Economic complexity (ECI) correlates very strongly and positively with the number of products a country exports competitively (correlation of 0.811), confirming that more complex economies have much broader export baskets. However, ECI's negative correlation with the Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI) of export concentration is considerably weaker at -0.557. This asymmetry suggests ECI captures more than just low export concentration — it reflects the depth and sophistication of the export structure in ways that simple concentration metrics miss. Meanwhile, birthplace diversity itself shows a positive but more modest correlation with export breadth (0.342), and a slight negative correlation with weighted product ubiquity (-0.185 to -0.188), hinting that diverse countries tend to export somewhat less ubiquitous products.
supporting_quotes: Table 7 shows the correlation across those measures of diversification, export uniqueness and our measures of birthplace diversity, GDP per capita as well as ECI.

the table also documents that economic complexity and immigration diversity are positively correlated with measures of export diversification (or, in fact, negatively correlated with measures of concentration).

Concerning measures of export uniqueness we find a negative and significant correlation between our weighted measures of competitive products and diversity, suggesting that more diversity is related with less ubiquitously exported goods.

data_points.metric: Correlation between ECI and products with RCA>1
Correlation between ECI and HHI (export concentration)

Correlation between birthplace diversity and weighted product ubiquity

questions.original_text: Is there a meaningful difference between how economic complexity relates to export diversification versus export concentration?

questions.text: From 1995 onward, how well did a country's existing export basket predict what it would export in the future, and did this prediction get better or worse over longer time periods through 2016?
questions.source_paper: Implied Comparative Advantage

paper_ground_truth.answer: The predictive power of implied comparative advantage — derived from a country's existing export basket — improves substantially over longer time horizons. Using data from 1995 to 2016, the minimum explanatory power (R²) across any time window exceeds 8.5%, the mean across all horizons is 14.6%, and it reaches roughly 20% at the full 21-year horizon. This means that the structural gaps between where a country 'should' have comparative advantage (based on its neighbor industries and similar countries) and where it actually does are better signals of long-run export evolution than short-run shifts. The implication is that trade diversification is a slow, path-dependent process — not a sudden reorientation — and that the product space and country space together act as structural attractors pulling export patterns toward their implied equilibrium over decades.
supporting_quotes: Figure 2 shows adjusted R² values for international trade regressions (including base-year controls) over all possible year combinations. Each regression explains a sizable portion of the variation, with the lowest adjusted R² exceeding 8.5%, and a mean R² of 14.6%. Explanatory power generally improves as the interval increases
Residual, Hybrid Density, 1995: -0.024*** (0.001)... Table 6 shows regressions of growth in international exports.

data_points.metric: Minimum R² for short-term growth prediction
Maximum R² for long-term growth prediction

questions.original_text: How well does a country's existing export basket predict what it will export in the future, and does this prediction get better or worse over longer time periods?

questions.text: As of 1995, which was a stronger predictor of what a country exported — the products its neighbors already exported (product space), or what similar countries exported (country space)?
questions.source_paper: Implied Comparative Advantage

paper_ground_truth.answer: Product space density — measuring how close an industry is to a country's existing export basket — is a stronger predictor of current comparative advantage than country space density, which measures how much similar countries export the same product. For 1995 international trade data, product space density alone explains 63.6% of the variation in export intensity across country-product pairs, while country space density alone explains 49.3%. Together, they explain 65.4%, meaning the two dimensions are complementary but product space carries more weight. Both are highly statistically significant (p<0.001) with coefficients close to unity, suggesting that the two mechanisms are operating simultaneously and independently to shape a country's export structure.
supporting_quotes: Table 4 shows that both the PS and CS density terms are highly significant (p < 0.001), with coefficients very close to unity... the terms also explain a very large fraction of the variance of the country-product export intensity, though the PS density generates a significantly higher R² values than the CS density.

data_points.metric: R² for Product Space density alone
R² for Country Space density alone
R² for both PS and CS density together

questions.original_text: Which is a stronger predictor of what a country exports — the products its neighbors already export (product space), or what similar countries export (country space)?

questions.text: Between 1995 and 2010, if a country was exporting something it 'shouldn't be' based on its broader capabilities, what happened to that export over time?
questions.source_paper: Implied Comparative Advantage

paper_ground_truth.answer: Industries that are 'unexpectedly present' — meaning a country has comparative advantage in them despite low product space and country space density — tend to disappear over time at significantly elevated rates. This pattern holds across international trade data and subnational data for the US and Chile. In the US, the probit coefficient for residual on disappearance is +2.50 (p<0.001), and in Chile it is +1.76 (p<0.001). The international export dataset shows a coefficient of +1.28 (p<0.001). Conversely, industries that are 'unexpectedly absent' — where implied density is high but current exports are low — tend to appear over time. The US coefficient on appearance is -2.39 and Chile's is -2.33 (both p<0.001). Together, this reveals a strong gravitational pull: export structures tend to converge toward what their product space and country space imply they should be, rather than drifting randomly.
supporting_quotes: Table 9... shows that residual term is always negative (as expected), highly significant, and with a similar magnitude to that of our original density formula... [for disappearances] large positive residuals signify an 'unexpectedly present' industry-location, and should thus predict disappearances
This means that unexpectedly absent industries tend to preferentially appear over time while unexpectedly present industries tend to disappear.
paper_ground_truth.paper_title: Implied Comparative Advantage

questions.text: As of 2010, how similar were China's and the United States' export structures, and how did that compare to the most similar country pairs in the world?
questions.source_paper: Implied Comparative Advantage

paper_ground_truth.answer: China and the United States have the most dissimilar export structures of any country pair in the international dataset, with a location similarity score of just 0.326 — far below the random baseline of 0.5. This means the two economies tend to export complementary rather than overlapping baskets of goods. At the other extreme, Sudan and Chad have the highest observed similarity at 0.867, reflecting shared geography, climate, and possibly regional knowledge spillovers. Other highly similar pairs include Japan-South Korea (0.78) and Finland-Sweden (0.745). The contrast between the China-US pair and these regional pairs illustrates how geographic proximity and shared endowments drive convergent export structures, while the US-China divergence reflects deep specialization differences between a highly complex advanced economy and a manufacturing-intensive emerging giant.
supporting_quotes: For example, the lowest similarity value we observe is between China and the US (φChina,USA = 0.326); this is far below random (0.5), meaning that we can expect one country to export what the other does not.

We note that the most similar countries are those in the same geographic region, a phenomenon that can be explained by geological and climate effects as well as regional knowledge spillovers... Table 2 shows the top ten most similar pairs of countries and products in the most recent year.

questions.original_text: How similar are China's and the United States' export structures, and how does that compare to the most similar country pairs in the world?

questions.text: Between 1995 and 2016, were high-tech export sectors more predictable in their growth trajectories than commodity or agricultural exports?
questions.source_paper: Implied Comparative Advantage

paper_ground_truth.answer: Yes, and the difference is meaningful. High-tech products like electronics, machinery, and medical devices show the strongest predictability under implied comparative advantage frameworks, with R² values of around 0.19–0.20 for growth predictions. By contrast, extractive industries and agricultural products show the weakest predictability, with R² values in the range of 0.14–0.15. The intuition is that high-tech manufacturing requires dense, overlapping capabilities — the same knowledge that makes a country good at one type of machine makes it likely to be good at adjacent machines — while comparative advantage in commodities often depends on geographic luck (e.g., oilfield discoveries or soil conditions) that has little to do with a country's broader technological capabilities. Apparel and textiles also show relatively low predictability (R²≈0.14), likely because labor cost dynamics can shift these industries across countries in ways unrelated to capability relatedness.
supporting_quotes: For product subsamples (based on the HS chapters)... it appears that the most easily-explained categories are high-tech goods like electronics or medical devices. The worst predictions are in the extractives and agricultural categories; this makes sense, since shifts in these commodities may have more to do with geographic luck (e.g., oilfield discoveries) than shared technological requirements.
paper_ground_truth.paper_title: Implied Comparative Advantage

questions.original_text: Are high-tech export sectors more predictable in their growth trajectories than commodity or agricultural exports?

questions.text: Between 1995 and 2016, were low-income countries' export trajectories more predictable than high-income countries', and what did that say about economic flexibility?
questions.source_paper: Implied Comparative Advantage

paper_ground_truth.answer: Somewhat counterintuitively, low-income and upper-middle-income countries show higher predictability (R²=0.201 and 0.212 respectively) than high-income countries (R²=0.137) when it comes to how their exports evolve over time. The likely reason is that rich, complex economies have more degrees of freedom — they can shift comparative advantage in ways driven by innovation, firm strategy, or policy that the product space and country space framework doesn't fully capture. Low-income countries, by contrast, may be more tightly constrained by their existing capabilities: they can only realistically expand into sectors that are closely related to what they already do. This finding suggests that export diversification in developing economies is more structurally determined and therefore more foreseeable, while advanced economies retain more flexibility to escape their current specialization patterns.
supporting_quotes: Next, we can divide countries by income level (according to World Bank Group classifications): the groups are relatively close to each other, though low-income countries are the most predictable under our framework (possibly because they are less likely to shift their comparative advantage over the period).
paper_ground_truth.paper_title: Implied Comparative Advantage

questions.original_text: Are low-income countries' export trajectories more predictable than high-income countries', and what does that say about economic flexibility?

questions.text: How well could you predict whether an industry would be present in a location in 1995, 2003, or 2005, just by knowing what else that location produced and what similar locations produced?
questions.source_paper: Implied Comparative Advantage

paper_ground_truth.answer: The predictive accuracy is remarkably high. Using a probit model that combines product space and country space density, the area under the ROC curve (AUC) — a measure of binary classification accuracy — reaches approximately 0.93–0.94 across all datasets tested. For US establishments the AUC is 0.934, for Chilean establishments it is 0.936, and for international export data it is 0.936. An AUC of 0.5 is random chance, and 1.0 is perfect prediction. These results mean that knowing a location's existing export or industry mix, and knowing what similar locations do, gets you nearly all the way to correctly predicting which industries will be present or absent — before even observing any industry-specific data. This validates the product space concept at a binary presence/absence level, not just as a continuous measure of export intensity.
supporting_quotes: Table 8 applies our probit regression model to the US and Chilean establishment data and international export data, for the first year of each. Our PS and CS densities combined explain one half of the variance in industry-location; AUC values are also very high (close to 94% for hybrid models).
paper_ground_truth.paper_title: Implied Comparative Advantage

questions.original_text: How well can you predict whether an industry will be present in a location at all, just by knowing what else that location produces and what similar locations produce?

questions.text: Based on 2016 data, does the standard Ricardian trade model correctly assume that a country's productivity advantage in one sector is unrelated to its advantage in other sectors?
questions.source_paper: Implied Comparative Advantage

paper_ground_truth.answer: No — the evidence directly contradicts this core assumption of standard Ricardian models. These models typically assume that productivity parameters are uncorrelated across industries, meaning a country's likelihood of having comparative advantage in, say, motorcycle production should be completely independent of whether it has comparative advantage in car-making or coffee. But the data shows that revealed comparative advantage patterns are highly correlated across industries: the median industry-industry similarity score is 0.724 (with a mode of 0.74–0.75), well above the 0.5 that would be expected in a random, uncorrelated world. This non-random structure — where countries tend to be good at clusters of related industries — is precisely what the product space and economic complexity literature has been built to explain, and these findings provide formal statistical evidence that the Ricardian assumption of independence is empirically invalid.
supporting_quotes: In addition, current Ricardian models assume that the relative productivity parameters are uncorrelated across industries. This implies that the likely productivity of a country in motorcycle production, for example, is equally independent of whether it currently has comparative advantage in car-making or in coffee. We provide evidence that appears to contradict this.
the distribution of these values is also roughly symmetric, but... distributed around a peak greater than 0.5 (median = 0.724, mode at 0.74-0.75).
paper_ground_truth.paper_title: Implied Comparative Advantage

questions.original_text: Does the standard Ricardian trade model correctly assume that a country's productivity advantage in one sector is unrelated to its advantage in other sectors?

questions.text: Between 1995 and 2016, did the product space framework predict export growth beyond what you'd expect just from knowing which industries and countries tend to grow fastest in general?
questions.source_paper: Implied Comparative Advantage

paper_ground_truth.answer: Yes, the implied comparative advantage signal adds genuine predictive power even after controlling for all industry-level and location-level fixed effects. When location and industry fixed effects are included — which absorb all average differences between countries and between sectors — the density residual (the gap between implied and actual comparative advantage) remains a highly significant predictor of export growth with a coefficient of -0.024 (p<0.001). The R² rises from 0.412 with fixed effects alone to 0.439 when the density residual is added. Crucially, the density residual is constructed entirely from base-year data, with no forward-looking information, so it's not capturing anything mechanical. This confirms that the product space contains genuine structural information about which specific country-industry combinations are under- or over-shooting their implied equilibrium.
supporting_quotes: Column 8 shows the baseline growth equation with location and industry fixed effects and initial location-industry size. Column 9 reintroduces the density residual and shows that its economic and statistical significance is undiminished. It is important to again point out that the density residual uses only base-year data and thus contains no information regarding future growth, while the coefficients on the fixed effects are calculated ex post.

data_points.metric: R² with fixed effects + density residual

paper_ground_truth.paper_title: Implied Comparative Advantage

questions.original_text: Does the product space framework predict export growth beyond what you'd expect just from knowing which industries and countries tend to grow fastest in general?

questions.text: How did the predictive power of the product space framework compare across different subnational contexts in the early 2000s — was it stronger for the US (2003), India (1990), or Chile (2005)?
questions.source_paper: Implied Comparative Advantage

paper_ground_truth.answer: The framework performs well across all three countries but with interesting variation. For explaining current industry composition, US establishment data shows the strongest fit (R²=0.795), followed by Chile (R²=0.697), India's employment (R²=0.403), and US employment (R²=0.275). For predicting growth, India stands out with the highest R² at 0.428 for employment growth over 1990–2005, suggesting that India's industrial evolution was particularly tightly constrained by relatedness during this period of rapid structural change. Chile's establishment growth prediction is also strong (R²=0.327), while US employment growth is somewhat lower (R²=0.220). The variation between employment and establishment data in the US (R²=0.220 vs. R²=0.231) suggests the framework picks up industry-location dynamics at multiple margins. Overall, the consistency across three very different country contexts — a high-income, a middle-income, and a large emerging economy — strengthens confidence that the product space mechanism is a general feature of economic geography.
supporting_quotes: Table 5 shows the regressions for the US, India and Chile datasets. In all cases, both PS and CS density are significant (p < 0.001 in all cases); interestingly, they also yield coefficients that sum close to unity on average (from 0.75 to 1.1). As with the export data, R² values are substantial, especially for establishments.

questions.text: As of 2016, what were the most closely related product pairs in international trade, and was there a pattern to which types of products clustered together?
questions.source_paper: Implied Comparative Advantage

paper_ground_truth.answer: The most closely related product pairs in international trade are dominated by machinery and electrical equipment. The single most similar pair is gaskets and ship propellers, with a similarity score of 0.982, followed by electrical furnaces and specialized machines at 0.977. This strong clustering of machinery products aligns with earlier economic complexity research showing that machinery and capital equipment industries form a densely interconnected core of the product space — countries that export one type of machine tend to have the capabilities to export many others. This makes intuitive sense: manufacturing complex machinery requires overlapping sets of engineering knowledge, precision manufacturing capabilities, and supply chain access that generalize across many specific product categories.
supporting_quotes: The list of most similar product pairs contains a mix of different categories, though the lower half of the list is dominated by machinery and electrical products. This matches the observation in Hausmann et al. (2014) that such industries are highly interconnected.

questions.text: Between 1995 and 2016, why did Latin American countries appear less predictable in their export evolution than their income levels would suggest?
questions.source_paper: Implied Comparative Advantage

paper_ground_truth.answer: When countries are grouped by region, Latin America and the Caribbean show the lowest export predictability (R²=0.167), which is unexpectedly low given their income levels. By comparison, Europe and Central Asia scores the highest (R²=0.211), followed by South Asia (0.187), the Middle East and North Africa (0.183), and East Asia and the Pacific (0.180). The finding suggests that Latin American countries' export trajectories deviate more from what their existing capabilities would imply — potentially reflecting commodity boom-and-bust cycles, greater dependence on natural resource exports whose dynamics are geologically rather than capability-driven, or structural diversification patterns that don't follow the product space as closely. The regional ranking broadly mirrors income levels except for Latin America, which stands out as an outlier on the low side of predictability relative to its development stage.
supporting_quotes: Finally, the results by region appear to fall roughly in order of (non-oil) income (unlike looking at income directly); this would make Latin America and the Caribbean somewhat less predictable than expected based on income alone.

questions.text: As of 2018, Albania exported most of its packing lids to Italy and Greece, but how significant was Albania as a supplier to those markets — and were those even the biggest markets for packing lids globally?
questions.source_paper: Accelerating Growth in Albania through Targeted Investment Promotion

paper_ground_truth.answer: Despite Italy and Greece accounting for around 80% of Albanian packing lid exports, Albania supplies well under 1% of each country's demand for packing lids. To compound the issue, neither Italy nor Greece are major global markets for the product — Italy represents only 2.5% of global demand and Greece just 0.2%. The larger global markets are countries like Germany and France, where Albania currently has almost no presence. This means Albania's packing lid exports are heavily concentrated in small, niche markets where it holds a negligible share, leaving significant untapped potential in larger European markets.

questions.text: What happened to Albania's packing lid exports after the 2008 Global Financial Crisis through the 2010s, and why did they decline?
questions.source_paper: Accelerating Growth in Albania through Targeted Investment Promotion

paper_ground_truth.answer: Albania's packing lid exports grew substantially in the second half of the 2000s but stagnated and then declined following the Global Financial Crisis. The key reason was competitive displacement: Albania lost market share specifically in Greece, which continued to import packing lids throughout the 2010s but shifted its sourcing away from Albania. Given that Greece (along with Italy) accounted for about 80% of Albanian packing lid export demand, this shift in Greek import sourcing had an outsized negative effect on Albania's overall performance in this product category.
supporting_quotes: Albania's packing lids exports increased substantially in the second half of the 2000s, but then stagnated and declined after the Global Financial Crisis. This was largely due to losing market share to Greece (which continued to import packing lids in the 2010s, but not much from Albania).

questions.text: Between 2000 and 2018, how did Namibia's economic complexity compare to its regional peers, and was this gap consistent over that period?
questions.source_paper: A Growth Diagnostic of Namibia

paper_ground_truth.answer: Namibia's Economic Complexity Index (ECI) ranks among the lowest of its regional and international peers, and this has been a persistent feature for at least two decades — not a recent development. Among its peer group, only Angola ranks lower than Namibia in economic complexity. This consistently low ECI is rooted in structural features of Namibia's export basket: it is composed mostly of primary products, has low diversity, and the products it does export tend to have high ubiquity (meaning many other countries also export them). Notably, no product in Namibia's current export basket has a Product Complexity Index (PCI) above the global median, and the sectors that account for most of the country's export diversity — Agriculture and Mineral products — are themselves low-complexity sectors.
supporting_quotes: Namibia's ECI is amongst the lowest of its regional and international peers, with an export basket composed mostly of primary products.
Low ECI has been a constant for the previous two decades, surpassing only Angola among the group of regional and international peers.

no product in its current export basket displays an average PCI above the global median, and the products that concentrate most of the country's diversity – Agriculture and Mineral products – tend to be of low complexity.

data_points.metric: Products with PCI above global median

questions.text: Namibia's exports grew sevenfold between 2000 and 2018 — but did that mean the country became more economically complex over that same period?
questions.source_paper: A Growth Diagnostic of Namibia

paper_ground_truth.answer: Not really. While Namibia's net exports multiplied by a factor of 7 between 2000 and 2018 — driven mostly by diamonds, copper, zinc, and gold — this impressive export growth did not translate into greater economic complexity. The ECI remained consistently low throughout the entire period. The growth was concentrated in primary commodity exports, which are low-complexity products with high ubiquity. Furthermore, despite the volume of export growth, Namibia did not achieve substantive gains in global market shares for its main products, with trajectories remaining highly volatile. So the export boom was largely a price and demand-driven phenomenon tied to the global commodity super-cycle, rather than a reflection of Namibia building new, more complex productive capabilities.
supporting_quotes: until 2018, net exports multiplied by a factor of 7, driven mostly by higher demand for diamonds, copper, zinc, and gold, and outperforming most of its peers.
growth in exports has not been accompanied with substantive growth in market shares of its main products, with highly volatile trajectories.
Low ECI has been a constant for the previous two decades, surpassing only Angola among the group of regional and international peers.

questions.text: As of 2018, what did Namibia's product space position tell us about its future diversification potential?
questions.source_paper: A Growth Diagnostic of Namibia

paper_ground_truth.answer: Namibia's product space position paints a challenging picture for future diversification. Most of Namibia's exported products lie at the periphery of the product space and are distant from each other, which means there are very few nearby products that Namibia could feasibly jump into using its existing capabilities. This is captured by Namibia's Complexity Outlook Index (COI), which is low — indicating few absent complex products that are close enough to Namibia's current know-how to be reachable in the near term. Moreover, when looking at density (a measure of how close Namibia is to exporting each non-exported product) across all export categories, Namibia scores lower than the average of its regional and international peers in every single category. Together, these metrics suggest that the structural barriers to upgrading into more complex exports are significant.
supporting_quotes: Most of Namibian export products lie at the periphery of the product space and distant from each other, which leaves very few potential nearby jumps.

The country's Complexity Outlook Index (COI), which captures the number of absent complex products that demand knowhow and productive capabilities that are similar to those in place, shows that Namibia has few complex products within a short distance.

questions.text: Between 2003 and 2018, was Namibia diversifying its export base, and if so, was it moving into more complex products?
questions.source_paper: A Growth Diagnostic of Namibia

paper_ground_truth.answer: Namibia has actually been diversifying its export base at a rate above the average country given its productive capabilities — outperforming most of its peers on this measure. The country has successfully entered products adjacent to its existing capabilities. However, the quality of this diversification is a major concern: of all the value added generated by products Namibia entered since 2003, a striking 95% corresponds to products with a PCI below the global mean. The new products are mostly in transport, metals, and stone — sectors that are relatively low in complexity. So while Namibia is diversifying, it is diversifying sideways rather than upward along the complexity ladder, adding breadth in low-complexity space rather than breaking into the more sophisticated products that tend to support sustained, inclusive growth.
supporting_quotes: Over the previous decades, Namibia has been able to diversify into products which are adjacent to its exiting capabilities. As a matter of fact, the country has been able to diversify differentially more than the average country and more that most of its peers, given its current set of productive capabilities.
Out of the products added since 2003, 95% of the value added corresponds to products with an average PCI lower than the global mean, mostly transport, metals, and stone products.

questions.text: As of 2018, which sectors actually contributed positively to Namibia's economic complexity, and why were most sectors a drag?
questions.source_paper: A Growth Diagnostic of Namibia

paper_ground_truth.answer: Only one sector — Chemicals and plastics — has an average weighted PCI higher than zero, meaning it is the only sector contributing positively to Namibia's economic complexity. All other sectors in Namibia's export basket have weighted PCIs at or below zero, which pulls the country's overall ECI down. This is because the sectors that dominate Namibia's export diversity — Agriculture and Mineral products — are inherently low-complexity sectors. The country has no exported products with a PCI above the global median, so even the "best" products in Namibia's basket are only average or below-average in terms of embedded know-how and sophistication.

questions.text: By 2019, how trade-dependent was Namibia's economy, and did the composition of its trade between 2000 and 2018 reflect that dependence?
questions.source_paper: A Growth Diagnostic of Namibia

paper_ground_truth.answer: Namibia is highly trade-dependent: foreign trade represents 84% of GDP as of 2019, making it pivotal to the entire economy. Despite this deep integration with global markets, the composition of trade does not reflect a sophisticated or diversified export structure. The export basket is dominated by primary products — diamonds, copper, zinc, and gold have been the main drivers of export growth — and no exported product exceeds the global median in product complexity. This creates a vulnerability: a country so reliant on trade, but with exports concentrated in volatile commodity markets, faces significant risk when commodity cycles turn. The 7x growth in net exports between 2000 and 2018 was largely tied to the commodity super-cycle, and market shares for those key products remained volatile throughout.
supporting_quotes: Foreign trade represents 84% of GDP in 2019 and is pivotal to its economy.
until 2018, net exports multiplied by a factor of 7, driven mostly by higher demand for diamonds, copper, zinc, and gold.

questions.text: Between 2010 and 2018, was Namibia's diversification performance genuinely impressive, or did the 95% low-complexity figure from 2003–2018 undercut that story?
questions.source_paper: A Growth Diagnostic of Namibia

paper_ground_truth.answer: Namibia's diversification record presents a genuine paradox. On one hand, Namibia has diversified faster than the average country given its productive capabilities, and faster than most of its regional and international peers — which is a meaningful achievement for a low-complexity economy. On the other hand, the content of that diversification is almost entirely low-complexity: 95% of value added from products entered since 2003 comes from products with a PCI below the global mean. The new exports — primarily transport, metals, and stone products — are adjacent to existing capabilities but do not represent a significant step up the complexity ladder. This means Namibia is good at doing what its current capabilities allow, but its current capabilities are themselves limited. The Complexity Outlook Index confirms this: few complex products are within reach, and density across all export categories lags peers. So the diversification story is real but bounded — Namibia is maximizing a constrained opportunity set rather than expanding it.
supporting_quotes: the country has been able to diversify differentially more than the average country and more that most of its peers, given its current set of productive capabilities.
Out of the products added since 2003, 95% of the value added corresponds to products with an average PCI lower than the global mean, mostly transport, metals, and stone products.
Put in a different way, within the context of low ECI, the country has been able to materialize diversification opportunities by conquering adjacent products.
Namibia has few complex products within a short distance.

data_points.value: low — few complex products within reach

questions.text: How did Jordan's economic complexity ranking change from 2009 to 2019, and what did that decline imply for its growth prospects through 2029?
questions.source_paper: What Will It Take for Jordan to Grow?

paper_ground_truth.answer: Jordan's economic complexity ranking declined significantly over the 2010s — from 47th in the world in 2009 to 65th by 2019, a drop of 18 places. This deterioration reflects a shift in Jordan's export composition away from more complex, higher-value goods, partly driven by the collapse of key regional trade partners like Iraq and Syria. Despite this decline, Jordan's current level of complexity relative to its GDP per capita still implies an annualized growth projection of about 2.5% over the next decade — slightly above its recent de facto steady state, but modest given the country's potential.
supporting_quotes: Jordan was the 47th most complex economy in the world in 2009, based on the composition of its exports, and had fallen to 65th by 2019.
This level of complexity in relation to Jordan's GDP per capita implies an annualized growth projection over the next ten years of 2.5% — slightly above the de facto steady state.

questions.text: During the 2010s, Jordan exported more complex goods to its neighbors than to the US — what explains this pattern, and what happened when those regional markets collapsed after 2014?
questions.source_paper: What Will It Take for Jordan to Grow?

paper_ground_truth.answer: Jordan's export basket to neighboring countries is notably more diverse and complex than what it ships to the rest of the world, including the United States. Iraq alone once accounted for 15–20% of Jordan's goods exports before the Daesh conflict disrupted trade routes. As these regional markets — particularly Iraq and Syria — collapsed, Jordan's export concentration shifted toward the US and other MENA markets. However, the exports that grew toward the US were heavily concentrated in lower-complexity products like garments, rather than the more sophisticated goods Jordan had been selling regionally. This shift helps explain part of Jordan's overall decline in economic complexity during the 2010s.
supporting_quotes: Jordan exports a more diverse and more complex mix of exports to its neighbors than it does the rest of the world, and the United States in particular, and Iraq was a large destination for Jordanian exports prior to the Daesh conflict (15-20% of Jordan's goods exports).

Exports to some other markets did grow, but these tended to be focused within lower complexity — and hence lower-wage — products. For example, export growth to the United States continued but these were highly concentrated in garments.
As these traditionally important trade partners collapsed, the concentration of exports shifted, most prominently to the United States, and to other MENA markets in more recent years.

questions.text: As of 2018, how large was the gap between Jordan's actual and predicted exports to Israel, and what did this suggest about untapped trade potential?
questions.source_paper: What Will It Take for Jordan to Grow?

paper_ground_truth.answer: The gap between Jordan's actual and predicted exports to Israel is striking. In 2018, Jordan's actual exports to Israel were under $100 million, while a gravity model — which estimates trade flows based on economic size and proximity — predicted exports of over $400 million. This means Jordan is exporting less than a quarter of what its geographic and economic relationship with Israel would suggest. Gravity model analysis also shows that Jordan generally over-exports to most of its large trading partners but under-exports specifically to Israel, suggesting that non-economic barriers are suppressing what could be a much more significant trade relationship. Notably, product-level analysis identifies pharmaceuticals like serums and vaccines as potential growth areas for Jordanian exports to Israel.
supporting_quotes: Whereas actual exports to Israel in 2018 (as captured by UN COMTRADE) were under US$100 million, predicted exports were upward of US$400 million.
Jordan appears to over-export to most of its large trading partners versus the model's prediction, and under-export to only a few, most notably Israel.
serums and vaccines may have potential to grow in Israel or France.

questions.text: What happened to Jordan's total exports as a share of GDP between 2008 and 2016, and what was driving the partial recovery through 2019?
questions.source_paper: What Will It Take for Jordan to Grow?

paper_ground_truth.answer: Jordan's total exports collapsed dramatically from 56% of GDP in 2008 to just 34% of GDP in 2016 — a decline of 22 percentage points over eight years. This was driven by a combination of shocks including the Global Financial Crisis, regional instability, and the loss of key trade corridors through Syria and Iraq. Goods exports never recovered as a share of GDP after the GFC, continuing to decline throughout the decade. However, a partial recovery began after 2016, led primarily by tourism (services exports), which had fallen mid-decade but began growing again. This recovery was then cut short by COVID-19's devastating impact on global travel.
supporting_quotes: Exports collapsed from 56% of GDP in 2008 to 34% of GDP in 2016, but export recovery had begun, led by tourism, as exports climbed gradually each of the next three years.
Exports of goods never recovered as a share of GDP after the GFC and in fact continued to decline throughout the decade.
Service exports fell as a share of GDP in the middle of the last decade and had begun to grow until COVID-19 introduced an unprecedented shock to global travel and tourism.

questions.text: As of 2019, which countries had the most complex export baskets in the world, and how did they compare to each other?
questions.source_paper: A Measure of Countries' Distance to Frontier Based on Comparative Advantage

paper_ground_truth.answer: Based on a distance-to-frontier ranking that measures how similar a country's export basket is to the technological frontier (defined by the US, Japan, and Germany), the top five most complex exporters in 2019 are Japan (score 1.57), South Korea (1.41), Germany (1.38), Switzerland (1.34), and Czech Republic (1.29). Notably, Czech Republic outranks many larger economies, reflecting the deep manufacturing integration of Eastern European countries into global supply chains. This ranking correlates very strongly with GDP per capita (0.83), meaning export complexity and economic prosperity tend to go hand in hand.

questions.text: Between 1995 and 2019, how did East Asia's export sophistication change compared to countries like Argentina and Russia?
questions.source_paper: A Measure of Countries' Distance to Frontier Based on Comparative Advantage

paper_ground_truth.answer: Between 1995 and 2019, there is a striking divergence in export sophistication across countries. East Asian economies that have been rapidly catching up — China, South Korea, Vietnam, and Thailand — all show increasingly similar export baskets to the global frontier (US, Japan, Germany). This reflects their successful climb up the value chain toward more complex manufactured goods. In sharp contrast, more stagnant economies like Argentina, Russia, and South Africa saw their export similarity with the frontier remain flat or even decline over the same period. This divergence neatly maps onto economic performance: the countries upgrading their exports grew faster, while those whose export baskets stagnated or regressed fell further behind the frontier.
supporting_quotes: Moreover, over time, the export baskets of countries that have been rapidly catching up like China, South Korea, or Vietnam, have become increasingly similar to the frontier (panel (b)).
By contrast, the export similarity with the frontier of more stagnant countries like Argentina, Russia, or South Africa remained constant or even declined (panel (c)).

questions.text: As of 2019, how well did export complexity predict a country's income compared to other standard economic measures?
questions.source_paper: A Measure of Countries' Distance to Frontier Based on Comparative Advantage

paper_ground_truth.answer: The distance-to-frontier ranking, which is based purely on comparative advantage in trade data, correlates remarkably well with a range of standard economic development measures. Its correlation with GDP per capita is 0.83 — matching exactly the correlation with capital per worker (0.83) and close to government effectiveness (0.86), R&D intensity (0.80), and human capital (0.77). What's striking is that a measure derived entirely from a country's export structure — with no direct input of income, investment, or policy data — achieves predictive accuracy on par with fundamental drivers of growth like physical and human capital. This suggests export complexity serves as a powerful summary statistic of a country's productive capabilities.
supporting_quotes: The ranking is very robust and highly correlated with GDP per capita (~.83).
log(RD) is the 19-year centered moving average of a country's log share of GDP invested in R&D
GE is 'government effectiveness' from the Worldwide Governance Indicators
log(HC) is the log Human Capital Index
log(K/L) is the log capital stock per capita

data_points.metric: rank correlation with GDP per capita
rank correlation with capital per worker
