questions.text: How did Ireland's export specialization change between 1995 and 2012, and what does it reveal about the country's economic complexity trajectory?
questions.source_paper: A Tale of Two Clusters: The Evolution of Ireland's Economic Complexity since 1995

paper_ground_truth.answer: Ireland's export structure has undergone a dramatic transformation since 1995, becoming increasingly specialized rather than diversified. The number of products exported with Revealed Comparative Advantage (RCA) has been shrinking, and Ireland's diversity level now approaches that of oil-rich Norway — a country whose specialization is driven by a single natural resource. The composition has shifted heavily toward pharmaceuticals and chemicals, while the share from food and agriculture has declined (though total food export values have grown). Strikingly, the correlation between Ireland's RCA values in 1995 and 2012 is low compared to other Northern European countries, meaning Ireland hasn't just specialized — it has radically changed its export mix. Meanwhile, its complexity rank has been declining relative to peer nations, and its GDP per capita is high relative to what its Economic Complexity Index would predict, suggesting limited growth potential at the current complexity level unless the capability base is broadened.
supporting_quotes: Over the past decade Ireland's coverage of the Product Space, in terms of the number of products exported with RCA, has been shrinking.
Ireland's diversity has been decreasing – its level of specialisation increasing - and today is very high within the context of similar nations, approaching that of oil-rich Norway.

We observe a low correlation value within the context of other Northern European countries suggesting that Ireland has not only specialised, but also radically changed its export mix relative to its neighbours during this period.
Relative to similar countries and the most complex countries, Ireland exhibits declining complexity rank.

questions.text: Between 1995 and 2012, why was Ireland's high GDP per capita seen as a potential vulnerability from an economic complexity perspective?
questions.source_paper: A Tale of Two Clusters: The Evolution of Ireland's Economic Complexity since 1995

paper_ground_truth.answer: Economic complexity theory predicts that a country's income level should broadly align with the sophistication of its export basket. Ireland breaks this pattern: its GDP per capita is high relative to other countries with comparable Economic Complexity Index (ECI) scores — both in 1995 and in 2012. This creates a negative residual in the GDP-ECI relationship, and that residual has been growing more negative over time. Even when chemicals and pharmaceuticals are stripped out of the analysis (to test whether those sectors are artificially inflating apparent complexity), the negative residual persists and worsens, suggesting the gap is not just a measurement artifact. In complexity theory, being wealthy relative to your complexity level implies limited upside growth potential — you've already captured more income than your observable capability base would typically support, leaving less room for complexity-driven growth.
supporting_quotes: Ireland's GDP per capita relative to other countries with comparable ECI is high in both 1995 and 2012, suggesting limited growth potential at the current complexity level.
In this case, we observe an increasing negative residual, further confirming that Ireland exhibits high wealth compared to its observable capability base, and diversity, as captured by exports.
We observe a high level of specialisation in Ireland's export structure, coupled with high income per capita as compared to the complexity level of its industrial activities.

questions.text: As of 2013, what two distinct clusters does Ireland occupy in the Product Space, and what are the implications of this dual structure?
questions.source_paper: A Tale of Two Clusters: The Evolution of Ireland's Economic Complexity since 1995

paper_ground_truth.answer: Ireland sits in two separate regions of the Product Space. The first is a peripheral but complex cluster dominated by high-tech electronics and chemicals/pharmaceuticals — industries that are sophisticated but relatively isolated from other product groups, making it harder to leverage them as stepping stones. The second is a denser, central cluster of foodstuffs, animals, and agriculture — slightly less complex but well-connected to neighboring products, offering better diversification pathways. This dual structure is unusual and creates a strategic tension: the high-tech cluster generates most of Ireland's export value and is dominated by foreign multinationals, but its peripherality in the Product Space limits organic spillovers. The food/agriculture cluster is more domestically owned and better positioned structurally for related diversification, even if each individual product is less complex.
supporting_quotes: Figure 5 shows that today Ireland occupies two distinct clusters in the Product Space – one including mainly complex but peripheral industries such as high tech/electronics and chemicals/pharmaceuticals, and a second cluster in the densely connected, but slightly less complex, central region of the Product Space including foodstuffs, animals and agriculture.

questions.text: By 2012, which countries did Ireland's export basket most resemble, and how did that compare to its peer group in 1995?
questions.source_paper: A Tale of Two Clusters: The Evolution of Ireland's Economic Complexity since 1995

paper_ground_truth.answer: Ireland's export basket has shifted dramatically in terms of which countries it resembles. In 1995, Ireland was positioned in the Country Space between the Scandinavian nations and Canada — broadly diversified, mid-complexity European-style economies. By 2012, Ireland had migrated to cluster with highly specialized high-tech Asian export economies such as Japan, Singapore, and South Korea. Even more specifically, Ireland's current Product Space is found to be most similar to Singapore and Hong Kong — city-state economies known for extreme specialization in high-tech and services. This is a striking finding because Ireland is a geographically and institutionally European nation that has come to resemble Asian tiger economies in its export structure.
supporting_quotes: In 1995, Ireland was positioned between the Scandinavian nations and Canada, and the Eastern European bloc. However, in 2012 Ireland had migrated to be more similar to countries reliant on high tech exports such as Japan, Singapore and South Korea.
We find, somewhat surprisingly, that the current Product Space of Ireland appears to be most similar to those of highly specialised high-tech Asian economies such as Singapore and Hong Kong.

questions.text: Between 2002 and 2012, how did the complexity of products Ireland was gaining and losing RCA in change over time?
questions.source_paper: A Tale of Two Clusters: The Evolution of Ireland's Economic Complexity since 1995

paper_ground_truth.answer: Ireland's pattern of product appearances and disappearances reveals a concerning shift in complexity. Between 2002 and 2008, the mean complexity of newly appearing products (those gaining RCA) was rising — Ireland was successfully entering more sophisticated industries. But since 2008, the complexity of new product appearances has been falling, meaning Ireland is gaining RCA in less complex products. At the same time, disappearances have been increasing across almost all sectors, with chemicals and electronics — Ireland's most complex industries — showing particularly high rates of exit. Lower-complexity sectors like food, animals, and agriculture have been gaining products since 2005 while losing fewer. Together, this paints a picture of a post-2008 complexity downshift: Ireland is exiting high-complexity niches faster than it is entering them, and its new product gains are concentrated in less sophisticated areas.
supporting_quotes: After a sustained rise in the mean complexity of product appearances between 2002 and 2008, after 2008 the complexity of new products exported with RCA has been falling.
Almost all sectors have seen an increase in the number of disappearances in recent years, in particular chemicals and electronics.
Lower complexity sectors such as foodstuffs, animals and agriculture have shown an increase in the number of products exported with RCA since 2005.

questions.text: Between 2000 and 2012, did the Product Space accurately predict what new products Ireland would export, and did that differ between domestic and foreign-owned industries?
questions.source_paper: A Tale of Two Clusters: The Evolution of Ireland's Economic Complexity since 1995

paper_ground_truth.answer: The predictive power of the Product Space — the idea that countries tend to develop new export capabilities in products that are 'nearby' in capability space — varies significantly depending on whether you look at domestic or foreign-owned sectors in Ireland. For domestically owned industries, the Product Space has become increasingly predictive over time: new product appearances are better explained by proximity to existing capabilities, suggesting that Ireland's indigenous firms have been building and expanding their underlying capability base in a coherent way. However, for foreign-owned sectors, the opposite is true — the Product Space's predictive power has been decreasing over time. This suggests that foreign multinationals are not generating spillovers into the broader Irish economy. The capabilities embedded in foreign-owned high-tech firms appear too distant from existing local capabilities to transfer effectively to the indigenous workforce.
supporting_quotes: Appearances in domestic sectors became more predictable over time suggesting that an expansion of the underlying capability base has given rise to new products as modelled by the Product Space.

In the latter case we observe a decrease in the prediction power of the Product Space structure, suggesting a lack of spill-over from the high-tech foreign sector possibly due to a lack of transfer of sufficient capabilities to the indigenous workforce due to their relative distance from existing local capabilities.
paper_ground_truth.paper_title: A Tale of Two Clusters: The Evolution of Ireland's Economic Complexity since 1995

questions.original_text: Does the Product Space accurately predict what new products Ireland will export, and does that differ between domestic and foreign-owned industries?

questions.text: As of 2011–2012, how dominant were foreign multinationals in Ireland's most complex export sectors compared to domestic firms?
questions.source_paper: A Tale of Two Clusters: The Evolution of Ireland's Economic Complexity since 1995

paper_ground_truth.answer: Foreign multinationals are overwhelmingly dominant in Ireland's highest-complexity export sectors, with the gaps being especially stark in turnover. In chemicals and pharmaceuticals, foreign firms outstrip domestic firms by a factor of 20 in annual turnover and 5 in employees. In electronics and computers, despite Ireland having roughly twice as many domestic firms as foreign ones, foreign firms generate 21 times the turnover and employ 4 times as many people. Even in the food sector — which is more domestically oriented with 570 Irish firms versus just 37 foreign — foreign firms still generate higher per-firm turnover ($14m vs $12m). Overall, in 2011, a small number of foreign-dominated sectors accounted for fully one quarter of Ireland's total economy-wide gross value added.
supporting_quotes: Foreign firms are dominant in terms of number of enterprises, number of employees (by a factor of 5) and annual turnover (by a factor of 20) for the chemical and pharmaceutical sectors.

While Ireland has approximately twice as many domestic firms as compared to foreign-owned firms in electronics/computers, foreign firms dominate in terms of employees (by a factor of 4) and turnover (by a factor of 21).
In 2011, a small number of sectors dominated by foreign-owned multinational enterprises accounted for one quarter of total economy-wide gross value added.

data_points.metric: foreign share of gross value added

questions.original_text: How dominant are foreign multinationals in Ireland's most complex export sectors compared to domestic firms?

questions.text: Between 1995 and 2012, what trade partners drove Ireland's export growth, and what did the product composition of those trade flows reveal?
questions.source_paper: A Tale of Two Clusters: The Evolution of Ireland's Economic Complexity since 1995

paper_ground_truth.answer: Ireland's export growth has been concentrated in two distinct geographic directions with different product profiles. Trade with North America and Western Europe has been increasing since 1995, and this growth is primarily driven by the USA and Belgium — both of which are mainly purchasing pharmaceutical inputs. This reflects the multinational-dominated, pharmaceutical-heavy cluster in Ireland's export basket. Closer to home, the UK and Germany import a much broader range of products from Ireland, including food and agricultural goods — reflecting Ireland's second, more domestically-grounded cluster. This bifurcation in trade partners mirrors the dual cluster structure of Ireland's Product Space: complex, peripheral pharmaceutical exports going to specialized industrial buyers in the US and Belgium, while more diversified food and agricultural products flow to nearby European markets.
supporting_quotes: Export to the North America and Western Europe has been increasing since 1995, driven by the USA and Belgium, who mainly buy pharmaceutical inputs.
Closer to home, the UK and Germany also import a large range of products including food and agricultural products.
paper_ground_truth.paper_title: A Tale of Two Clusters: The Evolution of Ireland's Economic Complexity since 1995

questions.original_text: What trade partners are driving Ireland's export growth, and what does the product composition of those trade flows tell us?

questions.text: As of 2012, what pathway did the Product Space suggest Ireland could take to increase its economic complexity?
questions.source_paper: A Tale of Two Clusters: The Evolution of Ireland's Economic Complexity since 1995

paper_ground_truth.answer: Despite Ireland's declining complexity trend, its position in the Product Space is not without opportunity. Ireland's Opportunity Value — a measure of how well-positioned a country is to move into nearby complex products — is roughly as expected for its wealth level, indicating reasonably good structural positioning. Crucially, a large range of complex industries sit close to Ireland's current products in the Product Space. One concrete pathway identified is diversification into varied machinery sectors, which are in close proximity to Ireland's existing domestic cluster. These machinery sectors could then serve as a stepping stone toward even higher-complexity products like measurement instruments, which sit further along in the Product Space. This suggests a sequenced diversification strategy rooted in Ireland's existing, domestically-grounded capabilities rather than relying on the more isolated foreign-owned high-tech cluster.
supporting_quotes: Ireland's Opportunity Value is more or less as expected for its level of wealth, confirming that it is in a reasonably good position to move into nearby more complex industries.
In Ireland's case, a large range of complex industries are close to current products and hence Ireland is well-positioned in the Product Space to increase its aggregate complexity level.

Diversification into varied machinery sectors which are at close proximity to the current domestic cluster could be a stepping stone to higher complexity measurement instruments towards the left-hand side of the Product Space.
paper_ground_truth.paper_title: A Tale of Two Clusters: The Evolution of Ireland's Economic Complexity since 1995

questions.original_text: What pathway does the Product Space suggest Ireland could take to increase its economic complexity?

questions.text: Between 1995 and 2012, how did the stability of Ireland's export base differ between its high-complexity and low-complexity sectors?
questions.source_paper: A Tale of Two Clusters: The Evolution of Ireland's Economic Complexity since 1995

paper_ground_truth.answer: Ireland's complex and simpler sectors show very different dynamics in terms of stability and churn. High-complexity sectors such as chemicals, stone/glass, plastics, and machinery/electrical have experienced high and increasing churn — meaning frequent entry and exit of products in terms of RCA. In contrast, lower-complexity sectors like animals, food, vegetables, minerals, and wood show lower and in some cases declining churn, indicating greater stability and consolidation. This divergence is significant: the sectors that anchor Ireland's complexity credentials are highly volatile, with most sectors showing net negative product appearances after 2000 (positive 1995-2000 but negative thereafter). Meanwhile, Ireland's domestically-oriented, lower-complexity food and agricultural sectors are building more stable export bases. This raises questions about the durability of Ireland's complex export profile, which appears dependent on a relatively volatile foreign-driven industrial base.
supporting_quotes: There was high entry and exit of many sectors, with increasing churn in high complexity sectors such as stone/glass, plastics, chemicals and machinery/electrical.
Churn is lower for animals and food, and declining for vegetables, minerals and wood.
In many cases the net appearances of new industries was positive during the period 1995-2000, yet turned negative in later years.

questions.text: As of 2008, why was Pakistan's specialization in garments and textiles considered a structural trap rather than a foundation for growth?
questions.source_paper: Structural Transformation and Competitiveness in Pakistan

paper_ground_truth.answer: Pakistan's garments and textiles specialization is a trap because of how the product space is structured. While the garment cluster is internally tight — meaning once you can make pants, it's relatively easy to also make shorts or skirts — it is very weakly connected to the rest of the product space. Moving from pants to spark plugs or hard drives requires an almost entirely different set of capabilities. Pakistan has essentially fully saturated this garment cluster between 1975 and 2000 without making any substantial jumps to new areas of the product space. The problem is that continued consolidation within garments offers diminishing returns: there are few nearby products outside the cluster that Pakistan can realistically target, since the capabilities built in garments don't transfer well to higher-complexity industries. This is in sharp contrast to the Philippines, Thailand, and Indonesia, which used their garment footholds as a springboard — the Philippines jumped to electronics, while Thailand and Indonesia went further and penetrated the industrial core.
supporting_quotes: While this cluster is tightly connected within itself (i.e. once you make pants, it is easy to then make shorts and skirts) it is very weakly connected to the rest of the space (once you make pants, it is not much easier to make spark plugs or hard drives). Pakistan has almost fully occupied the tight cluster and seems to be left with few nearby options for structural transformation around these sectors.
Between 1975 and 2000 Pakistan did not make any substantial jumps and occupy new areas of the product space.

The Philippines has also occupied that cluster, but that country has also made the jump to another tightly-knit cluster, this one much better connected to the rest of the industrial core: electronics.

data_points.finding: High internal, very low external connectivity
Successfully moved from garments to electronics

questions.original_text: Why is Pakistan's specialization in garments and textiles considered a structural trap rather than a foundation for growth?

questions.text: As of 2006, how large was the export growth potential from Pakistan's 'low-hanging fruit' — products that were nearby in the product space?
questions.source_paper: Structural Transformation and Competitiveness in Pakistan

paper_ground_truth.answer: Despite being the most feasible path for export diversification, Pakistan's low-hanging fruit offers surprisingly limited upside. Even if Pakistan were to achieve revealed comparative advantage in all of the high-density nearby products (those in the 90th percentile of density, including things like frozen foods, oilseeds, dairy products, jewelry, and paper products), total exports would increase by only approximately $408 million, or about 2% of current exports. The analysis notes this is a conservative estimate, but even scaling it up tenfold would still represent a relatively modest gain. This finding underscores the fundamental challenge Pakistan faces: the nearby, achievable opportunities in the product space are simply not numerous or large enough to drive a major transformation of the export basket. Real structural transformation would require jumping to more distant, better-connected parts of the product space — which is precisely what Pakistan has failed to do over the past 40 years.
supporting_quotes: Even if Pakistan were able to achieve comparative advantage in the sectors listed in Table 1, exports would only increase by approximately $408 million, or 2%. This estimate is quite conservative, but even an increase of ten times that is relatively small.

data_points.metric: potential export increase from low-hanging fruit

questions.text: As of 2008, was it true that Pakistan could naturally 'move downstream' in textiles — say into more processed goods — as a viable diversification strategy?
questions.source_paper: Structural Transformation and Competitiveness in Pakistan

paper_ground_truth.answer: This is a commonly held belief, but the product space analysis suggests it's largely a myth for Pakistan. Downstream activities — products that use Pakistan's current outputs as physical inputs — are for the most part not on Pakistan's efficient frontier, meaning they don't offer the best combination of proximity and sophistication. More critically, downstream products are not actually closer to Pakistan's current location in the product space than non-downstream products. The intuition that 'we grow cotton, so we should make cotton shirts, then fashion garments, then luxury apparel' treats capability transfer as following physical input-output chains — but productive capabilities don't work that way. The Switzerland example is illustrative: Switzerland is world-renowned for chocolate but imports all its cocoa. Similarly, raw diamonds, cut diamonds, and jewelry are not adjacent in the product space despite their obvious vertical relationship. The capabilities needed to produce one stage of a value chain are often quite different from those needed for the next stage.
supporting_quotes: Although it is a commonly-held belief that presence of the physical input implies that moving downstream is a natural, logical progression in structural transformation, we can see that this is not the case: downstream products are not closer to Pakistan's current location in the product space than non-downstream activities.
Switzerland is renowned for its chocolate, but imports all of its cocoa.
Raw diamonds are not next to cut and polished diamonds which are not next to jewelry. Physical input-output relationships have little to do with the structure of the product space.

data_points.finding: No significant difference in product space distance
Competitiveness does not require domestic input production

questions.text: As of 2006, among Pakistan's stated industrial policy priorities — machinery, electronics, automobiles, pharmaceuticals, and chemicals — which was most accessible from Pakistan's current production capabilities, and which were least accessible?
questions.source_paper: Structural Transformation and Competitiveness in Pakistan

paper_ground_truth.answer: According to product space analysis, all of Pakistan's Vision 2030 priority industrial sectors are far from its current production capabilities, but there is meaningful variation among them. Chemicals is the closest sector in terms of product space density (0.12), making it the 'low-hanging fruit' of the strategy — but it also carries the lowest sophistication and strategic value among the priority sectors. Pharmaceuticals sits in a similar position of modest accessibility. Machinery, Electronics, and Automobiles are all very far away. Among these three, Machinery offers the best trade-off: it has the highest sophistication (PRODY ~$16,267) and strategic value, meaning it connects well to other high-value products. Electronics sits in the middle in terms of both distance and sophistication (~$13,967), while Automobiles are both far away and have the lowest sophistication (~$12,327), making them arguably the worst-positioned priority sector. The analysis suggests that if Pakistan were to pursue any of these ambitious targets, machinery would offer the most strategic leverage.
supporting_quotes: The Chemicals sector is the one sector that is closest, but also has the lowest level of sophistication and strategic value of the priority sectors. This would make it the 'low-hanging fruit' sector in the strategy.

Machinery, Automobiles, and Electronics are very far away, but compared to one another machinery has higher sophistication and strategic value, automobiles the lowest, and electronics somewhere in between.

data_points.finding: All far, machinery has highest strategic value

questions.text: As of 2008, what specific capability gaps were holding Pakistan back from accessing the largest new export markets, and how big were those markets?
questions.source_paper: Structural Transformation and Competitiveness in Pakistan

paper_ground_truth.answer: The capability gap analysis reveals that Pakistan's most impactful missing capabilities are in technical and business administration skills. If Pakistan could develop engine and machine assembler skills, it could potentially access markets worth $518 billion. General and operations management skills, if present, would unlock $327 billion in markets. Painter and transportation equipment skills would add another $244 billion in accessible markets. Beyond skills, non-traded input gaps are also significant: the absence of adequate water and sewage systems blocks access to grain farming (a $40 billion global market), while missing business support services prevent entry into analytical laboratory instrument manufacturing ($39 billion). Veterinary services gaps block animal production markets worth $17 billion. These findings show that Pakistan's export constraints are not just about being in the wrong part of the product space — specific missing capabilities are acting as bottlenecks that, if resolved, could unlock access to massive global markets. Sectors requiring only 1–2 missing capabilities (like farm machinery and equipment) are more feasibly targeted than those requiring 3 or more (like automobiles and pharmaceuticals).
supporting_quotes: Technical and business administration skills are missing in Pakistan, and their presence might add significantly to Pakistan's feasible set.

Water, sewage and other systems: Grain farming (40 billion); Business support services: Analytical laboratory instrument manufacturing (39 billion); Veterinary services: Animal production, except cattle and poultry and eggs (17 billion)
Those sectors missing only one or two capabilities are more likely to appear in similar countries over time, and should therefore be more likely to emerge in Pakistan.

data_points.metric: market unlocked by engine/machine assembler skills
market unlocked by general/operations manager skills
market unlocked by business support services

questions.text: As of 2008, how did the product space divergence between Pakistan, Bangladesh, and Sri Lanka on one hand, and Thailand and Indonesia on the other, explain their different growth trajectories?
questions.source_paper: Structural Transformation and Competitiveness in Pakistan

paper_ground_truth.answer: The product space offers a compelling structural explanation for these countries' diverging economic trajectories. Pakistan, Bangladesh, and Sri Lanka share very similar positions in the product space — all specialized in the garments and textiles cluster on the periphery, with few connections to the broader industrial economy. This peripheral position means structural transformation is inherently difficult: the capabilities built in these sectors transfer poorly to higher-complexity products. Thailand and Indonesia, by contrast, are in a completely different situation — they not only dominate garments and electronics but have also penetrated the industrial core of the product space, where products are densely connected and diversification is much easier. Since countries with higher open forest (better-connected product positions) grow their export sophistication faster, and since export sophistication is itself positively linked to GDP growth, this structural divergence compounds over time. The product space essentially predicts that Thailand and Indonesia will continue to upgrade more easily than Pakistan, Bangladesh, and Sri Lanka unless the latter group makes successful jumps to new, better-connected clusters.
supporting_quotes: Pakistan's orientation in the product space is similar to Bangladesh and Sri Lanka, which are also specialized in the garments and textiles area of the space.

Thailand and Indonesia are in a completely different situation when it comes to structural transformation, as they dominate not only garments and electronics, but have also penetrated the industrial core.
Countries with a high level of open forest enjoy faster subsequent growth in export sophistication and overall economic growth.

Countries that have managed to develop a sophisticated export package relative to their income level grow faster. However, countries specialized in relatively unsophisticated export baskets suffer lagging economic performance.

data_points.relationship: positive with future EXPY and GDP growth

questions.text: In 2008, what were Pakistan's most promising 'strategic bet' export sectors — products that required a bigger jump but offered high future value?
questions.source_paper: Structural Transformation and Competitiveness in Pakistan

paper_ground_truth.answer: Beyond the immediate low-hanging fruit, Pakistan's product space analysis identifies a set of 'strategic bet' sectors that are further along the efficient frontier — requiring more of a capability leap but offering significantly higher sophistication and strategic value. Key examples include paint and coating manufacturing (strategic value ~$12,595), plastics packaging materials, film and sheet (~$12,453), and tire manufacturing (~$11,151). These sectors are more sophisticated than Pakistan's current exports and better connected to the industrial core of the product space, meaning success in them would open up a wider range of subsequent diversification options. They represent a middle path between the modest nearby opportunities (dairy, jewelry, oilseeds) and the very distant Vision 2030 targets (machinery, electronics). For comparison, even Pakistan's nearby 'low-hanging fruit' like dairy products offer high sophistication scores but are in world markets of $48 billion, while jewelry represents a $174 billion global market — but the strategic bets in intermediates like plastics and paints offer the additional advantage of better connecting Pakistan to higher-complexity manufacturing.
supporting_quotes: Table 3 Further along the Efficient Frontier: Strategic Bets... Paint and coating manufacturing... Plastics packaging materials, film and sheet... Tire manufacturing...

In terms of the market size of the opportunities, the largest are aluminum, dairy, jewelry, oilseeds, and coffee/tea production. Dairy products in particular offer high sophistication and strategic value.

questions.text: In 2008, were labor-intensive sectors more accessible to Pakistan than to other developing countries with high unemployment?
questions.source_paper: Structural Transformation and Competitiveness in Pakistan

paper_ground_truth.answer: Yes — and this is one of the more positive findings in the analysis. Many labor-intensive products are relatively close to Pakistan's current location in the product space. Examples include nursery and forestry products and animal products, which require capabilities similar to those Pakistan already has. This is genuinely good news given Pakistan's large labor force and employment challenges. The contrast with South Africa is instructive: in South Africa, sectors intensive in unskilled labor are actually very far from current production in the product space, meaning diversification into job-creating industries would require much larger capability leaps. Pakistan's proximity to labor-intensive sectors means that — at least in terms of productive capabilities — there is a plausible path to export diversification that could also address employment. The caveat is that many of these labor-intensive nearby options are relatively unsophisticated and small in global market terms, so while they're accessible, they won't by themselves drive a major upgrade in export complexity.
supporting_quotes: Although relatively unsophisticated, many of these products are very near to Pakistan's current location in the product space, such as nursery & forestry products and animal products. Overall, labor-intensive products are relatively nearby for Pakistan, which is good news for the country.
Compare this to another country with an even larger unemployment problem: South Africa. In that country, sectors intensive in unskilled labor are very far away from current production.

data_points.finding: Relatively high — nearby in product space

questions.text: In 2008, why didn't vertical input-output relationships — like having cotton to make textiles — reliably predict which export sectors Pakistan could diversify into?
questions.source_paper: Structural Transformation and Competitiveness in Pakistan

paper_ground_truth.answer: The product space framework reveals a fundamental disconnect between physical input-output relationships and the actual similarity of productive capabilities across industries. The key insight is that what makes two products 'close' in the product space is whether they tend to be co-exported by the same countries — a proxy for whether they require similar underlying capabilities (labor skills, knowledge, institutions, infrastructure). Physical input-output chains don't capture this. The Switzerland-chocolate example is illustrative: Switzerland imports all its cocoa yet dominates global chocolate exports, because the relevant capabilities are in precision manufacturing, quality control, and product development — not raw material production. Even more strikingly, raw diamonds, cut diamonds, and jewelry are not adjacent in the product space despite forming an obvious vertical chain. For Pakistan, this means that having cotton doesn't make downstream apparel easy, and having apparel doesn't make downstream luxury goods or synthetics easy — each requires a largely different set of capabilities. Empirically, the capability-based (input-output) measure of product proximity has only one-third the predictive power of the outcomes-based (co-export probability) measure for forecasting structural transformation.
supporting_quotes: Switzerland is renowned for its chocolate, but imports all of its cocoa.
Raw diamonds are not next to cut and polished diamonds which are not next to jewelry. Physical input-output relationships have little to do with the structure of the product space.
Downstream products are not closer to Pakistan's current location in the product space than non-downstream activities.
Our outcomes-based measure of density has greater statistical and economic significance than the capability-based measure.

data_points.finding: Downstream products no closer than non-downstream products
World-class exporter imports all raw inputs

questions.text: In 2012–13, how did Sri Lanka's participation in producer-driven global value chains compare to its regional peers like Vietnam, Thailand, and Malaysia?
questions.source_paper: Can industrial zones address the binding constraints to Sri Lanka's growth?

paper_ground_truth.answer: Sri Lanka is dramatically behind its regional peers when it comes to producer-driven exports — the kind associated with cars, airplanes, and electronics manufacturing. Only about 6% of Sri Lanka's exports fall into producer-driven categories, compared to 34% for Vietnam, 59% for Thailand, and 69% for Malaysia. Meanwhile, Sri Lanka's export basket is dominated by buyer-driven products like garments, footwear, and gems, which account for 61% of exports. This gap suggests Sri Lanka has largely missed out on the global production-sharing networks that have propelled manufacturing-led growth in much of Southeast Asia.
supporting_quotes: Sri Lanka's exports are dominated by buyer-driven products (mainly garments, footwear and gems); there is very little export activity from sectors associated with producer-driven networks (such as car, airplane or electronics manufacturing).
Figure 4. Sri Lanka's low share of producer-driven exports suggests a lack of global production sharing

questions.text: As of 2015, what did Sri Lanka's intrafirm trade profile reveal about its integration into global value chains, and how did it compare to countries like Vietnam and Thailand?
questions.source_paper: Can industrial zones address the binding constraints to Sri Lanka's growth?

paper_ground_truth.answer: Sri Lanka's export basket is overwhelmingly concentrated in sectors with low intrafirm trade — 79% of its exports fall into these low-intrafirm-trade categories. This is a striking contrast to regional peers: only 43% of Vietnam's exports, 25% of Thailand's, and 29% of Malaysia's fall into the same low-intrafirm-trade bucket. Intrafirm trade matters because it signals deep integration into multinational production networks — for example, in the US market, 87% of automobile imports, 75% of pharmaceutical imports, and 67% of electrical machinery imports are transacted between related parties, versus only 15% for apparel. Since Sri Lanka specializes heavily in apparel-type goods, it misses out on the technology transfer, capital, and stable demand that come with being embedded in producer-driven, intrafirm trade networks.
supporting_quotes: In Sri Lanka, the export basket is largely lacking goods associated with high levels of intrafirm trade (Figure 5), indicating that the economy may in fact have issues attracting investment in those sectors.
in the US in 2000, 87% of automobile imports, 75% of pharmaceutical imports, and 67% of electrical machinery imports were from related parties, compared to only 15% of apparel imports
Figure 5. Sri Lanka has few exports in sectors dominated by intra-firm trade

questions.text: In 2016, how concentrated was global trade in selected agricultural products, and which markets dominated?
questions.source_paper: Opportunity analysis of agriculture products in Sri Lanka: Measuring markets and feasibility

paper_ground_truth.answer: Global trade in selected agricultural products is highly concentrated. Just five market blocs — the EU, USA, Middle East, East Asia, and India — account for 80.7% of total world trade in these products. The EU alone commands 42.0% of world imports (about $40.6 billion), making it by far the dominant market. The USA is the second-largest at 18.8% ($18.2 billion), followed by East Asia (China, Japan, South Korea, Singapore, Hong Kong) at 15.4% ($14.9 billion). This means that nearly any agricultural export strategy targeting these sectors must prioritize the EU and USA above all others.

East Asian Market (China, Japan, South Korea, Singapore, Hong Kong) 14.9 [USD, bn], 15.4% [of world]
Total 78.0 [USD, bn], 80.7% [of world]

questions.text: In 2016, which agricultural products had the largest global markets, and how did their growth trajectories between 2012 and 2016 compare?
questions.source_paper: Opportunity analysis of agriculture products in Sri Lanka: Measuring markets and feasibility

paper_ground_truth.answer: The four largest global markets among selected agricultural products are Shrimp ($15 billion), Plantains & Bananas ($14 billion), Cuttlefish/Octopus & Bivalves ($11 billion), and Tomatoes ($8.5 billion). However, market size and growth don't always align. Shrimp stands out as both the largest and fastest-growing product globally. Plantains & Bananas also have a very large market with moderate growth. Cuttlefish/Octopus & Bivalves have a large market growing at a moderately fast pace. Tomatoes, despite their large market, show low overall growth — with the EU market for tomatoes actually shrinking. This means Shrimp and Plantains offer the most attractive combination of scale and momentum, while Tomatoes represent a mature, slower-growth opportunity.

questions.text: Between 2012 and 2016, which agricultural products were growing fastest in global markets, and which ones should exporters have been cautious about?
questions.source_paper: Opportunity analysis of agriculture products in Sri Lanka: Measuring markets and feasibility

paper_ground_truth.answer: Among selected agricultural products, Shrimp, Pepper, Mango, Vanilla, Ginger, and Cinnamon are growing fastest in world markets between 2012–2016. A second tier of moderately growing products includes Cuttlefish/Octopus & Bivalves, Plantains & Bananas, Soursop & Passion Fruits, and Bell Pepper. Exporters should exercise caution with products showing limited growth — including Cucumber & Gherkin, Tomatoes, Clove, Pineapple, Strawberry, Cut Flowers, Live Fish, and Sea Cucumber. Most concerning are products with actively shrinking world markets: Cardamom, Nutmeg & Mace, Cassava, Live Plants, and Foliage & Branches. These declining global trends suggest structural demand shifts rather than temporary fluctuations.

questions.text: Between 2012 and 2016, how did the shrimp market vary across key global regions, and where was the biggest growth opportunity?
questions.source_paper: Opportunity analysis of agriculture products in Sri Lanka: Measuring markets and feasibility

paper_ground_truth.answer: Shrimp is the largest and fastest-growing agricultural product globally ($15 billion world market), but its growth profile differs significantly across regions. In the EU, US, and Middle East, shrimp markets are both large and experiencing considerable growth rates, making these the most attractive combination of size and momentum. In East Asia, while the market is also large, growth is comparatively slow. This regional divergence suggests that for shrimp exporters, Western markets (EU and US) and the Middle East offer better growth prospects despite East Asia being a significant buyer by volume.

questions.text: Between 2012 and 2016, where did Plantains & Bananas show the most promising growth opportunities across different regional markets?
questions.source_paper: Opportunity analysis of agriculture products in Sri Lanka: Measuring markets and feasibility

paper_ground_truth.answer: Plantains & Bananas have the second-largest global agricultural market at $14 billion, and their growth prospects vary considerably by region. The Middle East stands out as the most dynamic opportunity — combining a large market with high growth rates. East Asia offers moderate market size with moderate growth. By contrast, the EU and US, despite having the largest absolute markets for plantains, show slow growth, suggesting they are mature markets. This pattern makes the Middle East the highest-growth frontier for plantain exporters, even though the EU and US remain the largest destinations by volume.
supporting_quotes: Plantains & Bananas: Growing moderately fast with a very large world market; In East Asia, moderate growth and moderate market size; In EU & US, large market but slow growth; In Middle East, large market and high growth

questions.text: In 2016, Sri Lanka exported Cinnamon primarily to Mexico — is that consistent with where the biggest global markets for Cinnamon were between 2012 and 2016?
questions.source_paper: Opportunity analysis of agriculture products in Sri Lanka: Measuring markets and feasibility

paper_ground_truth.answer: Sri Lanka's primary Cinnamon export destination is Mexico, which is a notable mismatch with broader global market trends. Globally, the EU and USA together account for over 60% of world imports across selected agricultural sectors, and Cinnamon is identified as one of the fastest-growing products in the EU market specifically. Meanwhile, Mexico is not among the five major market blocs (EU, USA, Middle East, East Asia, India) that represent 80.7% of world agricultural trade. This suggests Sri Lanka may be underexploiting the high-growth EU and US markets for Cinnamon, where demand momentum is strongest.

questions.text: Between 2012 and 2016, how did Pepper compare to other spices in terms of global market opportunity, and what did Sri Lanka's export performance look like for it by 2016?
questions.source_paper: Opportunity analysis of agriculture products in Sri Lanka: Measuring markets and feasibility

paper_ground_truth.answer: Pepper dominates the global spice market by a substantial margin, and it is among the fastest-growing products worldwide between 2012–2016. Its growth is high-to-moderate across the EU, US, and Middle East. However, the East Asian market for Pepper is actually shrinking — an important regional nuance. For Sri Lanka, Pepper is one of the two largest agricultural exports (alongside Cinnamon), and both are growing, though Cinnamon is growing faster. The combination of Pepper's dominant spice market position, strong Western market growth, and established Sri Lankan export presence makes it a key strategic product — though exporters should be aware of the declining East Asian demand.

questions.text: Between 2012 and 2016, was the Sea Cucumber market a good opportunity in East Asia, and what did trade data say about it?
questions.source_paper: Opportunity analysis of agriculture products in Sri Lanka: Measuring markets and feasibility

paper_ground_truth.answer: Despite being targeted for development in Sri Lanka, Sea Cucumber faces a shrinking market in East Asia — its primary destination. This is a significant red flag from a market attractiveness standpoint. East Asia (China, Japan, South Korea, Singapore, Hong Kong) represents 15.4% of world imports for selected agricultural products, and while it is a major seafood market overall, the demand trend for Sea Cucumber within that bloc is declining between 2012–2016. Additionally, Sea Cucumber is classified globally as a product with limited growth. This combination of regional market contraction and weak global momentum suggests Sea Cucumber carries substantial market risk as an export priority.

questions.text: Between 2012 and 2016, how did the growth dynamics of the floriculture sector differ across product types, and what did this mean for export strategy?
questions.source_paper: Opportunity analysis of agriculture products in Sri Lanka: Measuring markets and feasibility

paper_ground_truth.answer: The floriculture sector shows a stark split in global market dynamics. Cut Flowers have a significant market size but very low growth, making them a large but mature category. Live Plants and Foliage & Branches, by contrast, are actively shrinking in world markets — classifying them among the most concerning products from an export perspective. Interestingly, Live Plants show high growth rates in East Asia specifically (2012–2016), which is an exception to the global trend. For Cut Flowers, the EU market is one of the largest globally, but growth there is slower — meaning the overall volume opportunity exists but the market is competitive and stagnant. Export strategies in floriculture should therefore focus on Cut Flowers for volume while being cautious about Live Plants and Foliage given declining global demand.
supporting_quotes: Live Plants and Foliage are shrinking on the world market. Cut Flowers have a significant market size and low market growth.
While Pineapple, Cinnamon, Vanilla, Plantains & Bananas, Live Plants, and Strawberries have the highest growth rates [in East Asia].
Plantains & Bananas, Bell Pepper, and Cut Flowers have large markets but lower growth rates [in EU].
Cardomom, Nutmeg & Mace, Cassava, Live Plants, Foliage & Branches have shrinking markets

questions.text: Between 2012 and 2016, how did fruit export opportunities from Sri Lanka compare across different products in terms of market size and growth?
questions.source_paper: Opportunity analysis of agriculture products in Sri Lanka: Measuring markets and feasibility

paper_ground_truth.answer: Among fruits, Plantains & Bananas dominate with the largest global market and a considerable growth rate, making them the strongest large-scale opportunity. Soursop & Passion Fruit has a smaller market but is also growing strongly, suggesting emerging demand. Mango & Guava show a higher growth rate than most fruits but have a comparatively smaller market size. Strawberry and Pineapple are growing more slowly with market sizes similar to other fruits excluding Plantains. From Sri Lanka's perspective, most fruit exports are small in scale but growing very fast, which aligns with the global growth trajectory of products like Mango — suggesting there is momentum to build on, even if volumes are currently low.

questions.text: By 2016, which products should exporters have prioritized for the EU market — considering both market size and growth between 2012 and 2016?
questions.source_paper: Opportunity analysis of agriculture products in Sri Lanka: Measuring markets and feasibility

paper_ground_truth.answer: The EU is by far the largest single market for selected agricultural products, accounting for 42% of world imports. Within the EU, the most attractive opportunities differ by risk profile. Mango, Vanilla, Ginger, Turmeric, and Cinnamon offer the highest growth rates, making them dynamic opportunities even if their absolute market size may be smaller. Shrimp and Cuttlefish/Octopus & Bivalves combine large EU markets with considerable growth rates — offering both scale and momentum. Plantains & Bananas, Bell Pepper, and Cut Flowers have large EU markets but lower growth rates, positioning them as high-volume but mature, competitive categories. Tomatoes present the weakest EU picture — large market but the EU is actually a shrinking market for tomatoes. Spice exporters should particularly note that the EU is one of the best markets for Cinnamon and Ginger, which is significant for Sri Lanka given its spice export base.

questions.text: Between 2012 and 2016, how did the Middle East compare to other regions as a destination market for agricultural exports, and which products were best positioned there?
questions.source_paper: Opportunity analysis of agriculture products in Sri Lanka: Measuring markets and feasibility

paper_ground_truth.answer: The Middle East is smaller overall than the EU, USA, and East Asian markets in absolute import volume for selected agricultural products. However, it stands out for its rapid growth across most selected products between 2012–2016, making it one of the most dynamic regional markets by growth rate. The highest market opportunities in the Middle East are in Tomatoes, Plantains & Bananas, and Shrimp — combining meaningful market size with high growth. Spice exporters should note that Cardamom, Nutmeg & Mace, and Turmeric are specifically oriented toward the Middle East as their primary destination. The Middle East thus represents a high-growth, mid-scale opportunity particularly attractive for producers of perishable staples and spices.

questions.text: Between 2012 and 2016, which spices faced declining global demand and how should that have affected Sri Lanka's export priorities?
questions.source_paper: Opportunity analysis of agriculture products in Sri Lanka: Measuring markets and feasibility

paper_ground_truth.answer: Among spices analyzed, Cardamom and Nutmeg & Mace face negative overall growth in global markets, making them structurally declining categories globally. Clove is in a slightly better position but still shows slow growth on the world market alongside a low global volume — essentially a small, stagnant category. Sri Lanka's export data adds more nuance: Cardamom exports from Sri Lanka are actually growing (likely capturing market share from competitors even in a declining market), while Clove exports are not growing. By contrast, Cinnamon and Pepper — Sri Lanka's two largest spice exports — are both growing globally, with Cinnamon ranking among the fastest-growing products worldwide. This divergence suggests Sri Lanka should double down on Cinnamon and Pepper while carefully monitoring whether Cardamom's export growth can be sustained against a negative global demand backdrop.

questions.text: Between 2012 and 2016, which agricultural products offered the US market the best combination of large market size and high growth?
questions.source_paper: Opportunity analysis of agriculture products in Sri Lanka: Measuring markets and feasibility

paper_ground_truth.answer: The US market is notably dynamic — most selected products show high or very high growth rates there between 2012–2016, making it a broadly favorable destination by growth metrics. The products with the very largest US market sizes are Shrimp, Tomatoes, Bell Pepper, Cut Flowers, and Plantains & Bananas. Bell Pepper is particularly notable as most of its global market growth is actually being driven by the US. Shrimp combines a very large US market with considerable growth, making it arguably the strongest single opportunity. Tomatoes have a large US market but show only moderate growth there (and are shrinking in the EU). The US market's broad growth momentum across most product categories — unlike the more selective EU market — makes it an attractive and relatively lower-risk diversification destination for agricultural exporters.

questions.text: As of 2016, how did East Asia's import profile for agricultural products differ from the EU and US, and which products were uniquely suited to that market?
questions.source_paper: Opportunity analysis of agriculture products in Sri Lanka: Measuring markets and feasibility

paper_ground_truth.answer: East Asia (China, Japan, South Korea, Singapore, Hong Kong) represents 15.4% of world imports for selected agricultural products — significantly smaller than the EU (42%) and US (18.8%), but still a substantial market at $14.9 billion. Its product composition differs meaningfully from Western markets. The largest East Asian markets are concentrated in seafood — Shrimp and Cuttlefish/Octopus & Bivalves — rather than fruits, vegetables, or spices that dominate Western demand. Three products are primarily oriented toward East Asia rather than other markets: Cassava, Live Fish, and Sea Cucumber. By growth rate, Pineapple, Cinnamon, Vanilla, Plantains & Bananas, Live Plants, and Strawberries are growing fastest in East Asia. However, a key warning: the Pepper market is shrinking in East Asia, and Sea Cucumber — despite being a targeted development product — also has a shrinking East Asian market. This market therefore rewards seafood exporters and some tropical fruits while being a poor fit for spice exporters focused on Pepper.

questions.text: As of 2014, how diversified were Albania's exports, and how had that changed since the mid-1990s?
questions.source_paper: What is the Binding Constraint to Growth in Albania?

paper_ground_truth.answer: Albania's export base is remarkably undiversified and has barely changed since the mid-1990s. As of 2014, roughly 80% of Albania's exports were concentrated in just four low-value, low-complexity categories: mineral products and metals (46%), footwear (18%), and garments (16%), with crude oil being the single largest export. What makes this particularly striking is that this concentration has remained essentially frozen for about two decades — there has been very little diversification since the mid-1990s. Albania also ranks as the lowest in the Western Balkans region on both export share of GDP and the percentage of firms that export, making it an outlier even among its regional peers.
supporting_quotes: As of 2014, Albania's largest single export was crude oil. Mineral products and metals together accounted for 46% of Albania's exports, while footwear and garments made up 18% and 16% of exports, respectively. Together, these low value and low complexity goods make up 80% of Albania's exports.
There has been very little diversification of Albania's manufactured exports over the last two decades, as exports have remained heavily concentrated in these four categories since the mid-1990s.

Albania's exports are low as a share of GDP and only a small percentage of firms in Albania are exporters (Figure 5). On both of these measures, Albania is the lowest in the region based on the most recent data.

questions.text: As of 2014, why was it harder for Albania to diversify into more complex products compared to its neighbors in the Western Balkans?
questions.source_paper: What is the Binding Constraint to Growth in Albania?

paper_ground_truth.answer: Albania's position in the Product Space — the map of how products relate to each other through shared productive capabilities — puts it at a structural disadvantage for diversification. Albania's exports cluster in garments, footwear, scattered agricultural goods, wood and paper products, and minerals and metals. These product categories sit on the periphery of the Product Space and are not closely connected to higher-complexity products like chemicals, machinery, electrical goods, or transportation equipment, which occupy a denser, more interconnected core. Because moving into new products is easier when a country already exports something similar (i.e., the 'distance' is short), Albania's peripheral position means the 'jumps' required to reach more complex products are larger than those facing regional peers. When the distance to new products is calculated, Albania's path to diversification is measurably more difficult than that of other Western Balkan countries.
supporting_quotes: Albania's exported products are clustered in garments and footwear (green), and scattered across agricultural products (yellow and orange), a few wood and paper-based products (red), and minerals and metals (brown). These products do not tend to be co-exported with higher complexity products like chemicals, machinery, electrical, or transportation products, which are located in the left half of the Product Space.
When one calculates the 'distance' to new products, Albania's path to diversification is more difficult than that of other countries in the region (Figure 9).

data_points.value: large — no co-export linkages with chemicals, machinery, electrical, transport

questions.text: In 2016, how did Albania's tourism and shipping performance compare to other coastal countries in the region?
questions.source_paper: What is the Binding Constraint to Growth in Albania?

paper_ground_truth.answer: Albania presents a paradox when it comes to its coastal geography. On tourism (travel services), Albania outperforms some non-coastal regional peers — its service exports per capita exceed those of Bosnia and Herzegovina, Macedonia, and Serbia. However, when compared specifically to other Adriatic coastal countries — Greece, Montenegro, Croatia, and Slovenia — Albania lags significantly behind in travel services per capita, suggesting its coastline is heavily underutilized for tourism. The shipping picture is even starker: Albania scores just 3.4 out of 100 on the UNCTAD liner shipping connectivity index (2016), placing it on par with landlocked or small island economies like Latvia, Eritrea, and Tonga — an extraordinary outcome for a country with coastal access. Despite its geography, Albania is also comparatively weak in transport services more broadly.
supporting_quotes: Albania's comparative strength is in travel services, but it is comparatively weak in transport services (despite having a coastline) and information and communications technologies.
However, even in travel, when compared to the group of countries with the access to the Adriatic coastline (Greece, Montenegro, Croatia and Slovenia), Albania lags significantly behind.

On the United Nations Conference on Trade and Development (UNCTAD) index for liner shipping connectivity, Albania scores very poorly (a score of 3.4/100 in 2016), on par with Latvia, Eritrea and Tonga, as well as Montenegro.

data_points.metric: travel services per capita vs coastal peers
data_points.value: significantly lower than Greece, Montenegro, Croatia, Slovenia

questions.text: Between 2012 and 2014, what role did the Albanian diaspora and return migration play in shaping Albania's export capabilities?
questions.source_paper: What is the Binding Constraint to Growth in Albania?

paper_ground_truth.answer: Return migration has been a surprisingly important channel through which Albania has acquired productive knowhow and accessed new export markets. In agriculture, return migrants from Greece introduced commercial practices and technologies — including greenhouses — that pulled Albanian workers from subsistence farming into formal commercial agriculture, driving productivity gains. This knowhow transfer was linked to the near-doubling of fruit and vegetable exports between 2012 and 2014 (from $36M to $66M). In herbs and spices, return migrants from the United States enabled Albanian companies to discover and access U.S. and European markets — an effect also visible in firm surveys that found a strong correlation between U.S. export capability and returning Albanian-American diaspora members. Meanwhile, Italian FDI (not diaspora-driven) was responsible for establishing the fason textile and footwear sector in the 1990s. The pattern suggests that personal networks and embedded market knowledge, rather than just capital, are a key mechanism for knowhow transfer into Albania's export sector.
supporting_quotes: Businesses run by return migrants pulled Albanians from self-employment and subsistence agriculture into formal employment and commercial agriculture, oftentimes through the introduction of simple, but powerful innovations such as greenhouses.
Herbs and spices Return migration from the U.S. allowed companies to discover new markets

Interestingly, firm surveys conducted by CID revealed a strong link between firms that were able to export to the U.S. market and returned members of the Albanian diaspora who had spent time living in the United States.
Fruits and vegetable exports almost doubled between 2012 and 2014 (from $36 to $66 million, in constant USD), led by growth in tomatoes and apples, as well as nuts.

data_points.value: return migrants from Greece introduced greenhouses and commercial agriculture
return migrants from US enabled new market access

data_points.metric: US export capability link to diaspora

questions.text: As of 2014, how concentrated was Albania's garment and footwear sector in terms of its export relationships, and what were the implications?
questions.source_paper: What is the Binding Constraint to Growth in Albania?

paper_ground_truth.answer: Albania's garment and footwear exports — which together represent 34% of total goods exports as of 2014 — are deeply embedded in the Italian fason (outsourced manufacturing) model. The sector was originally established through Italian FDI in the 1990s and Albanian producers remain highly reliant on Italian companies as their primary clients. This buyer concentration means that Albanian firms are essentially operating as subcontractors rather than independent exporters with control over their own market relationships, pricing, or product development. The WEF Global Competitiveness Index data reinforces this picture, flagging Albania's lack of control of international distribution and weak value chain integration as key business sophistication deficiencies — consistent with a sector where Italian clients dictate terms and Albanian firms have limited autonomy in the export relationship.
supporting_quotes: for most garments and footwear (Figure 12), for which Albanian producers in the 'fason' sector remain highly reliant on Italian companies as clients.
Italian foreign direct investment (FDI) had already helped to establish the fason sector (textiles and footwear) in Albania.
Low business sophistication can be seen once again in the weakness of value chains, absence of developed industry clusters, and lack of control of international distribution, among other indicators.

data_points.metric: export share - garments and footwear combined

questions.text: As of 2014, how dependent was Albania's export profile on natural resource extraction, and was this a recent development?
questions.source_paper: What is the Binding Constraint to Growth in Albania?

paper_ground_truth.answer: Natural resource extraction forms a substantial and long-standing pillar of Albania's export profile. As of 2014, mineral products alone accounted for 35% of goods exports — broken down as 20% from crude oil and 15% from other minerals and electrical energy — making crude oil the single largest individual export. When combined with metals, the minerals-and-metals category reaches 46% of total exports. This heavy resource dependence coexists with extremely limited FDI into non-mining manufacturing; most incoming FDI has instead flowed into electricity generation, mining, and telecommunications rather than into productive manufacturing sectors. The pattern suggests that Albania's resource exports reflect its natural endowments more than accumulated productive knowhow, which partly explains why its ECI is lower than expected for its income level.
supporting_quotes: As of 2014, Albania's largest single export was crude oil. Mineral products and metals together accounted for 46% of Albania's exports.

However, very little FDI has flowed into non-mining manufacturing.

questions.text: As of 2018, which sector was considered the most feasible diversification opportunity for Albania, and why?
questions.source_paper: Diversification in the Industrial Sector of Albania: Identifying Strategic Areas

paper_ground_truth.answer: Plastics and Rubbers ranks first in diversification feasibility for Albania. What makes this notable is that it combines relatively high average product complexity with a strong feasibility score — meaning the products in this sector are more sophisticated than what Albania currently exports, yet they are still considered attainable given Albania's existing productive capabilities. This positions Plastics and Rubbers as a potentially high-reward diversification target compared to sectors where Albania's export base already sits.
supporting_quotes: The sector ranks first in feasibility, with a high average complexity of its products.
paper_ground_truth.paper_title: Diversification in the Industrial Sector of Albania: Identifying Strategic Areas

questions.text: As of 2018, how did the Agriculture and Foodstuffs sector compare to Plastics and Rubbers as a diversification opportunity for Albania?
questions.source_paper: Diversification in the Industrial Sector of Albania: Identifying Strategic Areas

paper_ground_truth.answer: Agriculture and Foodstuffs ranks second overall in diversification feasibility for Albania, trailing Plastics and Rubbers. The key trade-off is this: Agriculture and Foodstuffs offers lower average product complexity than Plastics and Rubbers, but it sits much closer to Albania's existing export base in terms of capability proximity. Albania already exports significant quantities of fruits, vegetables, and seafood, making the jump to new products in this sector less risky. However, the sector's higher-potential products — particularly prepared and processed foods — are ones Albania currently produces very little of, even though these rank highly for diversification potential and carry relatively low risk. So while Agriculture and Foodstuffs is more accessible, the specific sub-segment where the biggest gains lie (processed foods) still requires a capability shift.
supporting_quotes: The sector comes second in the ranking, with a lower average complexity but a higher proximity to current exports.
Albania already produces many products in this sector; for example, it already exports different types of fruits, vegetables, and seafood in significant quantities.
However, it does not produce many prepared or processed foods, which ranked highly for their potential to Albania and lower risk in diversification.

questions.text: As of 2016, Albania already exports agricultural products — so what's the actual gap in its food-related export portfolio, and how much potential exists for diversification into processed foods by 2018?
questions.source_paper: Diversification in the Industrial Sector of Albania: Identifying Strategic Areas

paper_ground_truth.answer: Albania's gap lies specifically in processed and prepared foods. While the country already exports meaningful volumes of raw agricultural products like fruits, vegetables, and seafood, it largely skips the value-added step of processing these into prepared food products. This is significant because prepared and processed foods rank highly in terms of diversification potential for Albania and also carry relatively low diversification risk — meaning they are both attainable given existing capabilities and valuable to pursue. The fact that Albania has the raw agricultural base but hasn't moved up the value chain into processed foods represents a concrete and under-exploited opportunity.
supporting_quotes: Albania already produces many products in this sector; for example, it already exports different types of fruits, vegetables, and seafood in significant quantities.
However, it does not produce many prepared or processed foods, which ranked highly for their potential to Albania and lower risk in diversification.

questions.text: As of 2014, Uganda has been diversifying its exports over time, but does that diversification actually represent a move toward more complex products?
questions.source_paper: How should Uganda grow?

paper_ground_truth.answer: Not really. While Uganda's exports have become more diverse over time, this diversification has been largely confined to other primary products — particularly mining and fish. The country remains heavily concentrated in peripheral product communities like tree crops, flowers, food processing, animal products, and fish and seafood, and has made very few inroads into larger, more complex, and more connected communities such as garments, construction materials, chemicals, and machinery. There has been a small but notable uptick in exports of light manufactured building materials like cement and steel products to neighboring countries, but this remains limited. In short, Uganda's diversification so far has been horizontal within the primary sector rather than a structural upgrade toward more complex products.
supporting_quotes: Although exports have become more diverse over time, this has been mostly due to diversification into other primary products such as mining and fish.
Uganda has a significant presence in many of the peripheral communities, particularly, tree crops and flowers, as well as food processing, animal products and fish and seafood.
Yet Uganda has made few inroads into the larger, more complex, and more connected communities such as garments, construction materials, chemicals, and machinery.
There has also been an increase in exports of light manufactured building materials including cement and steel products especially to the neighboring countries.

data_points.product: tree crops, flowers, food processing, animal products, fish

questions.text: As of 2014, why did Uganda's attempt to develop a cotton garments industry fail, and what does the product space framework tell us about that outcome?
questions.source_paper: How should Uganda grow?

paper_ground_truth.answer: Uganda's cotton garments industry failed because, despite the surface-level logic of adding value to domestic raw cotton, the industry turned out to be too distant from Uganda's existing position in the product space. The product space framework suggests that countries can most successfully diversify into products that are 'nearby' — meaning they require similar capabilities, knowledge, and inputs to what the country already produces. Cotton garments, while seemingly related to cotton production, actually require a very different set of capabilities (manufacturing, logistics, quality control, international market access) that Uganda had not developed. This case illustrates why proximity in the product space matters: jumping to a distant product, even one that appears logically connected to an existing export, often fails because the underlying capabilities don't transfer.
supporting_quotes: An illustrative case is the failure of the cotton garments industry in Uganda (see World Bank, 2007). While this industry provided the opportunity to add value to Uganda's raw cotton, it proved too distant from Uganda's position in the product space.

data_points.value: too distant in product space from existing capabilities

questions.text: As of 2014, how does Uganda's export complexity look different depending on which market it's selling to?
questions.source_paper: How should Uganda grow?

paper_ground_truth.answer: Uganda's export complexity varies dramatically by destination. Exports to Europe are dominated by agriculture — essentially raw or minimally processed primary commodities. Exports to Asia and the Middle East are slightly more diverse, including fish and animal products alongside agriculture. But the African market stands out as by far the most diversified destination, where Uganda's exports include machinery, construction materials and equipment, chemicals, and food processing in addition to agricultural goods. This pattern reveals a striking insight: Uganda is already exporting more complex products, but almost exclusively within the regional African market. This suggests that regional trade is serving as an incubator for Uganda's more sophisticated exports, and that growth in African import demand — projected to be among the fastest globally — could be a critical pathway for Uganda to upgrade its export complexity.
supporting_quotes: Exports to Europe are dominated by agriculture, whereas exports to Asia and the Middle East also include fish and animal products. But the local African market is by far the most diversified and also includes exports of machinery, construction materials and equipment, chemicals, and food processing (Figure 24).
Uganda would do well to continue diversifying its exports towards SSA and Asia, as these are expected to be the fastest growing import markets.

data_points.value: diversified: machinery, construction, chemicals, food processing
data_points.product: machinery, construction materials, chemicals, food processing

questions.text: In 2010, what did Nigeria's export performance compared to Mexico and Norway reveal about the relationship between oil wealth and export complexity?
questions.source_paper: How should Uganda grow?

paper_ground_truth.answer: Nigeria's export data offers a striking illustration of how oil dependence can coexist with — and perhaps contribute to — extreme underdevelopment of non-oil exports. In 2010, Nigeria's oil exports stood at $433 per capita. But its non-oil exports were only $15 per capita. Mexico had a similar level of oil exports per capita, yet its non-oil exports were 139 times larger at roughly $2,085 per capita. Norway exported about 33 times more oil per capita than Nigeria, yet its non-oil exports were 653 times larger — approximately $9,795 per capita. This comparison shows that oil wealth alone doesn't build economic complexity; what matters is whether a country develops the productive capabilities to export a wide range of non-oil products. Norway and Mexico both did; Nigeria largely has not.
supporting_quotes: In 2010, Nigeria's oil exports were only 433 dollars per capita. But its non-oil exports were barely 15 dollars per capita. By contrast, Mexico had a similar level of oil exports, but its non-oil exports per capita were 139 times larger than Nigeria's. In the case of Norway, its oil exports were 33 times larger than Nigeria's. In spite of this abundance of oil, its non-oil exports were 653 times those of Nigeria.

questions.text: According to the product space framework used in 2014 research, why are oil products considered a particularly poor foundation for economic diversification?
questions.source_paper: How should Uganda grow?

paper_ground_truth.answer: Oil products score the lowest on both complexity and connectedness among all major product communities in the product space. Connectedness measures how many pathways a product offers into other products and communities — a high score means that producing this product builds capabilities that transfer easily to many other goods. Oil, by contrast, is linked to only one other product and to only one other community (petrochemicals) in the product space map. This isolation means that even a very successful oil export sector does little to build the productive capabilities needed to branch out into other industries. Compare this to machinery or chemicals communities, which score high on both complexity and connectedness, meaning success there opens doors to many further diversification opportunities. The implication is that oil-dependent economies face a structural disadvantage in building economic complexity, which helps explain why countries like Nigeria have struggled to develop robust non-oil export sectors.
supporting_quotes: Conversely, another large community – oil – exhibits both the lowest complexity and connectedness score. This is also apparent in the map of the product space where oil products are linked to only one other product and to only one other community (petrochemicals).

In Figure 19 we see that the more complex communities are generally more connected. This means that as countries diversify into more complex products, they also increase their opportunities for further diversification. For example the machinery and the various chemicals communities are highly complex as well as highly connected.

data_points.value: lowest — linked to only 1 product and 1 community
