questions.text: As of 2014, what are the most viable near-term diversification targets for Uganda based on its position in the product space?
questions.source_paper: How should Uganda grow?

paper_ground_truth.answer: Based on Uganda's current position in the product space, the most viable near-term diversification opportunities fall into two tiers. The first — what might be called 'parsimonious' or low-distance moves — includes food processing products like margarine, confectionery, and jams, as well as agrochemicals. These are close to Uganda's existing capabilities in agriculture and require relatively modest capability jumps. The second tier, representing more strategic bets with higher complexity and opportunity value, includes construction and industrial materials such as plastics, metal products, and paper products. Food processing is identified as the closest community along Uganda's efficiency frontier above its current average complexity level, and Uganda already has some export presence there. Construction materials and equipment come next, offering sizable opportunity value without being prohibitively distant. Critically, both tiers align with markets where Uganda is already seeing export growth — particularly to neighboring African countries.
supporting_quotes: Above Uganda's current average level of complexity, the closest community along the efficiency frontier is food processing. Further Uganda already has some presence in this community.
Construction materials and equipment is the next community along the efficiency frontier. Importantly, this community offers a sizable opportunity value without being prohibitively distant.

Parsimonious transformation – Examining the top ranking products we find that they are mostly processed inputs or outputs of the agricultural industry, that is, food processing and agrochemicals (Figure 29).
Strategic bets – Examining the top ranking products we see that they represent mostly construction and industrial materials such as plastics, metal and paper products (Figure 30).

questions.text: How well does economic complexity predict income levels across countries as of 2014, and what does Uganda's position on that relationship tell us?
questions.source_paper: How should Uganda grow?

paper_ground_truth.answer: Economic complexity is a remarkably strong predictor of income: the ECI can explain approximately 75% of the variation in GDP per capita across countries, with high-complexity countries consistently achieving higher incomes. Uganda's position on this relationship is particularly instructive — it falls significantly below the prediction line, meaning it has a higher level of economic complexity than you would expect for a country at its income level. This is actually a favorable signal: research shows that countries whose complexity exceeds what their income would predict tend to subsequently grow faster, as their productive capabilities are ahead of their current income, creating upward pressure on economic performance. For Uganda, this misalignment between complexity and income represents latent growth potential rather than a problem.
supporting_quotes: It shows that economic complexity can explain about 75 percent of the variation in income; countries with high ECI tend to have higher per capita income.
Uganda appears significantly below the prediction line, indicating that it has a relatively high level of complexity for its level of income.

Figure 17 shows that countries whose economic complexity is greater than what we would expect, given their level of income, tend to grow faster than those that are 'too rich' for their current level of economic complexity.

data_points.metric: R-squared of ECI on GDP per capita

data_points.value: significantly below prediction line (higher complexity than income implies)

questions.text: As of 2014, Uganda's export diversity is low globally but average within Sub-Saharan Africa — what does that tell us about the region's overall export complexity challenge?
questions.source_paper: How should Uganda grow?

paper_ground_truth.answer: Uganda's export diversity profile reveals something important about Sub-Saharan Africa as a whole. Globally, Uganda's diversity is low — it exports a relatively narrow range of products. But when compared only to other Sub-Saharan African countries, it looks average. This means that low export diversity is the regional norm in SSA, not a Uganda-specific anomaly. Combined with the fact that Uganda's products are fairly ubiquitous — meaning they are produced by many countries and therefore tend to be less complex — this paints a picture of a region where most exporters are clustered in the same peripheral, low-complexity product space. The broader implication is that any SSA country seeking to stand out globally must break from regional norms by moving into more complex, less ubiquitous product categories.
supporting_quotes: Uganda places in the upper left of the distribution; its diversity is fairly low compared to all countries, but average when compared to other SSA countries (shown in orange).
And in line with our theory, the products that Uganda produces are fairly ubiquitous.
paper_ground_truth.paper_title: How should Uganda grow?

questions.text: As of 2014, South Sudan has emerged as an important trade partner for Uganda — what kinds of products is Uganda exporting there, and why does this matter for Uganda's complexity trajectory?
questions.source_paper: How should Uganda grow?

paper_ground_truth.answer: South Sudan's reconstruction needs have made it an important and fast-growing market for Uganda, driving demand for food and manufactured materials. This regional trade relationship is significant for Uganda's complexity trajectory because it is through African markets — including South Sudan — that Uganda is already exporting its most complex products. The African market is Uganda's most diversified export destination, encompassing not just agricultural goods but also machinery, construction materials, chemicals, and food processing products. South Sudan's ongoing reconstruction creates sustained demand for exactly the kinds of light manufactured goods — cement, steel, and building materials — that Uganda has started producing and exporting. This makes regional demand a real-world testing ground and scaling opportunity for Uganda to move up the product complexity ladder.
supporting_quotes: Recent export growth has been stimulated by demand in neighboring countries. South Sudan has emerged to be an important trading partner with Uganda demanding food and other manufactured material to help with reconstruction.
But the local African market is by far the most diversified and also includes exports of machinery, construction materials and equipment, chemicals, and food processing (Figure 24).
There has also been an increase in exports of light manufactured building materials including cement and steel products especially to the neighboring countries.

questions.text: As of 2018, which U.S. export sectors were most vulnerable to a stronger dollar, and which ones seemed immune?
questions.source_paper: The Exposure of U.S. Manufacturing Industries to Exchange Rates

paper_ground_truth.answer: U.S. export sectors vary dramatically in their sensitivity to dollar appreciation. Motor vehicles are the most exposed, with an elasticity of -1.86 — meaning a 10% dollar appreciation reduces motor vehicle export volumes by nearly 19%. Aluminum and iron & steel exports also have elasticities greater than 1 in absolute value, making them highly sensitive. Wood and pulp exports have an elasticity of -1.27, also above unity. Paper exports are moderately sensitive at -0.63. By contrast, pharmaceuticals, chemicals, and plastics exports show no statistically significant response to exchange rate movements at all. This divide likely reflects product sophistication: pharmaceuticals (with a product complexity ranking of 234) are differentiated, hard-to-substitute goods where buyers don't easily switch based on price, while commodities like aluminum and steel face much more price-driven competition.
supporting_quotes: Motor vehicle exports have an exchange rate elasticity of -1.86, indicating that a 10 percent appreciation of the dollar will reduce the volume of exports by almost 19 percent.
Aluminum and iron & steel exports also have elasticities greater than unity.
Forestry exports are sensitive to the exchange rate, with the elasticity for wood & pulp exports equal to -1.27
and the elasticity for paper exports equal to -0.63
On the other hand, there is no evidence that pharmaceuticals, chemicals, and plastics exports are affected by exchange rates.
Product Complexity Ranking [for Pharmaceuticals (30)]: 234
paper_ground_truth.paper_title: The Exposure of U.S. Manufacturing Industries to Exchange Rates

questions.text: Between 2008 and 2015, was the U.S. trade deficit with China mainly a China-specific problem, or was the broader manufacturing deficit with the rest of the world just as large?
questions.source_paper: The Exposure of U.S. Manufacturing Industries to Exchange Rates

paper_ground_truth.answer: Both deficits are substantial and roughly comparable in scale — the U.S. manufacturing trade deficit with China and with all other countries each exceed 2% of GDP. This is a non-obvious finding because public debate often focuses exclusively on the China deficit. The data suggest the U.S. has a systemic manufacturing competitiveness challenge that extends well beyond its bilateral relationship with China. One structural factor reinforcing the China-specific deficit is that U.S. income growth triggers especially large increases in Chinese imports: the income elasticity for imports from China is 4.13, compared to 2.48 for the world average and just 1.75 for other countries. So while the headline deficit sizes are similar, the China deficit is more dynamically tied to U.S. economic growth and thus may be harder to reduce through demand-side adjustments alone.
supporting_quotes: It shows that the U.S. deficits with both China and with other countries exceed 2 percent of GDP.
Comparing overlapping HS categories across the three tables, the average income elasticities are 2.48 for imports from the world, 4.13 for imports from China
and 1.75 for imports from other countries.

questions.text: As of 2017, which sectors did analysts identify as having the best combination of economic impact and investor interest for Sri Lanka's export diversification?
questions.source_paper: Learning to Engage New Investors for Economic Diversification: PDIA in action in Sri Lanka

paper_ground_truth.answer: Among the sectors evaluated for Sri Lanka's FDI promotion strategy, solar panels and airport hotels scored positively on both economic impact and market opportunity/investor interest. Solar panels fell under the broader 'Computer, electronic and optical products' category and rated well on both dimensions. Airport hotels were part of the tourism sector, which showed both significant impact potential and large market opportunity. Logistics, by contrast, showed an asymmetric profile — scoring low on economic impact for Sri Lanka but high on market opportunity and investor interest — making it a more selective case. This suggests that solar panels and tourism/hospitality represent the strongest dual-benefit sectors for Sri Lanka's export diversification push.
supporting_quotes: Solar panels featured as a specific sector in the general sector 'Computer, electronic and optical products', and scored positively in terms of both 'impact for the Sri Lankan economy' and 'market opportunity and investor interest'.
Airport hotels were part of the tourism sector, which has both potential for significant impact in Sri Lanka and a large market opportunity and investor interest.
Logistics was not seen as having a very large impact on the Sri Lankan economy but did score highly in terms of market opportunity and investor interest.

questions.text: As of 2017, why might solar panel manufacturing be a harder sell for Sri Lanka despite scoring well on both impact and investor interest?
questions.source_paper: Learning to Engage New Investors for Economic Diversification: PDIA in action in Sri Lanka

paper_ground_truth.answer: Even though solar panel manufacturing scores positively on both economic impact and investor interest for Sri Lanka, there are structural challenges that complicate its viability. First, solar manufacturing is capital-intensive, meaning it would not generate the large headcounts that Sri Lanka's Board of Investment (BOI) typically requires — specifically, the BOI mandates that investors employ 500 workers to qualify for incentives like access to land in dedicated zones. Second, the high cost of electricity in Sri Lanka was identified as a major production constraint for solar manufacturing, which is particularly ironic given that solar panels are energy products. These two factors — capital intensity limiting labor creation and high input energy costs — create a difficult environment for attracting this type of FDI despite its apparent strategic fit.
supporting_quotes: They were concerned that because solar manufacturing is a capital-intensive sector, it would not be successful in Sri Lanka. They worried that it would not be considered for BOI incentives (which include access to land in dedicated zones), given that the BOI requires that investors employ 500 workers.
The price of electricity was identified as a major constraint and something the team would need to take into consideration as it moved ahead.

questions.text: Between 1975 and 2000, how likely was Paraguay to move into new export products, and what did that tell us about its structural transformation?
questions.source_paper: Growth Diagnostic: Paraguay

paper_ground_truth.answer: Paraguay's probability of transitioning into new, previously un-exported products is remarkably low — even after accounting for its position in the product space. The unconditional probability of moving to a new product during the 1975–2000 period was only 1.3%. But beyond this already low baseline, statistical analysis shows that Paraguay-specific factors — things beyond just where the country sits in the product space — reduced that probability by more than half. In other words, Paraguay's poor structural transformation cannot be explained purely by the fact that it is in a sparse, disconnected part of the product space; there are additional country-level barriers that further suppress the ability to diversify into new products.
supporting_quotes: Interestingly, in the case of Paraguay we see a statistically significant and negative dummy. That is, between 1975 and 2000 there was a statistically significant pattern of slow structural transformation, controlling for open forest.

The unconditional probability of transitioning to an un-exported product in this period is only 1.3%, so this means that country-specific factors in Paraguay other than their orientation in the product space are reducing the probability of moving to new products by over half!

data_points.value: 1.3% unconditional, reduced by over half by country-specific factors

paper_ground_truth.paper_title: Growth Diagnostic: Paraguay

questions.text: Between 2000 and 2004, did trading within Mercosur help Paraguay export more sophisticated products, like it did for other Mercosur members?
questions.source_paper: Growth Diagnostic: Paraguay

paper_ground_truth.answer: Surprisingly, no — Paraguay is the odd one out in Mercosur in this regard. For all other Mercosur members, trade within the bloc tends to involve more sophisticated products compared to their global export mix. Paraguay is the only Mercosur country where the opposite is true: its exports within Mercosur are actually less sophisticated than its exports to the rest of the world. This is likely related to the fact that Argentina and Brazil — Paraguay's main Mercosur partners — are themselves net exporters of all or nearly all of Paraguay's major export products, meaning Mercosur offers Paraguay no real market advantage for the things it makes best.
supporting_quotes: This is a quite surprising finding that is unique to Paraguay within Mercosur. Unlike all of the other members of Mercosur, Paraguay's export sophistication within the block is actually lower than outside of it, as shown below.

Interestingly, the major Mercosur countries are not net importers of these products. Argentina is a net exporter of all 15 products, and Brazil is a net exporter of 13 of the 15, with Argentina's net exports of those two products larger than Brazil's net imports.

data_points.value: lower within Mercosur (unique among Mercosur members)
Argentina exports all 15 of Paraguay's major products; Brazil exports 13 of 15

questions.text: Between 1990 and 2005, how did Paraguay's terms of trade evolve compared to its neighbors Brazil and Argentina?
questions.source_paper: Growth Diagnostic: Paraguay

paper_ground_truth.answer: Paraguay's terms of trade have been deteriorating relative to both Brazil and Argentina since 1990, creating a compounding disadvantage. From 1990 to 2000, international prices for Paraguay's exports declined steadily. After 2000, export prices did recover somewhat, but import prices climbed even faster — driven by rising global energy prices (Paraguay is a net energy importer) and the appreciation of the Brazilian Real, which affects many of Paraguay's imports since Brazil is a key source country. The net result is that Paraguay has faced a persistent and worsening terms-of-trade squeeze relative to its neighbors, which has undermined real income growth even in periods of nominally rising commodity prices.
supporting_quotes: The following figure shows that since 1990, Paraguay's terms of trade have been collapsing relative to Brazil and Argentina.

From 1990 until 2000, international prices for Paraguay's exports declined steadily. This trend reversed in 2000, but by that time the prices of Paraguay's imports began to climb as well, and much more strongly than the prices of its exports. The increase in the price of imports was associated not only to the rise in international energy prices, of which Paraguay is a net importer, but also because of the appreciation of the Brazilian Real that affects many of Paraguay's imports.

data_points.value: declining relative to Brazil and Argentina since 1990
export prices declined 1990-2000; after 2000 import prices rose faster than export prices

questions.text: By 2006, Paraguay had massively expanded soybean cultivation — was that land use well-matched to the country's agricultural potential?
questions.source_paper: Growth Diagnostic: Paraguay

paper_ground_truth.answer: The expansion is dramatically misaligned with Paraguay's natural land suitability. By 2006, Paraguay had planted over 2.2 million hectares of soybeans, but only 542,000 hectares of its land is classified as suitable or very suitable for the crop — meaning Paraguay has planted soybeans on roughly four times more land than is actually well-suited for it, pushing into only moderately suitable territory. Meanwhile, Paraguay has left a massive opportunity largely untapped: the country has over 4.6 million hectares classified as very suitable or suitable for sugar, yet has planted only about 75,000 hectares — just 1.6% of its suitable sugar land. This contrast is especially striking given that the relative price of sugar to soybeans doubled between 2004 and 2006, partly due to demand for sugar-based bio-ethanol.
supporting_quotes: The country only has 542000 hectares of land which is classified either as suitable or very suitable for soybeans, but it has already planted 2.2 million hectares, 4 times more than this amount. To do so, it has had to go to lands which are deemed only moderately suitable.
By contrast, the country has over 4.6 million hectares that are deemed either very suitable or suitable for sugar but has planted barely 75.000 or 1.6 percent of the total.
But this situation has now reversed with the relative price of sugar doubling between 2004 and 2006 in part due to the use of sugar in bio-ethanol.

questions.text: Between 1969 and 2003, how has Paraguay's export growth historically been driven, and what does the pattern reveal about the sustainability of its growth model?
questions.source_paper: Growth Diagnostic: Paraguay

paper_ground_truth.answer: Paraguay's export growth has been episodic and almost entirely dependent on latching onto specific commodities enjoying favorable external conditions, rather than driven by broad-based structural transformation. The first major boom was from 1969 to 1977, when exports per capita more than doubled, fueled by agricultural exports — particularly soybeans, cotton, coffee, and wood, with coffee and wood growing ten-fold. The second boom ran from 1986 to 1989, driven almost exclusively by soybeans and cotton benefiting from surging international prices. When those prices reversed and cotton faced production problems, the boom ended and output per capita fell back to late-1970s levels. Since 2003, soybeans and, more recently, meat have led growth. This pattern — growth only when a lucky commodity is booming — is consistent with Paraguay's very low export sophistication and failure to diversify, and explains why long-run growth has been so poor.
supporting_quotes: Between 1969 and 1977, exports per capita more than doubled, as shown in the figure below. This export boom was fueled by a basket of agricultural exports, such as soybeans and cotton, along with coffee and wood exports, which increased 10-fold.

There was another export boom from 1986 to 1989, almost entirely due to soybean and cotton exports, which enjoyed surging international prices and a significant positive supply response. A reversal in international prices, as well as production problems in cotton led to the end of the export and GDP with output per capita roughly on par with levels of the late 1970s before the Itaipu output spike.
Paraguay has exhibited low long-run growth. In the few instances when growth has been high it has been because the country was able to latch on to some dynamic export products.

data_points.value: low EXPY associated with low long-run growth

paper_ground_truth.paper_title: Growth Diagnostic: Paraguay

questions.text: By 2006, why was the soybean sector's dominance in Paraguay's export basket considered a structural problem from a complexity standpoint?
questions.source_paper: Growth Diagnostic: Paraguay

paper_ground_truth.answer: The dominance of soybeans is problematic for several interconnected reasons from a complexity perspective. First, soybeans are an extremely land- and capital-intensive product with very low labor intensity — in the US a hectare requires only 6 hours of labor per year, and in Brazil about 15 hours, which is less than one-fifteenth of the labor required for a crop like sugar. This means soybean expansion generates limited employment and backward linkages. Second, Paraguay has already overshot its most suitable land — planting 2.2 million hectares against only 542,000 hectares of truly suitable land — limiting future yield gains. Third, soybeans sit in a peripheral, isolated part of the product space, meaning the capabilities built up around soybean production do not easily transfer to more complex or higher-value products. Finally, Paraguay's export sophistication (EXPY) has remained very low precisely because it has deepened into soybeans rather than diversifying, and countries with low EXPY tend to grow more slowly over the long run.
supporting_quotes: In the US, a hectare of soybeans requires barely 6 hours of work per year while the equivalent number for Brazil is about 15 hours per year. This is at least less than 1/15th of the equivalent labor input for a crop such as sugar, assuming mechanized harvesting.
The country only has 542000 hectares of land which is classified either as suitable or very suitable for soybeans, but it has already planted 2.2 million hectares, 4 times more than this amount.
These figures show that Paraguay's productive capabilities are concentrated in very sparse parts of the product space, and they have undergone very little changes over time.
Given the finding that countries with less-sophisticated export baskets grow slower, Paraguay's low long-term export growth should not be very surprising.

questions.text: As of 2010, how concentrated was Rwanda's export basket, and how did that compare to what you'd expect from a developing economy?
questions.source_paper: Moving to the Adjacent Possible: Discovering Paths for Export Diversification in Rwanda

paper_ground_truth.answer: Rwanda's export basket is extraordinarily concentrated. Just three commodities — coffee, tea, and tin — have historically accounted for more than 80% of the country's total exports, and these same products continue to drive the bulk of export growth today. This extreme concentration is reflected in Rwanda having comparative advantage (RCA > 1) in fewer than 40 products as of 2010, and an average Product Complexity Index of -0.95, meaning the products Rwanda exports are roughly one standard deviation below the world average in complexity. With exports per capita below $50 — one of the lowest levels globally — Rwanda's trade profile is characteristic of a heavily commodity-dependent economy with very limited diversification.
supporting_quotes: Three basic commodities – coffee, tea, and tin – made up more than 80 percent of the country's exports through its history and still drive the bulk of export growth today.
at less than US$50 per capita, Rwanda still has one of the lowest levels of exports in the world

questions.text: In 2010, did Rwanda export more complex products to its regional neighbors than to Europe and the US?
questions.source_paper: Moving to the Adjacent Possible: Discovering Paths for Export Diversification in Rwanda

paper_ground_truth.answer: Yes, and it's a counterintuitive finding. Despite Rwanda's overall export basket being low-complexity, the products it sells to regional East African neighbors are actually more complex and diverse than what it exports to its major global partners. Europe, Asia, and the United States import basic commodities from Rwanda — primarily coffee, tea, and minerals. By contrast, regional neighbors import a greater variety of processed and semi-manufactured goods, including fertilizers, energy products, fabrics, and wood products. This pattern suggests that regional trade acts as an important but underutilized channel for moving Rwanda up the value chain, even though the East African market accounts for less than 10% of Rwanda's total exports.
supporting_quotes: Indeed, current export patterns already show a difference in the complexity of products that Rwanda exports to the regional market versus what it exports to the world. While Europe, Asia, and the United States import basic commodities from Rwanda, its regional neighbors import a greater variety of processed and semi-manufactured goods, including fertilizers, energy, fabrics, and wood products
Meanwhile, exports to the nearby markets of East Africa, which could incur lower transportation costs, account for only 10 percent of Rwanda's total exports.

questions.text: In 2010, what did the structure of Rwanda's top export sectors look like in terms of value, and what dominated?
questions.source_paper: Moving to the Adjacent Possible: Discovering Paths for Export Diversification in Rwanda

paper_ground_truth.answer: As of 2010, Rwanda's exports are dominated by two broad sectors that together account for over 70% of total export value. Mineral products are the single largest sector at 35.5% of total exports, followed closely by vegetable products at 34.9% — the latter capturing Rwanda's coffee and tea exports. This means Rwanda's entire export economy is essentially built on raw materials from the ground and unprocessed agricultural products. Machinery and electrical products, by contrast, represent just 4.3% of exports despite Rwanda having RCA in 4 such products, illustrating the very limited industrial base in the country's export portfolio.

questions.text: How severe was the collapse in Rwanda's trade performance between 1979 and 1990, and what does it reveal about the vulnerability of commodity-dependent exporters?
questions.source_paper: Moving to the Adjacent Possible: Discovering Paths for Export Diversification in Rwanda

paper_ground_truth.answer: Rwanda's trade collapse in the 1980s was dramatic and illustrates the fundamental vulnerability of economies dependent on a handful of commodities. Exports as a share of GDP fell from a high of 21% in 1979 to just 6% in 1990 — a drop of 15 percentage points over a single decade. This decline was almost certainly driven by external commodity price shocks, since Rwanda's export revenue was concentrated in coffee, tea, and tin, leaving the economy with virtually no buffer when prices for these goods fell on world markets. The fact that Rwanda has less than $50 per capita in exports even decades later suggests the economy never fully recovered its export capacity from that period.
supporting_quotes: As a result, exports of goods and services fell precariously from a high of 21 percent of GDP in 1979 to a low of 6 percent of GDP in 1990

at less than US$50 per capita, Rwanda still has one of the lowest levels of exports in the world

questions.text: In 2010, where did Rwanda actually send its main exports, and was there a disconnect between its geography and its trading partners?
questions.source_paper: Moving to the Adjacent Possible: Discovering Paths for Export Diversification in Rwanda

paper_ground_truth.answer: There is a striking geographic disconnect in Rwanda's trade relationships. Despite being landlocked in East Africa, Rwanda's top export destinations are largely distant Western markets. Belgium receives the largest single share of Rwanda's exports at 20.7%. Coffee goes primarily to Switzerland, Germany, and the US. Tea travels through Kenya to the UK and Pakistan. Metal exports are mostly destined for China. Meanwhile, the nearby East African markets — which would incur lower transportation costs — account for less than 10% of Rwanda's total exports. This pattern is unusual and points to a heavy reliance on commodity-driven trade relationships with wealthy distant importers rather than trade integration with the immediate regional economy.

questions.text: As of 2010, how large was the untapped regional trade opportunity in East Africa for chemicals and medicines, and how did it compare to other product categories?
questions.source_paper: Moving to the Adjacent Possible: Discovering Paths for Export Diversification in Rwanda

paper_ground_truth.answer: The regional trade gap in chemicals represents the single largest opportunity among the identified frontier product categories. East African neighbors import $1.2 billion worth of chemical products, agrochemicals, and medicaments annually, while the region exports only $86 million of these goods — leaving a gap of over $1.1 billion. This dwarfs the gaps in other categories: machinery and electrical products show $888 million in regional imports versus just $38 million in regional exports, and construction materials show roughly $700 million in regional imports versus about $100 million in regional exports. Taken together, these three categories alone represent over $2.7 billion in regional import demand that is largely being met from outside the region, creating a substantial opportunity for any East African producer — including Rwanda — that can competitively supply these goods.
supporting_quotes: The regional imports of the 14 frontier products on this category amount to US$ 1.2 billion wile exports from the region are only US$ 86 million, representing a large opportunity for any regional producer who can competitively supply these products.
The 17 frontier products in this category account for US$888 million of regional imports and only US$38 million of exports from the region.
The region imports close to $700 million of these goods, and exports roughly US$ 100 million.

questions.text: In 2010, why did Rwanda's export portfolio consist almost entirely of non-perishable goods, and what does that reveal about its trade constraints?
questions.source_paper: Moving to the Adjacent Possible: Discovering Paths for Export Diversification in Rwanda

paper_ground_truth.answer: Rwanda's export basket is almost exclusively composed of non-perishable edibles — products that can tolerate slow or unreliable access to markets. This pattern is a direct reflection of the country's geographic and logistical constraints as a landlocked nation. Products like coffee, tea, and minerals can sit in transit for extended periods without spoiling, whereas fresh produce, dairy, flowers, or other perishables require fast market access. The dominance of non-perishables in Rwanda's exports is therefore not just a reflection of what the country produces, but of what it can actually get to market competitively given its transportation situation. This constraint also helps explain why Rwanda sends less than 10% of exports to nearby East African neighbors despite lower transport costs — even short regional journeys may be too slow for perishable goods.
supporting_quotes: Consistent with our claim that transportation infrastructure is one of the key constraints, Rwanda's current export portfolio features almost exclusively non-perishable edibles, which do not rely on fast access to markets.
Meanwhile, exports to the nearby markets of East Africa, which could incur lower transportation costs, account for only 10 percent of Rwanda's total exports.

questions.text: How fast did the East African regional import market grow during the 2000s, and what did that mean for Rwanda's export potential as of 2010?
questions.source_paper: Moving to the Adjacent Possible: Discovering Paths for Export Diversification in Rwanda

paper_ground_truth.answer: The East African regional market has been one of the fastest-growing import markets in the world during the 2000s. The five neighbors most relevant to Rwanda — Burundi, DRC, Kenya, Tanzania, and Uganda — collectively represent over $85 billion in GDP and approximately $20 billion in annual imports as of 2009, with a combined population exceeding 120 million. Crucially, this market more than doubled its import volumes during the 2000s. This rapid growth is particularly significant for Rwanda given that the region still imports far more from outside the region than from within it — as evidenced by the massive import gaps in chemicals ($1.2 billion), machinery ($888 million), and construction materials ($700 million) that regional producers are not supplying. Rwanda currently captures less than 10% of its exports from this market, suggesting enormous untapped potential in a rapidly growing nearby market.

questions.text: In 2010, Rwanda had comparative advantage in leather and hide products, but how much was it actually earning from those exports, and what did that signal about its animal products sector compared to neighbors?
questions.source_paper: Moving to the Adjacent Possible: Discovering Paths for Export Diversification in Rwanda

paper_ground_truth.answer: Despite having revealed comparative advantage in leather and hide products, Rwanda's actual export earnings from these goods amounted to less than $5 million in 2010 — a negligible figure. The contrast with regional neighbors is stark: Kenya exports more than $100 million in animal products annually, and Uganda more than $200 million. This gap highlights that having RCA in a product category does not automatically translate into significant export revenues — scale, supply chain depth, and market access all matter. For Rwanda, the animal products and leather sector represents an area where RCA exists in principle but the productive base is not yet developed enough to generate meaningful export volumes, especially relative to regional peers who have built much larger industries in adjacent categories.
supporting_quotes: Although the cumulative export earnings of these products amounted to less than US$5 million in 2010

questions.text: As of 2010, how did Rwanda's current textile export base compare to what the product space analysis suggested it could realistically target?
questions.source_paper: Moving to the Adjacent Possible: Discovering Paths for Export Diversification in Rwanda

paper_ground_truth.answer: The gap between Rwanda's current textile exports and its identified potential is enormous. As of 2010, Rwanda exports just $100,000 in textiles and footwear — a negligible amount — and has RCA in only 6 textile products, compared to 27 for Kenya and 28 for Tanzania. Yet the product space analysis identifies 12 frontier textile and garment products as feasible targets for Rwanda's global export diversification, representing more than $100 billion in world trade. The logic is that some of these products are 'adjacent' to capabilities Rwanda already has, making them achievable stepping stones rather than distant leaps. The contrast between Rwanda's current $100k baseline and a $100+ billion global market opportunity illustrates both the scale of the challenge and the potential upside if Rwanda can build the necessary productive capabilities.

questions.text: Between 1998 and 2014, what happened to Panama's goods versus services exports — did both grow?
questions.source_paper: Shifting Gears: A Growth Diagnostic of Panama

paper_ground_truth.answer: The two have moved in starkly opposite directions. Services exports per capita grew 3.5 times between 1998 and 2014, accelerating especially after 2005. Goods exports, by contrast, had a brief period of modest growth — up 25% between 2000 and 2008 — but then fell sharply, declining 38% from 2008 to 2014. By 2014, goods exports per capita were actually 23% below their year-2000 level. So over the long run, Panama's goods export base has shrunk while services have surged, widening an already large gap between the two.
supporting_quotes: Service exports per capita have steadily increased in time, while exports of goods remained stagnant. Between 2000-2008, goods exports per capita increased 25%, but during the next six years decreased 38%; ending 23% below their initial value. To the contrary, exports of services per capita have expanded continuously, in particular from 2005 onwards, growing by a factor of 3.5 times.

data_points.metric: services exports per capita growth factor
goods exports per capita net change from 2000

questions.text: Between 1998 and 2014, how did the composition of Panama's service exports shift?
questions.source_paper: Shifting Gears: A Growth Diagnostic of Panama

paper_ground_truth.answer: Panama's service export mix has changed significantly since 1998. Canal tolls and port services — once the dominant category at nearly 30% of service exports — have shrunk to about 15% by 2014, not because they declined in absolute terms, but because other categories grew much faster. Air passenger transport more than doubled its share, from 5.8% to 15.7%, reflecting Panama's emergence as a regional aviation hub. Panama also outperformed OECD economies on transport services (exporting 2.4 times more per capita) and travel services (40% above the OECD average) by 2014. More recently, business and entrepreneurial services became a significant new contributor, generating a net surplus of US$1.3 billion in 2015 and the first three quarters of 2016.

By 2014 Panama had also outperformed OECD economies on Travel services per capita by 40%.
In 2015 and the first three quarters of 2016, Business and Entrepreneurial services became relevant, registering a net surplus of US$1.3 billion on each.

questions.text: Panama showed a promising diversification trend between 2003 and 2012 — what happened to it by 2014?
questions.source_paper: Shifting Gears: A Growth Diagnostic of Panama

paper_ground_truth.answer: Between 2003 and 2012, Panama's export basket was moving in a favorable direction: it was diversifying into products with lower average ubiquity, meaning goods that fewer other countries could produce. This is a positive signal because it suggests Panama was accumulating relatively rare capabilities. However, after 2012 both trends reversed sharply. By 2014, Panama's export diversification had fallen back to its 1990 level, and average ubiquity had reverted to 2003 levels — effectively erasing a decade of progress. Even with this reversal, Panama still sits around the middle of Latin American countries on diversification, but remains well below most high-income OECD countries.
supporting_quotes: The Panamanian export basket did not only diversify from 2003 to 2012, but it did so into products that fewer other countries were able to make (i.e. with a decreasing average ubiquity). Based on our previous reflection, this was good news for the economy in the long run.
Unfortunately, since 2012 both trends reversed, and by 2014 Panama's diversification and average ubiquity were back at 1990 and 2003 levels, respectively.

In spite of the reverse in trend, in 2014 Panama had a mid-level of export diversification relative to other countries in Latin America. However, it was still lower than most of high income OECD countries.

questions.text: As of 2014, how did Panama's Economic Complexity Index compare to its neighbors, and what did its Complexity Outlook Index suggest about future growth potential?
questions.source_paper: Shifting Gears: A Growth Diagnostic of Panama

paper_ground_truth.answer: Panama ranks among the highest in Latin America on the Economic Complexity Index (ECI), which reflects the sophistication of its current export basket relative to regional peers. Its income level in 2014 was roughly in line with what its ECI would predict. However, Panama's Complexity Outlook Index (COI) — which measures how well-positioned a country is to diversify into more complex products in the near future — is notably low. This combination is significant: Panama currently exports relatively sophisticated products compared to regional peers, but the structure of its product space offers limited nearby opportunities to jump into new, more complex sectors without first accumulating substantially new capabilities.
supporting_quotes: relative to the region, Panama is located among the Latin American countries with the highest ECI, although its COI is low.

questions.text: As of 2014–2015, how did the complexity levels of Panama's different economic sectors compare, and which sectors represented the biggest structural upgrade?
questions.source_paper: Shifting Gears: A Growth Diagnostic of Panama

paper_ground_truth.answer: Panama's sectors span an enormous range of complexity. At the bottom are agriculture and fishing with an Industry Complexity Index (ICI) of -2.1, and mining at -1.3 — both requiring few and widely-available capabilities. Construction sits at 0.8. The biggest structural upgrades are in services: hotels and restaurants score 1.2, retail and wholesale 1.8, logistics/transport/communications 2.5, and information services top the scale at 4.2. To put this in perspective, moving economic activity from agriculture (ICI -2.1) to the information sector (ICI 4.2) represents a gap of more than 6 index points in complexity. This matters because Panama's agriculture sector still employs 16% of the workforce despite contributing only 3.1% of GDP in 2015, while industry and services are roughly 7 times more productive per worker than agriculture.
supporting_quotes: Moving the provinces of Panama out of low-skill sectors that require a relatively low and ubiquitous number of capabilities such as agriculture and fishing (ECI -2.1), mining (-1.3) and construction (0.8); while stirring the wheel towards more complex sector that demand the combination of numerous capabilities such as Hotels and Restaurants (1.2), Retail and Wholesale (1.8), Logistic, transport and communications (2.5), or information (4.2) will demand a strong skill upgrade.
Agriculture, forestry and fishing also lost relative weight within GDP, from 6.2% to 3.1%.

Using WDI data for Agriculture, Industry and Services, we computed an indicator of productivity by dividing the share of GDP by sector into its share in employment. Figure 7 portrays an economy with productivity in industry and services about seven times higher than agriculture.

paper_ground_truth.answer: CARICOM countries export surprisingly unsophisticated goods relative to their income levels. Countries like Belize, Dominica, St. Vincent and the Grenadines, Grenada, and Guyana have among the poorest export baskets for countries at their level of development. This means their EXPY — a measure of export sophistication that reflects the income levels of countries that export similar products — is well below what you'd expect given their GDP per capita. This is a notable underperformance compared to peer countries at similar development stages.

questions.original_text: How does CARICOM countries' export sophistication compare to other countries at similar income levels?

questions.text: Is CARICOM's low export sophistication in 2005 just a result of being tourism-heavy economies? How did Cyprus and Malta compare in 2005, and what was their product space position around 2000?
questions.source_paper: Policies for Achieving Structural Transformation in the Caribbean

paper_ground_truth.answer: Tourism specialization alone doesn't explain CARICOM's low export sophistication. Cyprus and Malta are useful comparators here — both have comparable tourism intensity to several CARICOM economies (measured by visitor arrivals per 100,000 population), yet they maintain significantly higher merchandise export sophistication than their Caribbean counterparts. Malta has moved into electronics manufacturing and machinery, while Cyprus exports machinery, metal products, and chemicals — all products approaching or within the industrial core of the product space. So CARICOM countries appear to be underperforming on merchandise export complexity even after accounting for their tourism orientation.
supporting_quotes: What is interesting to note is that, although those two countries have comparable tourism activity (among those CARICOM countries for which we have arrivals data), they also have higher export sophistication in their merchandise export baskets. So at least taking these two comparators into account, the CARICOM countries are not suffering a lagging EXPY simply because they are concentrated in tourism.

But in addition to those sectors, both countries have made greater inroads into the industrial core, with Malta moving into electronics manufacturing and machinery, and Cyprus becoming a significant exporter of machinery and other metal products, as well as some chemical products.
paper_ground_truth.paper_title: Policies for Achieving Structural Transformation in the Caribbean

questions.text: In 2005, what products were boosting Jamaica's export sophistication score, and did that mean Jamaica's economy was truly more complex than its neighbors?
questions.source_paper: Policies for Achieving Structural Transformation in the Caribbean

paper_ground_truth.answer: Jamaica's EXPY is being pulled upward by two specific products: beer (6% of exports, PRODY of $10,200) and chemicals (3.7% of exports, PRODY of $14,700). These are relatively high-sophistication products that inflate the overall sophistication score. However, this doesn't necessarily mean Jamaica has a broadly complex or well-connected export base. In fact, Jamaica is an outlier in terms of having extremely few nearby opportunities for structural transformation in the product space — it has already fully occupied the peripheral agriculture and garment activities, leaving it with almost nowhere nearby to expand. So Jamaica's elevated EXPY is driven by a narrow set of products rather than deep economic complexity.
supporting_quotes: One exception seems to be Jamaica, whose EXPY is being pulled up by exports of beer (6% of exports with a PRODY of $10,200) and chemicals (3.7% of exports with a PRODY of $14,700).

Jamaica, on the other hand, has completely occupied the peripheral agriculture and garment activities. It has no other activities that are nearby because it has already capitalized on all those products that require similar capabilities, and it is not clear where it can go next.

Even countries that are not outliers in terms of having highly unsophisticated current exports, most importantly Jamaica, are outliers in terms of having few nearby opportunities for structural transformation.
paper_ground_truth.paper_title: Policies for Achieving Structural Transformation in the Caribbean

questions.original_text: What products are boosting Jamaica's export sophistication score, and does that mean Jamaica's economy is truly more complex than its neighbors?

questions.text: Around 2000–2005, which CARICOM countries had the most and least promising positions in the product space for future economic diversification?
questions.source_paper: Policies for Achieving Structural Transformation in the Caribbean

paper_ground_truth.answer: Among CARICOM countries, St. Kitts and Nevis stands out as having the most promising product space position, with relatively well-connected exports in food and beverages, machinery, and electronics as of 2000 — sectors that are closer to the industrial core and offer more pathways to diversification. The Bahamas also has some exports in beverages and chemicals that are approaching the industrial core. On the other end, Belize is in the worst position — with extremely low EXPY and exports confined to peripheral agriculture, aquiculture, and forestry with very few nearby alternatives. Jamaica, despite a higher EXPY, is also severely constrained because it has already fully occupied all its peripheral agriculture and garment activities with no adjacent unoccupied products. Guyana's exports are highly peripheral in raw materials and timber, though it has some textile activities with nearby unoccupied textile products offering limited options.
supporting_quotes: Even more so is the case with St. Kitts and Nevis, which (at least in 2000) had somewhat well-connected exports in areas such as food and beverages, machinery, and electronics.

Given the Bahamas' hyper-specialization in tourism and finance, it is difficult to draw many conclusions from their data, but that country does have some export activity in beverages as well as chemicals that are approaching the industrial core.

Belize in particular, with an extremely low EXPY, is squarely in the strategic bets quadrant of Figure 11 with no pathway to structural transformation: new areas of the product space have to be reached.

Jamaica, on the other hand, has completely occupied the peripheral agriculture and garment activities. It has no other activities that are nearby because it has already capitalized on all those products that require similar capabilities, and it is not clear where it can go next.

Guyana's products are also highly peripheral, with a concentration in raw materials and timber, but also some textile activities, and there are other textile activities not yet occupied by Guyana but nearby existing products.
paper_ground_truth.paper_title: Policies for Achieving Structural Transformation in the Caribbean

questions.text: Around 2000, how did Trinidad and Tobago's product space position compare to Singapore's, given both were major hydrocarbon exporters?
questions.source_paper: Policies for Achieving Structural Transformation in the Caribbean

paper_ground_truth.answer: Both Trinidad and Tobago and Singapore have significant hydrocarbon-related exports, but their product space positions are dramatically different. Trinidad and Tobago's exports are dominated by hydrocarbons, which are highly profitable but also highly peripheral — meaning the capabilities, institutions, and infrastructure they require have very few alternative uses in other industries. This limits diversification pathways. Singapore, by contrast, has moved well beyond peripheral hydrocarbon sectors to occupy the electronics cluster, industrial chemicals, and machinery that are well-connected to the industrial core of the product space. This means Singapore's existing capabilities can be leveraged to enter many new adjacent industries, enabling continued structural transformation. Trinidad and Tobago's hydrocarbon wealth essentially creates a dead end in the product space, while Singapore's diversification into core manufacturing sectors creates a virtuous cycle of capability-building.
supporting_quotes: Although Trinidad and Tobago has some other exports like food goods, these are swamped by the country's significant exports of hydrocarbons, which are highly profitable but also highly peripheral, meaning that the institutions, skills, and infrastructure they use do not have many alternative uses.

Singapore has moved beyond the peripheral natural resource and hydrocarbon sectors to also occupy the electronics cluster, industrial chemicals, and other machinery that is well connected to the industrial core of the product space. That country can then be expected to enjoy continued accelerated structural transformation because many alternative activities can use the inputs of its existing product set.

questions.text: As of 2005, what would happen to CARICOM's export sophistication and structural transformation potential if the region were treated as a single integrated economy?
questions.source_paper: Policies for Achieving Structural Transformation in the Caribbean

paper_ground_truth.answer: The results are striking. If CARICOM were treated as a perfectly integrated single country, its export sophistication (EXPY) would actually be considered high relative to its level of development — a complete reversal from the picture at the individual country level, where most members are underperformers. Moreover, this integrated CARICOM's opportunities for structural transformation (measured by open forest) would be significantly better than for any individual member country — not merely an average of the members, but actually higher than the best individual member. Interestingly, if GDP per capita were weighted by population (giving Jamaica a larger weight), CARICOM's income level would appear even lower on the scale, making the export sophistication look even more impressive relative to development level. The implication is that deeper regional economic integration could be a meaningful lever for improving the Caribbean's collective trade competitiveness and diversification prospects.
supporting_quotes: if CARICOM were a single, fully integrated country, its export sophistication would be considered high. And note that here we assigned the simple median GDP per capita across all CARICOM countries. If it were population-weighted, then Jamaica would be given a much larger weight, CARICOM would be even further to the left on the x-axis, and therefore even higher given its level of development.

Although future opportunities for structural transformation facing the Caribbean countries as an integrated block are more or less average for the level of development, they are significantly better than when considering each country individually.

It is important to note that the result is not merely an averaging of the individual members' level of sophistication and connectedness. For most sophistication and opportunities for future structural transformation, the levels for the block as a whole are higher than for any individual member country.

data_points.metric: EXPY and open forest (integrated vs. individual)

questions.text: Between 2000 and 2005, how did CARICOM countries' structural transformation prospects compare to Latin American peers like Costa Rica and Guatemala in terms of product space position?
questions.source_paper: Policies for Achieving Structural Transformation in the Caribbean

paper_ground_truth.answer: CARICOM countries are significantly disadvantaged compared to Latin American peers in terms of product space connectivity. Costa Rica has established a presence in the electronics cluster — one of the most densely connected parts of the product space — in addition to diversified agriculture. Guatemala has significant agricultural diversification including processing activities, which creates more adjacencies for future expansion. By contrast, virtually all CARICOM countries are located in highly peripheral parts of the product space with few nearby activities. Their open forest values are extremely low, meaning there are few unoccupied products close enough to their current capabilities to move into. This places most CARICOM countries in the 'strategic bets' quadrant, requiring large leaps to new productive activities rather than incremental diversification, while countries like Costa Rica and Guatemala have more organic pathways available.
supporting_quotes: For example, Costa Rica is present in the electronics cluster and Guatemala has significant agricultural diversification, including processing.
In general these figures show that the CARICOM countries are located in a highly peripheral part of the product space, but with some differences.
These figures suggest that the CARICOM countries are in an extremely sparse part of the product space.

Both of these methodologies suggest that the CARICOM countries are, for the most part, in the lower left 'strategic bets' quadrant: they have to move to new productive activities but have very few nearby.

questions.text: Based on 2004–2005 data, what were the most promising 'nearby' export opportunities for Belize and Jamaica, and how did these compare across the two countries?
questions.source_paper: Policies for Achieving Structural Transformation in the Caribbean

paper_ground_truth.answer: Both Belize and Jamaica have low-hanging fruit concentrated in agricultural and marine products, but Jamaica's nearby products are actually closer in absolute density terms. For Belize, the highest-density nearby products are cocoa beans (density 0.058), fish fillets (0.054), and wood products (0.049), all in agriculture, forestry, and mariculture. Jamaica's top nearby products include vegetables (density 0.093), fish (0.091), and legumes (0.091) — noticeably higher densities than Belize's equivalents, suggesting Jamaica has stronger existing capability links to these products. Jamaica also already has a presence in some of these sectors, giving it a head start. However, both countries' low-hanging fruit products tend to have low strategic value — they don't create many new downstream capabilities. Moving to products with higher strategic value requires longer leaps in the product space for both countries, though for Belize this challenge is especially acute given its extremely low EXPY.
supporting_quotes: The low-hanging fruit in Belize is almost exclusively in agriculture: nontraditional agriculture, forest products, processed foods, and mariculture.
Jamaica's low-hanging fruit is also concentrated in a narrow range of activities: agriculture and mariculture, as well as a couple of garment and textile sectors.
It is interesting to note that comparing the two countries, the nearby sectors for Jamaica are much closer in absolute terms. Moreover, Jamaica already has a presence in some of these sectors.
But we can see in Figure 14 that on average these low-hanging fruit products have a lower strategic value: they do not represent the creation of a host of new capabilities with alternative uses.

questions.text: In 2004, why might garments have been a less attractive export target for Jamaica than agricultural processing or pharmaceutical products, from a product space perspective?
questions.source_paper: Policies for Achieving Structural Transformation in the Caribbean

paper_ground_truth.answer: While garments are nearby in Jamaica's product space (meaning Jamaica has the capabilities to produce them), they have a significantly lower PRODY than alternatives like agricultural processing or pharmaceutical/cosmetics products. PRODY reflects the income level of countries that successfully export a product — so a lower PRODY means garments are typically produced by lower-income countries and support lower wages. Moving further out on Jamaica's efficient strategic frontier, products like dairy goods, canned vegetables, and processed fish offer both proximity and higher PRODY than garments. Even further out, medicaments and cosmetics — including creams, soaps, non-soap surface-active agents, and hair products — enter the efficient frontier with higher income potential. This suggests that while garments are accessible for Jamaica, they offer limited wage and development benefits compared to agricultural processing or light manufacturing in personal care and pharmaceutical products.
supporting_quotes: In the case of Jamaica, both the very nearby products and those slightly up the efficient frontier are agricultural goods, such as dairy products, canned vegetables, and processed fish products. There are also activities in the garments cluster; however, these have a significantly lower PRODY, meaning they typically support only low wages.

Allowing for further jumps, other manufactured products enter the efficient frontier, such as simple plastic, as well as many goods in the medicaments and cosmetics industry, such as creams and soaps, non-soap surface-active agents, and hair products.

data_points.product: medicaments, cosmetics, creams, soaps, hair products

questions.text: For countries like Belize that were stuck in the periphery of the product space as of 2005, is there a trade-off between targeting nearby low-hanging fruit versus aiming for more distant but strategically valuable sectors?
questions.source_paper: Policies for Achieving Structural Transformation in the Caribbean

paper_ground_truth.answer: Yes, there's a clear trade-off. For Belize, nearby low-hanging fruit products in agriculture (like cocoa, fish fillets, wood products) are accessible given existing capabilities, but they have low strategic value — meaning they don't open up many new downstream diversification opportunities. As you allow for longer jumps to more distant products, the strategic value increases substantially. Specifically, manufacturing sectors like apparel and electronics, while far from Belize's current export base, appear on the efficient frontier because they offer the best combination of higher wages (PRODY) and the creation of new capabilities with broader alternative uses. This creates a genuine strategic dilemma: the easy path (agriculture) leads to a dead end, while the high-value path (manufacturing) requires a large leap that is difficult to achieve without significant external support or targeted policy intervention.
supporting_quotes: But we can see in Figure 14 that on average these low-hanging fruit products have a lower strategic value: they do not represent the creation of a host of new capabilities with alternative uses. As you allow for further and further jumps, activities emerge with a much higher strategic value.

Allowing for longer jumps adds some manufacturing sectors, namely apparel and electronics. As can be seen above, these sectors (the light-blue dots) are quite distant, but they represent the best mix of high wages and stimulation of subsequent structural transformation at that distance, making them sensible considerations.

Belize in particular, with an extremely low EXPY, is squarely in the strategic bets quadrant of Figure 11 with no pathway to structural transformation: new areas of the product space have to be reached.

questions.text: Between 2000 and 2023, how did Namibia's fishing sector perform as an export industry, and what happened to it during the 2019–2021 period?
questions.source_paper: Public–Private Dialogs to Spur Export-led Growth: The Case of Productivity Taskforces in Namibia

paper_ground_truth.answer: Namibia's fishing sector has had a dramatic arc. From the early 2000s through 2013, fish product exports grew at a compounded annual rate of over 3%, making fishing a cornerstone of the economy — consistently contributing around 3% of GDP since 2007 and accounting for over 10% of total annual exports between 2000 and 2018. Growth then stagnated from 2013 to 2019, mirroring a worldwide slowdown in international export growth. But after 2019, the sector experienced a sharp collapse: exports fell by over 35% between 2019 and 2021. This is particularly striking given that globally, fish product revenues and production volumes have sharply increased since the pandemic ended, and fish prices rose between 2021 and 2023 — suggesting demand outpaced supply worldwide. Namibia's export decline thus appears to reflect domestic constraints rather than weak global demand.
supporting_quotes: Fishing is a critical industry in Namibia, consistently contributing around 3% to the nation's GDP since 2007 and accounting for over 10% of total annual exports from 2000 to 2018.

The exports of fish products in Namibia consistently increased at an annual compounded growth rate of over 3% between the early 2000s and 2013, when they peaked. They stagnated between 2013 and 2019, a period of worldwide slowdown of international export growth. Yet after 2019, the fishing industry experienced a dramatic export collapse: over 35% between 2019 and 2021.
Worldwide revenues and the production volume for processed and fresh products have sharply increased since the end of the pandemic.
However, prices for fishing products had also increased between 2021 and 2023, suggesting that the surge in production was lower that of demand.
paper_ground_truth.paper_title: Public–Private Dialogs to Spur Export-led Growth: The Case of Productivity Taskforces in Namibia

questions.text: As of 2014, what competitive advantages did Namibia have for exporting high-value fruits like grapes to European and North American markets?
questions.source_paper: Public–Private Dialogs to Spur Export-led Growth: The Case of Productivity Taskforces in Namibia

paper_ground_truth.answer: Namibia has a surprisingly strong combination of structural trade advantages for high-value fruit exports. First, its harvest season runs from November to January — precisely the off-season for major grape-producing countries in the Northern Hemisphere like the US and Europe, meaning Namibia faces minimal direct competition when its produce hits those markets. Second, Namibia's ports are geographically closer to Europe than those of Peru, one of the world's leading fruit exporters, giving it a logistics cost edge on that key route. Third, Namibia's membership in AGOA provides preferential access to the US market compared to non-African exporters. Finally, Namibia signed an Economic Partnership Agreement with the EU in 2014 and has maintained commercial relationships with European countries since independence, providing favorable trade terms for its largest potential market.
supporting_quotes: One of main factors that could boost Namibia's fruit exports was that its harvest season does not overlap with that of the Northern Hemisphere. Fruit harvest in Namibia spans from November to January, which offers a strategic advantage as it aligns with the off-season in major grape-producing countries in the Northern Hemisphere, including the United States and Europe.

Namibia was in an excellent position to cater to demand from the North American and European markets. The ports that can be accessed from and in Namibia are closer in distance to Europe than those used in leading countries in the sector, like Peru.
Being part of the African Growth and Opportunity Act (AGOA) gave Namibia a competitive edge when exporting to the United States vis-à-vis other non-African exporters of high-value fruits.
Namibia had also established commercial relationships with multiple European countries since its independence, and, in 2014, signed an Economic Partnership Agreement with the European Union.
paper_ground_truth.paper_title: Public–Private Dialogs to Spur Export-led Growth: The Case of Productivity Taskforces in Namibia

questions.text: Between 2014 and 2023, how did Namibia's grape and date export trajectories compare, and what did the recent growth momentum look like?
questions.source_paper: Public–Private Dialogs to Spur Export-led Growth: The Case of Productivity Taskforces in Namibia

paper_ground_truth.answer: Namibia's two main high-value fruit exports have had quite different trajectories. Grapes, which represent about 70% of Namibia's fruit production, have been exported since 2000 and grew continuously until 2014, after which they declined. However, the sector has recently rebounded strongly: grape exports grew 40% between 2020 and 2022, and then a further 21% in 2023 alone. Harvested area also expanded significantly, up 28% (about 2,000 hectares) between 2018 and 2022. Dates are a much newer and smaller export story — producers only began exporting significant volumes in 2014 — but they achieved a fourfold increase in exports by 2019, and harvested area more than tripled between 2017 and 2022. Together, these trends suggest the sector is in a phase of renewed momentum after earlier setbacks, with grapes driving volume and dates showing rapid growth from a low base.
supporting_quotes: According to FAO production estimates, table grapes were 70% of fruit production in 2020 in Namibia. Grape growers have constantly increased their exports since they started in 2000, though they have declined since 2014.
Between 2018 and 2022, grape farms registered an increase of two thousand hectares or 28% in harvested area. Gross exports increased by 40% between 2020 and 2022.
In 2023, gross exports of grapes increased by 21% versus the previous year.
On the other hand, date producers started exporting significant amounts of produce in 2014 and had experienced a fourfold increase in exports by 2019.
Similarly, the harvested area for dates – although at a much lower scale – more than tripled between 2017 and 2022.
paper_ground_truth.paper_title: Public–Private Dialogs to Spur Export-led Growth: The Case of Productivity Taskforces in Namibia

questions.text: By 2021, how did Namibia's high-value fruit export opportunity compare to what Peru had achieved by 2000–2008, and what did that benchmark suggest about Namibia's potential?
questions.source_paper: Public–Private Dialogs to Spur Export-led Growth: The Case of Productivity Taskforces in Namibia

paper_ground_truth.answer: The comparison between Namibia and Peru is striking. Peru has become one of the world's most successful exporters of fruits and vegetables, particularly grapes and blueberries. Crucially, the regions of Peru where these crops boomed are semi-desertic — geographically similar to southern Namibia, where most of Namibia's high-value fruit farms are concentrated. Furthermore, Namibia's current output levels for grapes and blueberries are described as reminiscent of what Peru looked like roughly twenty years ago, suggesting Namibia may be at the beginning of a similar growth trajectory. One key mechanism in Peru's transformation was a dramatic consolidation and expansion of agricultural land: the government sold about 68,000 hectares in coastal areas between 1997 and 2008, with the average parcel size of 350 hectares being 100 times larger than the regional average of 3.5 hectares in 1997. This scale enabled the commercial farming model that drove Peru's export surge. Namibia also holds a geographic edge over Peru for European markets, as its ports are closer to Europe than Peru's. However, Namibia has not yet been able to access the fast-growing Asian markets that have absorbed more African fruit exports, partly due to productivity constraints.
supporting_quotes: Peru has been one of the most successful countries in exporting fruits and vegetables – particularly grapes and blueberries.

Some of the regions of Peru where high-value fruits like blueberries and table grapes boomed are also semi-desertic areas like the South of Namibia, where most of the high-value fruit farms are located. Likewise, the output of grapes and blueberries in Namibia at the time was also reminiscent of what was seen in Peru twenty years ago.

From 1997 to 2008, the government sold around 68,000 hectares in the coastal areas. The average size of land sold was 350 hectares, a hundred times larger than the size of the average property from the region, 3.5 hectares in 1997.
The ports that can be accessed from and in Namibia are closer in distance to Europe than those used in leading countries in the sector, like Peru.
However, the country was not in the same position to start exporting to the Asian markets, which was related to productivity constraints.

data_points.metric: average farm size vs. regional average

questions.text: Between 2010 and 2020, which African countries were most successful at breaking into the Chinese fruit market, and what growth rates did they achieve?
questions.source_paper: Public–Private Dialogs to Spur Export-led Growth: The Case of Productivity Taskforces in Namibia

paper_ground_truth.answer: Egypt and South Africa have been the main drivers of Africa's fruit export boom to China, largely due to favorable conditions: Egypt shares climate similarities with Namibia, while South Africa has strong commercial agriculture capabilities. Beyond these two leaders, Zimbabwe — another Southern African country — successfully entered the Chinese fruit market and achieved 200% export growth between 2016 and 2020, demonstrating that smaller African economies can also penetrate this market. More broadly, China, India, and Vietnam have all significantly increased their fruit imports from Africa over the previous decade, suggesting Asia represents a high-potential growth frontier for African fruit exporters. Namibia, despite having comparable climate conditions to some of these successful exporters, had not yet entered Asian markets at the time due to productivity constraints.
supporting_quotes: Egypt and South Africa were the main drivers of the African fruit export boom to China. South Africa is a relevant point of comparison for Namibia because of the shared history and multiple similarities. Conversely, Egypt's climate conditions are quite similar to those of Namibia.
Other countries in Southern Africa, like Zimbabwe, managed to enter the Chinese fruit markets – which resulted in a 200% export growth between 2016 and 2020.
Several Asian countries presented high-potential opportunities for Africa. China, India, and Vietnam had significantly increased fruit imports from Africa in the previous decade.
However, the country was not in the same position to start exporting to the Asian markets, which was related to productivity constraints.

questions.text: As of 2021, how did Namibia's economic complexity profile shape which export sectors were realistic to develop in the short term?
questions.source_paper: Public–Private Dialogs to Spur Export-led Growth: The Case of Productivity Taskforces in Namibia

paper_ground_truth.answer: Namibia's low Economic Complexity Index reflects limited knowledge agglomeration — meaning the country lacks the dense, overlapping industrial capabilities that make it easy to diversify into new, complex products. This has a direct implication for export strategy: because feasibility depends on how close a new product is to a country's existing productive knowledge, Namibia has few nearby 'stepping stones' in the product space to jump to more complex industries. As a result, working within relatively well-established sectors offers more realistic near-term returns than targeting sophisticated industries that may make theoretical sense for long-term diversification but are too distant from current capabilities. High-value fruits — grapes, dates, and berries — fit this logic well: they score lower on complexity and higher on feasibility compared to most products that would be identified as complexity-upgrading targets. They represent achievable wins within Namibia's current productive structure rather than a leap to more complex activities.
supporting_quotes: Namibia's low levels of knowledge agglomeration and scarce opportunities for productive diversification implied that working in relatively well-established sectors would yield more realistic returns in the short term than focusing on more complex yet less feasible industries that were part of a longer-term diversification strategy.
Figure 1 shows that products comprised within this category – grapes, dates, and berries – were less complex and more feasible than most products recommended in the economic complexity report.

questions.text: Despite being a heavy fruit importer, how much export growth potential did Namibia's fruit sector show between 2019 and 2023, and what did the investment activity suggest?
questions.source_paper: Public–Private Dialogs to Spur Export-led Growth: The Case of Productivity Taskforces in Namibia

paper_ground_truth.answer: Namibia imports the vast majority of its fruit consumption — only 4% of produce was sourced locally in 2019 — and just 17% of its total arable land was under cultivation in 2021, pointing to a large untapped productive base. Yet the export performance of high-value fruits has been accelerating rapidly: grape exports rose 40% between 2020 and 2022 and another 21% in 2023, while two high-value fruit farms ranked among Namibia's four fastest-growing companies in 2023. Investment signals are also strong: a US$80 million blueberry project backed by two large Spanish agri-food companies was announced in 2023, targeting a 250-hectare farm and aimed at exporting its first harvest in September 2024. Taken together, the picture is of a sector still in early stages — with most land uncultivated and most fruit still imported — but showing the kind of export acceleration and inward investment that can indicate the beginning of a structural transformation, especially in the context of global demand for grapes, dates, and blueberries rising consistently since the early 2000s.
supporting_quotes: Only 4% of the produce was sourced locally in 2019, and only 17% of the total arable land was under cultivation in 2021.
Gross exports increased by 40% between 2020 and 2022.
In 2023, gross exports of grapes increased by 21% versus the previous year.
Two high-value fruit farms were among the four top fastest-growing companies in Namibia, according to Africa's Fastest Growing Companies Report 2023.
A company focused on the production of blueberries (Namibia Berries) announced a US$80 million investment over seven years to establish a 250-hectare farm in the Kavango East region.
The global demand for grapes, dates, and blueberries had been consistently increasing since the early 2000s – with growing markets in the United States, Europe, and Asia.
